column13: Investigating the Effect of Ashwagandha on the Heat Shock Response of Drosophila melanogaster
column14: Parkinson’s disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease; PD lacks a cure and treatment focuses on the management of symptoms, including impaired motor function and memory. The heat shock response (HSR) is a mechanism to combat cellular stress through the production of heat shock proteins. Previous studies suggest Withania somnifera - or Ashwagandha - exhibits stress modulating properties, possibly bolstering the HSR. Because oxidative stress is a major contributor to PD, and Ashwagandha is an antioxidant, it is uncertain whether positive effects of Ashwagandha administration are due to an improved HSR, or if any antioxidant could replace Ashwagandha. Ashwagandha was administered to afflicted Drosophila melanogaster and compared with Vitamin C and absent treatments. Two assays were used: a negative geotaxis assay, where flies’ climbing speed was used to gauge motor function, and a choice chamber assay, where quinine was used as a repulsive stimulus to evaluate their memory. Vitamin C and Ashwagandha treated PD models showed significant improvements in motor function compared to untreated ones (p = 0.01), however there was no statistically significant difference between the two treatments (p = 0.47), which indicates that improvements came primarily from antioxidative qualities. In the choice chamber assay, the only treatment yielding a similar success rate to healthy flies was Ashwagandha, as despite being an antioxidant, Vitamin C treated flies showed no improvement. These findings suggest that Ashwagandha may have qualities which make it superior to antioxidants, however further investigation be required. Further research of Ashwagandha could contribute to improved therapeutic methods regarding PD.

column13: The Impact of an Alternative Cloud Seeding Chemical, Aluminum Oxide, on the Growth of and Health of Photosystem 2 in a Brassica Rapa Model
column14: Cloud seeding, the introduction of particles into clouds to enhance precipitation, is used in drought areas, but raises questions about its environmental impacts, particularly regarding chemical deposition on plant systems. Silver iodide is typically used, but other alternative chemicals, such as aluminum oxide, are being studied to see if they could improve plant health. This study investigates how varying concentrations of aluminum oxide, within the range of concentrations reaching the ground during seeding, affect growth and photosynthetic efficiency in Brassica rapa (field mustard). Plants were analyzed using a chlorophyll fluorometer to assess photosystem II activity through fluorescence and quenching, which measure energy absorption and dissipation during photosynthesis. Results show minimal differences in overall growth between control and treated groups across concentrations. However, fluorometer data indicate that the lowest aluminum oxide concentration reflected slightly higher fluorescence and quenching values, while higher concentrations did not differ significantly from the control. These patterns suggest a limited or potentially negligible effect on photosynthetic efficiency within the range. This research provides insight into the environmental impact of aluminum oxide as a cloud seeding agent, suggesting it may pose minimal risk to plant growth at realistic exposure levels. These findings contribute to ongoing evaluations of alternative cloud seeding materials and their ecological safety, though further investigation is needed to confirm long-term and ecosystem-level effects.

column13: The Persistence of Conditioned Addiction Behaviors in Planarians After a Regeneration Period
column14: While large-scale regeneration stands as a promising technique for recovery and revitalization after serious injury, the interaction between regeneration and learned behaviors is largely unknown. Planarians, commonly known as flatworms, are an excellent model for regeneration, having the capabilities to fully regenerate from complete head amputation. Planarians also pose as a model for the effects of addictive substances, such as sucrose, due to exhibiting clear changes in behavior when in the presence of conditioned addictive substances. How do behaviors conditioned through addictive substances persist after complete head regeneration? This experiment attempts to measure the persistence of sucrose addiction-induced increased positive phototaxis after complete head regeneration by exposing planarians to 10% sucrose solution over 7 testing days, testing the planaria’s phototaxis trends, then amputating the planaria and testing them again after a ~2 week regeneration period. Planarians that underwent both sucrose conditioning and head regeneration were compared to planarians that only underwent either sucrose conditioning, head regeneration, or no conditioning at all. While experimental data was unable to be obtained due to mass planarian death, the phototaxis trends exhibited in the control groups are consistent with known comparative values, validating the methodology as a way to determine the persistence of phototaxis trends after complete head regeneration.

column13: Effect of Varying Concentrations of Blueberry Anthocyanin Extract, Malvidin Chloride, on Mitochondrial Health in C. elegans
column14: Polyphenols, such as anthocyanin found in blueberries, provide pigment to berries/vegetables and have been shown to improve life span in various models. However, their effects on autophagy and lifespan in Caenorhabditis elegans have yet to be fully understood in varying concentrations. This study aims to investigate whether anthocyanin extract from blueberries increases the abundance of the LGG-1 protein, a key marker of autophagy, as concentration increases, therefore suggesting an enhancement in mitochondrial health. Decreased mitochondrial health has been shown to increase the likelihood of acquiring degenerative diseases, so anthocyanins could potentially combat this. C. elegans were cultured on NGM plates with and without anthocyanin (as the negative control). The concentrations cultured on the plates were 3 µg/ml, 5 µg/ml, and 7 µg/ml, and the protein abundance of LGG-1 was quantified using a Western Blot test. The experiment was repeated two times with a negative control group (no anthocyanin). The data were analyzed using the Kruskal-Wallis test to determine if the protein abundance differed significantly between the experimental and control groups. Preliminary data needs to be further analyzed to draw a conclusion. The results of this study could provide insight into the potential therapeutic effects of blueberry polyphenols on mitochondrial function, particularly in the context of pharmaceuticals targeted towards neurodegenerative diseases.

column13: Determining the Impact of Rising Water Temperatures on the Fecundity and Expression of the Caudodorsal Cell Hormone (CDCH-1) in Lymnaea stagnalis
column14: Greenhouse gases absorb heat and trap it in the atmosphere, raising atmospheric temperatures. This leads to increased surface water temperatures, which poses unknown dangers to aquatic organisms. This study aimed to determine if increased water temperatures impact the fecundity (ability to produce offspring) and expression of the Caudodorsal Cell Hormone (CDCH-1) in the common pond snail, Lymnaea stagnalis. The CDCH-1 gene has been shown to play a key role in egg-laying behavior in Lymnaea stagnalis by triggering egg-laying behavior and egg mass formation. It was hypothesized that when Lymnaea stagnalis were exposed to slightly higher water temperatures they would have increased reproduction rates and CDCH-1 expression due to increased metabolic activity, but those exposed to extremely higher temperatures would have decreased reproduction and lower CDCH-1 expression due to excess physiological stress. L. stagnalis were exposed to four increasing water temperatures for two days. After the incubation period, L. stagnalis were dissected for their central nervous system. RNA extraction and RT-qPCR was then performed on each sample. Trial one results showed that when exposed to slightly higher temperatures, there was an upregulation of CDCH-1 and when snails were exposed to extreme temperatures, they experienced a downregulation of CDCH-1, aligning with the hypothesis. Trial two data showed an extreme downregulation of CDCH-1 in all experimental groups. Additional work must be done to explain the differences between each trial.

column13: The Effect of Natural Antiandrogens on Drosophila melanogaster Induced with Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)-like Symptoms
column14: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) is a common hormonal disorder that involves higher levels of androgens (such as testosterone), or hyperandrogenism. Hyperandrogenism is a key part of PCOS and is responsible for many of its main symptoms, such as hirsutism and irregular periods. This study looks at whether spearmint, a natural substance that may have anti-androgen effects, can reduce signs of hyperandrogenism using Drosophila melanogaster as a model organism. In this experiment, fruit flies will be placed into a spearmint-treated group and a control group under the same lab conditions. Reproductive output will be measured and compared between the two groups as an indicator of changes in androgen-related effects on fecundity. It is expected that the spearmint-treated group may show lower signs of hyperandrogenism compared to the control group, based on differences in reproductive output. Overall, this study aims to see if spearmint could have an effect on androgen-related pathways in a simple model system. However, more research would still be needed to understand if these results apply to more complex organisms and if they could be relevant to PCOS in humans.

column13: Semi-Dynamic Leaching Assessment of Polyurethane Coatings on End-of-Life Tires for Sustainable Engineering Applications
column14:
There are an estimated four billion tires stored in landfills worldwide, with an additional one
billion reaching the end of their service life every year. End of life tires (ELTS) are commonly
recycled through mechanical shredding, an energy intensive process that increases surface
area and accelerates environmental degradation. Tire rubber contains leachable metals such as zinc and copper, which pollute watersheds and degrade our water quality. Previous studies have shown that polyurethane coatings can reduce metal leaching by, in some cases, over 99 percent, indicating potential for safer reuse of whole tires in sustainable construction methods. This project evaluated metal leaching from monolithic tire rubber using a semi dynamic leaching procedure based on the EPA Method 1315. Tire sections were cut from a single end of life tire. Sample edges were sealed and framed with chemically inert materials to ensure consistent leaching conditions. Over a fourteen day period, eluate samples were collected at predetermined intervals and analyzed for zinc and copper using a simultaneous spectrophotometric method with 4-(2-pyridylazo)resorcinol as the colorimetric reagent. Results show lower detected concentrations of zinc in the polyurethane coated samples when compared to uncoated samples. by the third day, the uncoated tire sample had reached a level higher than the EPA's maximum contaminant level of 5 mg/L, while the polyurethane coated sample stayed under for the leaching period. These results support polyurethane coatings as an effective method for reducing tire rubber
leachability. Future work will include longer leaching durations, additional metals testing, and longevity testing of this coated material under different environmental conditions.

column13: Comparison of the effects of lithium carbonate, lithium citrate, and lithium orotate on memory in a Drosophila melanogaster model of Alzheimer’s disease
column14: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a terminal neurodegenerative disease characterized by cognitive decline and memory loss resulting from the buildup of amyloid-beta plaques and tau protein tangles. Lithium salts, particularly lithium carbonate (LiCa), inhibit the enzyme Glycogen Synthase Kinase-3, which is upregulated in AD, reducing tau and amyloid-beta accumulation, inflammation, and oxidative stress. The other FDA-approved lithium drug, Lithium Citrate (LiCi) is used for its liquid formulation in the elderly and patients with gastrointestinal distress. However, LiCa use has declined due to long-term neurotoxicity. Lithium orotate (LiOr), an alternative, has greater bioavailability–delivering more ions to the brain–but was discontinued in the 1970s for toxicity concerns at equivalent doses. We hypothesized that LiOr is more effective than LiCa at lower doses (1mM) due to higher lithium ion transfer, while inferior at higher doses (10mM) due to LiOr’s toxicity. We also hypothesized that LiCa and LiCi perform similarly due to similar dosages and effects in bipolar disorder. This study compares LiCa, LiCi, and LiOr on memory in a Drosophila melanogaster model produced by crossing UAS-amyloid-beta 42 and Act5c-GAL4 flies via the GAL4/UAS system. Once aged to 25 days, flies were assessed for short-term memory using the aversive phototaxic suppression (APS) assay. Results suggest lithium may confer a benefit in healthy and AD flies, but significant differences between key groups were not found, possibly a result of low sample size. This study novelly compared three lithium compounds in an Alzheimer’s model and may guide research into safer, more effective AD and lithium treatments.

column13: The Effect of Alpha Lipoic Acid on Memory and Interpersonal Space in Drosophila melanogaster Models of Chronic Schizophrenia
column14: This study investigated the effects of alpha lipoic acid (ALA) on memory and interpersonal space behaviors in Drosophila melanogaster models of chronic schizophrenia. Schizophrenia is associated with cognitive impairments and altered social behaviors, partially linked to oxidative stress. Given ALA’s known antioxidant properties that neutralize free radicals, this experiment tested whether ALA could mitigate these deficits. Flies were divided into four groups: a disease control (schizophrenia model without ALA), a negative control (healthy flies without ALA), an experimental group (schizophrenia model treated with ALA), and a treatment control (healthy flies treated with ALA). Memory was assessed using the aversive phototaxic suppression (APS) assay, while interpersonal space was measured through the Social Space Index. Results showed that schizophrenia-model flies exhibited reduced memory performance and increased space compared to healthy controls. However, treatment with ALA improved both memory and interpersonal space in the experimental group, bringing performance closer to that of healthy flies. Statistical analyses supported the significance of these differences. These findings suggest that ALA may alleviate cognitive and social deficits associated with schizophrenia-like conditions, potentially through its antioxidant effects. This research highlights ALA as a promising candidate for further investigation in neuroprotective treatments targeting cognitive deterioration.

column13: The Effect of Creatine on the Memory of Drosophila melanogaster Exhibiting Alzheimer’s Disease
column14: Alzheimer’s disease is a neurodegenerative disease that affects around 7 million Americans 65 years of age or older. Alzheimer’s accounts for approximately 70% of dementia cases worldwide with the annual cost of care being $1.3 trillion (Gaugler et al., 2024).. There are three major mechanisms with Alzheimer’s disease that lead to neurodegeneration: Aβ plaque aggregation, oxidative stress, and neurofibrillary tangles. These cause memory loss and issues with cognitive function and motor skills due to the breakage of synaptic connections (Khan et al., 2024). These mechanisms of Alzheimer’s lead to various effects on the mitochondria such as mitochondrial fission, destroying the H+ ion gradient, and limiting oxidative phosphorylation, thereby decreasing ATP production and leading to neuronal death (Johri., 2021). Creatine, a popular dietary supplement used by athletes, boosts mitochondrial ATP production and protects against neurodegeneration within a Huntington’s model. Thus, creatine was hypothesized to increase the memory level of Drosophila melanogaster exhibiting Alzheimer’s as measured by the Aversive Phototaxis Suppression assay. The GAL4/UAS system allows for the easy manipulation of genes and the compact brain of the flies leads to Drosophila being an excellent model for studying neurodegenerative diseases. It was found that creatine did increase the memory of Drosophila exhibiting Alzheimer’s disease. An ANOVA statistical test with a post-hoc Tukey test found a p-value less than 0.01 for the Alzheimer’s and Alzheimer’s with creatine groups, indicating a significant difference. Possible sources of error include variances in exposure time to creatine and behavioral variances due to seasonal changes.

column13: The Effect of Propionic Acid (PPA) against Genetically-Induced Oxidative Stress via a Mutation Model in Drosophila melanogaster
column14: Oxidative stress is where there is an imbalance between the reactive oxygen species (ROS) and antioxidant defenses. This is a key factor into why neuronal dysfunction is implicated in many different neurodegenerative disorders. This study investigates whether or not propionic acid (PPA); which is a short chain fatty acid with reported anti-inflammatory properties and mild antioxidant properties, can regulate oxidative stress in an induced cognitive deficit in Drosophila melanogaster. A mutation based model with the key component of the reduced SOD2 function was used to induce elevated oxidative stress in neuronal tissue. Drosophila melanogaster was assigned to specific control and experimental groups based on mutation status and the dietary exposure to PPA. Cognitive performance was tested using the aversive phototaxic suppression (APS) assay, which measures the learning and short term memory by evaluating the ability of flies to suppress their natural attraction towards light after the appropriate training. Preliminary findings show that PPA treated flies exhibit improved APS performance compared to untreated mutation groups, showing that there is already a partial rescue of oxidative stress with the related cognitive deficits. These results support the hypothesis that PPA may reduce neuronal damage through its anti-inflammatory effects and regulate the specific pathways of oxidative stress. Overall, this study highlights the effectiveness of Drosophila melanogaster as a model for studying oxidative stress and shows that PPA may have potential as a neuroprotective agent. Further trials and statistical analysis are required to confirm these findings and determine optimal treatment conditions.

column13: The Effect of Exposure to Bisphenol A on the Learning and Memory in Male and Female Drosophila melanogaster of the Fragile X Syndrome Model
column14: Fragile X Syndrome (FXS) is a genetic neurodevelopmental disorder caused by a mutation in the FMR1 gene that diminishes synaptic plasticity and impairs learning and memory. Bisphenol A (BPA), commonly used in plastic production, is an endocrine-disrupting chemical known to interfere with hormone signaling. Because learning and memory are influenced by sex-specific hormones, with ecdysone enhancing synaptic plasticity in females and juvenile hormone enhancing spatial awareness in males, BPA exposure may produce different cognitive effects across sexes. Therefore, this study investigated the effects of BPA on learning and memory in male and female Drosophila of the FXS and wild-type models. It was hypothesized that BPA exposure would cause greater memory impairment in FXS males, as the higher levels of ecdysone in females would protect against BPA’s interference. Male and female flies were exposed to 1 mM BPA through food for two weeks. Learning and memory were measured through the aversive phototaxic suppression assay, in which flies are trained to associate light with the aversive stimulus quinine, and performance is measured as the percentage of flies avoiding the light vial. Kruskal Wallis analysis yielded a p-value less than 0.01, but pairwise comparisons did not reveal statistically significant differences between groups. Observed trends in the data suggest that BPA-exposed females, regardless of model, exhibited higher memory performance, while male performance remained relatively unchanged. These findings do not support the hypothesis but indicate the need for further investigation into sex-specific cognitive responses to BPA in FXS models.

column13: Effects of Ganoderma lucidum on Cognitive Dysfunction and Neuropeptide F Expression in Drosophila melanogaster Models of Neurodegenerative Disease
column14: Neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and Huntington’s disease (HD) are characterized by progressive neuronal dysfunction and cognitive decline driven by toxic protein aggregates, including hyperphosphorylated tau and mutant huntingtin proteins (mHTT). Current treatments target specific symptoms but fail to address underlying disease mechanisms. This gap between neurodegenerative diseases and modern treatments underscores the need for novel therapeutic strategies. The medicinal mushroom Ganoderma lucidum contains bioactive compounds with neuroprotective properties, including increased brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) levels, which are linked to Neuropeptide F (NPF) expression and enhanced neuronal resilience. NPF increases synaptic plasticity and stimulates aggrephagy, which clears mutant protein aggregates. This study evaluated the effects of G. lucidum on cognitive function and NPF expression in Drosophila melanogaster models of neurodegenerative diseases. Cognitive function was quantified using an Aversive Phototaxic Suppression (APS) assay, and NPF expression was measured with an Immunofluorescence (IF) assay to investigate potential biological changes associated with treatment. The data from the APS assay support the conclusion that G. lucidum improves cognitive function in AD models (p=0.03); however, there is insufficient evidence that G. lucidum improves cognitive function in HD models (p=0.08). Data collection from the IF assay is ongoing, and statistical analyses will provide insight into a potential neuroprotective mechanism connecting BDNF and NPF. This experiment suggests that G. lucidum offers cognitive benefits in certain neurodegenerative diseases. Ultimately, more data is needed to ensure greater confidence in the treatment and define the scope of therapeutic potential across different neurodegenerative diseases.

column13: The Effect of Cholecalciferol on Aggression in an Ethanol-Exposed Drosophila melanogaster modeling schizophrenia with an Overexpression of Metalloproteinase 2 in the Mushroom Body
column14:
Aggression is a symptom commonly associated with both schizophrenia and alcohol use disorder (AUD), conditions that frequently co-occur and share underlying neurobiological mechanisms such as neuroinflammation and dopamine dysregulation. This study investigates the potential of cholecalciferol, more commonly known as vitamin D3, to mitigate aggression in a Drosophila melanogaster model of schizophrenia induced by overexpression of the metalloproteinase 2 (Mmp2) gene in the mushroom bodies, combined with ethanol exposure. Male flies were divided into four groups: negative control (no mutation, no ethanol, no supplementation), positive control (Mmp2 overexpression and ethanol exposure), toxicity control (cholecalciferol only), and experimental (Mmp2 overexpression, ethanol exposure, and cholecalciferol supplementation). Aggressive behavior was quantified through a standardized assay measuring wing threats, tapping threats, lunging, boxing, tussling, and chasing over 20-minute trials.

Preliminary trends and expected outcomes suggest that flies with Mmp2 overexpression and ethanol exposure will exhibit significantly elevated aggression compared to controls, while cholecalciferol supplementation is anticipated to reduce aggressive events in the experimental group. 

These findings aim to contribute to understanding how dietary interventions targeting neuroinflammation may alleviate behavioral symptoms associated with schizophrenia and AUD. They also support the potential of vitamin D3 as a low-cost and low-risk alternative to clozapine, the current drug being used in treatment.

column13: The Effects of Taxifolin on Intestinal Permeability and Gut Derived α-Synuclein Aggregation in Drosophila melanogaster model of Parkinson’s Disease
column14: Parkinson’s disease (PD) is a prevalent neurodegenerative disorder that affects over 10 million individuals globally, with 1.3 million new cases diagnosed each year (Luo et al., 2025). PD causes progressive motor disabilities in aging adults, and is characterized by the progressive degeneration of dopaminergic neurons within the substantia nigra. A key pathological hallmark of PD is the presence of Lewy Bodies, or neuronal inclusions within the cell body. These inclusions are primarily composed of α-Synuclein, a misfolded protein. Although α-Synuclein aggregation has primarily been associated with motor symptoms in the brain, emerging research has found evidence that α-Synuclein may first develop in the enteric nervous system, causing “leaky gut” and other gastrointestinal symptoms. Taxifolin is a natural flavonoid examined for its treatment potential for neurodegenerative diseases due to its anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidant properties. This study utilizes an ESG-Gal4 model of Parkinson's Disease exhibiting α-Synuclein in the gut and measures the effect of taxifolin on intestinal permeability. While data continues to be collected, the current PD model did not exhibit expected gastrointestinal issues, and taxifolin does not have any statistically significant effects on intestinal permeability in this model.

column13: Investigating the Effect of the Inhibition of Type III Glutamatergic Neurons on the Duration of Learning-Dependent Sleep in Drosophila melanogaster
column14: Sleep is a necessary yet poorly understood function, of which there are several kinds. Glutamate is a common neurotransmitter that regulates sleep by promoting wakefulness, and there are several types of glutamate receptors present on neurons. While the types of sleep regulated by type I and type II glutamate receptors have been identified, it is unclear what kind of sleep type III glutamate receptors regulate. Glutamate is also present in learning and memory processes, and type III glutamate receptors have been identified as a potential regulator of learning-dependent sleep, or extended sleep following a waking period containing increased cognitive activity. This research aimed to investigate the effect of deactivated type III glutamatergic neurons on learning dependent sleep in a Drosophila melanogaster model. Type III glutamatergic neurons were intended to be inhibited via a split-GAL4-UAS cross involving glutamate receptors and phospholipase C, which interacts with type III glutamate receptors. Learning-dependent sleep was activated via modified courtship conditioning, a process in which a naive male fly is taught to repress his instincts to mate a previously mated female and that consistently activates learning-dependent sleep. Sleep was then quantified using a Drosophila Activity Monitor, which measures how many times an infrared beam running through a vial containing a fly is broken; more breaks would indicate less sleep and, therefore, a connection between type III glutamatergic neurons and learning-dependent sleep. Due to issues with Drosophila supply, I was unable to complete my cross; however, the data provide evidence for learning-dependent sleep being activated.

column13: Modeling the use of battery energy storage systems(BESS) for net-load ramp reduction in renewable power systems
column14: Due to the increasing use of renewable energy sources such as wind and solar a problem of short-term variability has occurred in power grids. Which leads to steep net-load ramps that challenge grid stability. Traditional Automatic Generation Control (AGC) mechanisms, designed for more predictable changes, are not adequate to respond effectively to power grids more prone to fast fluctuations. This project studies the effectiveness of integrating a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) with AGC to mitigate short-term net-load variability in a renewable-dominated power system. Using ENTSO-E data, this study is able to model net load behavior in a European grid specifically with a focus on Germany at 15-minute intervals. A simulation was developed in python to apply ramp rate constraints and evaluate how a BESS (Modeled at 100 MW / 200 MWh) can help smooth net-load changes. The system incorporates simplified AGC-inspired control logic, where the BESS responds to any deviations between actual and target net load, serving as a fast-acting balancing resource. Key metrics include peak ramp rate reduction, ramp energy, unmet ramp magnitude, and a proxy for frequency deviation. Results show that the BESS was able to reduce extreme ramp events and improve tracking of ramp limits, indicating improved system stability. However, the effectiveness depends on operational constraints such as state of charge limits and ramp thresholds. This helps show that BESS, when coordinated with AGC principles, can play a critical role in managing renewable intermittency. The findings help highlight the limitations and potential of a storage-based solution. This study suggests that future improvements are necessary for real-world applications.

column13: GMVAE-4P: an explainable GMVAE-based neural network for patient classification using single-cell and bulk RNA-seq
column14:
Disease states may be driven by various, specific cellular populations; identifying these cell types and the relevant marker genes represents a significant challenge in the field of precision medicine. While single-cell RNA sequencing enables the heterogeneity needed for classifying such patient phenotypes, high costs make it unrealistic in clinical deployment, limiting availability across cohorts. In contrast, bulk RNA sequencing has become the standard for transcriptomic profiling over two decades, generating large repositories across diseases and conditions at a relatively low cost. However, this results in a gap: most transcriptomic patient data is aggregated (being in the bulk RNA-seq form), while methods exploiting cellular information require single-cell, granular measurements. Existing approaches fall short in bridging this divide: single-cell-based predictors remain inapplicable to the existing, extensive, and accessible bulk data repositories in clinical settings. To alleviate this issue, deconvolution methods, which estimate cell type abundances and attempt to assign corresponding scRNA-seq output (though they often do so stochastically—reducing meaningful applicability), have been created. However, such methods fail to jointly model proportions (the parameter from which generated scRNA-seq outputs are almost purely derived) with other layers needed for phenotype prediction. 

I propose GMVAE4P, a Gaussian Mixture VAE-based framework for patient phenotype prediction that operates on both bulk and single-cell RNA-seq data. GMVAE4P learns to predict three modalities from bulk RNA-seq through supervised training on scRNA-seq-derived ground truth (cell type proportions, transcriptional states via highly variable genes, and ligand-receptor scores), then, once trained, operates on bulk RNA-seq alone, combining these modalities through uncertainty-weighted fusion with contrastive alignment. When applied to scRNA-seq data, GMVAE4P employs attention-based cell importance weighting (via a modified prototype approach) to identify informative cells and ensure explainability and interpretation at the single-cell level. Using three public datasets (Lupus, COVID-19 severity, and cardiovascular disease), I demonstrate that GMVAE4P achieves 0.952-0.986 ROC-AUC on single-cell data via patient-grouped 5-fold cross-validation, outperforming existing single-cell methods (Pascient: 0.812-0.839, ProtoCell4P: 0.794-0.821, ScRAT: 0.803-0.835, singleDeep: 0.788-0.816 ROC-AUC). More remarkably, GMVAE4P’s multi-modal bulk-based approach achieves 0.875-0.911 ROC-AUC, exceeding all non-GMVAE4P single-cell methods while operating solely on bulk measurements. 

To ensure fair comparison and applicability in compute-constrained settings, all benchmarks enforce equivalent computational budgets via floating-point operation (FLOP) constraints across hyperparameter sweeps, with GMVAE4P completing training in less than 6 hours on a single 22GB VRAM GPU.

column13: Enhancing Multi-Robot Motion Planning with Diffusion Models for Multi-Objective Environments
column14: Multi-robot motion planning (MRMP) faces challenges in achieving collision-free, efficient trajectories in complex, dynamic environments. Traditional methods struggle with high-dimensionality and computational overhead, highlighting the need for advanced solutions. This study investigates the potential of diffusion models to enhance MRMP, enabling robots to generate collision-free, efficient trajectories. Our methodology utilizes experimental data from benchmarks by Liang et al. (2025) and Shaoul et al. (2025), encompassing randomized and real-world-inspired maps. Data includes JSON/CSV files, images, and videos, preprocessed for obstacle configuration and trajectory filtering. Performance is quantitatively assessed using success rate, path length, acceleration, and collision ratio under varying robot densities and obstacle complexities. Analysis of simulated runs reveals significant variability in accuracy, emphasizing an inherent efficiency-smoothness tradeoff. Optimal path length often compromises smoothness due to collision avoidance. Path optimality, measured by deviations from straight-line displacement, highlights planning complexities. Findings suggest diffusion models hold promise for MRMP, but further statistical correlation between environmental parameters and performance is crucial. Future work will refine these models and hybrid planning frameworks for robust, collision-free, and efficient multi-robot motion plans in diverse settings.

column13: Enhancing Debris Flow Warning with Deep Machine Learning
column14: Debris flows are mixtures of water, sediment, and rock that fall down a mountainside. The solid components they carry make them extremely destructive to infrastructure and humans; however, it is difficult to predict debris flow events before they occur. As such, researchers have implemented methods to detect debris flows as they happen to give early warnings. One method utilizes seismograph to “listen” for debris flow rumbling against background noise. Currently, humans must analyze seismographs to determine if a debris flow event occurred. This research focused on the development of machine learning systems to automatically distinguish between background noise and debris flow events. In particular, the research determined if deep learning approaches, utilizing Long Short-Term Memory models (LSTMs), were more effective than traditional method machine learning models, Random Forest models (RFs), at differentiating between events. Additionally, this research explored if both types of models can generalize debris flow early warnings predictions across a wide geographical area. Three LSTMs and three RFs were individually trained on three datasets. A transfer learning technique (TL) was then applied to each set of models to determine their viability for generalization. The LSTMs achieved accuracies of 98%, 95%, and 95%, with their TL achieving an accuracy of 97%. The RFs achieved accuracies of 91%, 81%, and 85%, with their TL achieving an accuracy of 93%. These results highlight that deep learning approaches appears to be better suited for early debris flow warning predictions than traditional methods, and both models can generalize to a wide geographical area.

column13: Evaluating YOLOvllm for microplastic detection and classification using a custom dataset via F1 score, and developing a web application for morphology detection and polymer yype identification
column14: Microplastics are plastic particles less than 5mm in size that pose an environmental threat due to their ability to absorb and leach toxic pollutants. Current methods to detect and classify microplastics are slow and lack polymer type identification. According to a study done by Akkajit et al in 2024, object detection model YOLOv8x showed success in detecting and classifying microplastics by their morphology using a custom dataset with an inference time of 1.2 ms and accuracy of 99%. This study builds upon this research by utilizing a custom dataset to train the enhanced YOLOv11m model to detect and classify microplastics by their morphology and polymer type. A custom dataset was built by imaging microplastics created from large household plastics. This dataset was then duplicated and the microplastics were labelled with either their morphology or polymer type, verified through visual analysis and FTIR verification. Augmentation was also performed by flipping, rotation, adding noise, etc. to reduce overfitting. The YOLOvllm models  trained on both the morphology and polymer dataset achieved a 99% precision, recall, and F1 score. A web application was also built to allow users to complete automated dual detection. The website is functional and works as intended, however limitations in the dataset size and microplastics imaged reduce its overall accuracy. Nonetheless, this research serves as the foundation for faster, more thorough identification of microplastics, which can aid in the development of degradation methods and  future research efforts.

column13: The effects of Glutamic-Pyruvic Transaminase-targeting siRNA on Hepatocellular Carcinoma as modeled in HepG2 cells
column14: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), a type of liver cancer, is the third leading cause of cancer related deaths worldwide, characterized by the overexpression of the alanine aminotransferase (ALT) protein. This overexpression causes inflammation and tumor formation in liver cells. ALT is coded by the Glutamic-Pyruvic Transaminase (GPT) gene. Current treatments for HCC include liver transplants, chemotherapy, and immunotherapy; however, these treatments do not target the protein expression itself. A treatment that is prevalent in other in vitro cancer research is siRNA transfection, which works by targeting and silencing a specific gene, thus suppressing the expression of the associated protein. The question now arises as to how GPT-targeting siRNA will impact cell viability and ALT protein expression in HepG2 cells. An in vitro model of HCC was established by subculturing HepG2 cells. The cells were then treated with a range of 30 nM, 15 nM, 7.5 nM, and 3.75 nM GPT-targeting siRNA. To assess cell viability, an Acid Phosphatase assay was performed and data was analyzed via normalization. To quantify ALT protein expression, a Western blot analysis was conducted. Statistical analysis using a Kruskal-Wallis test revealed that there was no evidence of a statistically significant difference between the siRNA treatments and the negative control of HepG2 cells alone. This can be attributed to the decreased expression of ALT, allowing the cells to thrive in a less tumorigenic environment. Overall, this study aims to evaluate the effectiveness of gene knockdown via GPT-targeting siRNA on ALT expression as a potential therapeutic avenue.

column13: Determining Whether Bilobalide Can Enhance Curcumin's Ability to Induce p53-dependent Cell Death in Non-small Cell Lung Adenocarcinoma
column14: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) has a 65% 5-year mortality rate, urging the investigation of novel combination therapies involving naturally-found plant compounds. This study evaluated the effects of combining bilobalide and curcumin on the A549 lung adenocarcinoma cell line. The objective was to determine whether combining treatments increases the amount of p53-dependent cell death compared to individual treatments. A bilobalide dosage curve was first created to determine its IC50 value, calculated to be 234.1 uM. An acid phosphatase assay was conducted to determine the viability of cells treated with 234.1 uM bilobalide, 15 uM curcumin, their combination, as well as negative and vehicle controls. An ANOVA test yielded a significant difference across most cell groups (p<0.01), while a post-hoc test revealed no significant difference between the curcumin and combination-treated cells. The mean cell viability was 42.2% for bilobalide, 12.6% for curcumin, and 11.6% for the combination treatment. Despite a slight decrease in viability with the combination treatment, the combined effect was not synergistic, but was rather antagonistic. A western blot assay to evaluate p53 protein expression has yet to be conducted. Overall, these findings highlight the importance of validating combination treatments, as interactions between compounds may diminish therapeutic effects. Further investigations are necessary to better understand the molecular mechanisms behind these interactions and to guide future therapeutic strategies for NSCLC.

Systemic Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis, or sJIA, is a rare subtype of Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA). It has several defining symptoms, like a rash, joint pain, and daily fevers; it is a lifelong disease with no treatment, although a state of remission (little to no disease activity) can be reached. sJIA has no distinct cause and can onset at any point during childhood. Laboratory tests are typically done to either confirm or negate the diagnosis of sJIA. One of the most important inflammatory biomarkers of the disease is interleukin 6 (IL-6), contributing to a portion of disease activity through the Janus kinase/signal transduction and activator of transcription (JAK/STAT) pathway. IL-6 creates a positive feedback loop with itself, causing cells that recognize it to release more IL-6. Instead of targeting IL-6 directly, this research looks to break the positive feedback loop associated with IL-6 secretion and thus decrease inflammation overall (Zou et al., 2020). To accomplish this, a natural STAT3 inhibitor, napabucasin, was used. Different concentrations of napabucasin (0.1µM, 0.5µM, 1µM) were tested along with an inflammatory model inducer (lipopolysaccharides) to see if IL-6 production would be slowed.
    Cell Titer-Glo analysis, a test for viability, was done to ensure treatments were not killing cells. Western blot analysis was performed on several treatment groups for IL-6. While the blots succeeded, an ANOVA test for significance gave a p-value of 0.88, signaling that there was no significant difference between any of the groups. column13: Multimodal machine learning for solar energetic particle forecasting using HOPE signals and EUV images
column14:
Solar Energetic Particle (SEP) events can potentially cause harm to satellites, astronauts, and communication systems. Improving SEP forecasting is important because earlier warnings are able to help reduce damage to important systems. This project develops a machine learning model to predict SEP events using solar X-ray and extreme ultraviolet observations.
GOES x-ray data were processed to identify Hope Onset Precursor Events (HOPE) signals by calculating the plasma temperature, emission measure, time derivatives, and combined HOPE score. These features were used to train a BiLSTM model that predicted the proton flux values. To improve forecasting performance, the project also combined the BiLSTM model with EUV images.
The model was able to show that thermal flare behavior, specifically rapid changes in temperature and emission measure, can give useful warnings before SEP events. Adding EUV information allowed the model to have physical precursor events and visual solar data. The completed model did not show too much improvement from previous studies and was around the same in terms of accuracy. Future improvements to this model could be increasing the data tested on it and testing it with solar cycles.

column13: Determining the periodic variability of classical Cepheids, T Tauris, and suspected variable stars using SKYNET
column14: New suspected variable stars are stars with no properly diagnosed period of variability. Variable stars are stars that change in apparent brightness over time. column13: Assessing Cambodian-Trained Machine Learning Models for Predicting Childhood Malnutrition in Myanmar
column14: Around 30% of children under five in Myanmar and 24% in Cambodia suffer from stunting as a result of malnutrition. Data-rich nations have been able to develop machine learning (ML) models that can accurately predict individual-level stunting risk using data from the Demographic and Health Surveys (DHS), however it can’t be translated to data-poor countries currently. This study evaluates the feasibility of using Cambodian-trained models to predict malnutrition among children in Myanmar by investigating cross-country transfer learning. Data from 3,755 children from Cambodia (DHS 2021-22) and 4,186 children from Myanmar (DHS 2015-16) was analyzed. Exploratory analysis displayed consistent socioeconomic predictors across countries: wealth index, maternal education, child age, maternal BMI, and child education. Despite strong population-level effects, these variables showed zero model weight, suggesting fitting challenges from SMOTE balancing and 5-fold cross-validation of ensemble techniques. Cambodian validation achieved F1 and AUC scores of 0.37 and 0.55 respectively, performing below the 76.4% dummy classifier baseline. Myanmar external validation displayed scores of 0.49 and 0.62 respectively as well. Unexpectedly, Myanmar outperformed Cambodia, despite being external validation, indicating the model failed to learn robust Cambodia-specific patterns. The six year temporal gap between surveys (2015-2021) likely explains performance degradation. Validating on contemporary Myanmar DHS 2022 data, when available, would test whether temporal misalignment rather than geographic differences drives poor transfer. Future work requires larger pooled samples (8000+ children), expanded features (15-20 variables), advanced algorithms (XGBoost), and minimal threshold calibration while preserving transfer learning variables.

column13: Examining the Effects of Salt Type, Supporting Material, and Salt Loading on Thermochemical Reaction Efficiency for Renewable Energy Storage
column14: As renewable energy sources expand, the need for reliable and cost-effective storage systems has become increasingly important. The area under each temperature curve was analyzed to determine the energy released as heat. This was then compared to the theoretical chemical energy input of each composite based on its respective dehydration enthalpy.  Preliminary results find that certain composites show improved long-term stability across cycles, while others have higher thermal efficiency percentages and greater heat release during hydration. These findings suggest that material composition can significantly change the performance of thermochemical storage systems. By identifying which composites optimize thermal performance, this project advances the development of thermochemical energy storage systems in renewable applications.

column14: Prostate cancer is the second most common cancer in the world, and its incidence has been increasing rapidly due to an aging population. This causes the cells to proliferate or undergo a process called epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), where polarized epithelial cells transform into migratory mesenchymal cells, which can form more tumors. Decreasing the rate of EMT may result in a decrease in metastasis. Ibuprofen is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug which blocks the protein cyclooxegenase. A function of cyclooxegenase is converting arachidonic acid, an essential fatty acid, into prostaglandins, which are responsible for pain and inflammation. Since an initial cause of EMT is inflammation, administering an anti-inflammatory drug, may prevent EMT, preventing cancer metastasis. This hypothesis was tested by treating PC-3  prostatic adenocarcinoma cells with concentrations of ibuprofen (300 μM, 600 μM, 1200 μM) and hypoxic conditions for 48 hours, to trigger EMT. Then, the cells’ migration was measured using a scratch assay. An increase in ibuprofen concentration resulted in a significant (p<0.01) decrease in percent change in area of the scratch, or migration. However, there was not a significant difference (p=1.00) in migration between cells in hypoxia and normoxia. Later it was discovered that EMT already occurs in PC-3 cells without induction through hypoxia. Therefore, there is evidence to support that ibuprofen treatments may be able to reduce the instance of prostate cancer metastasis and potentially EMT.

column13: Determining the Efficacy of A-803467 on Reducing the Sodium Quantity in F11 Cells Modeling Peripheral Neuropathy
column14: Peripheral neuropathy is a chronic pain disorder affecting hundreds of thousands of people each year. Recent research has determined that voltage gated sodium channels NaV1.7 and NaV1.8 play a critical role in neuropathic pain. Excessive firing of these channels allows increased sodium into the cell resulting in pain. Blocking the activity of these sodium channels in the nerve has been shown to reduce the feeling of pain by the patient. A-803467, a specific sodium channel blocker, has been shown to mitigate the effects of acute pain in animal models, but its role in treating chronic pain is uncertain. This project modeled chronic pain in F11 dorsal root ganglia cells by exposing them to TNF-α which increased their intracellular sodium concentration. Cells were then treated with increasing concentrations (0, 4, 20, 100, 500, and 2500 nM) of A-803467 and the intracellular sodium concentration was measured using the fluorescent sodium indicator, CoroNa Green. A Kruskal-Wallis test demonstrated that some statistically significant differences (p < 0.05) were observed between the changes in fluorescence of different groups. These data indicate that A-803467 is able to reduce the intracellular sodium concentration in a TNF-α induced pain model in F11 cells only at the highest concentration. Large variations within the current data sets suggest that fine-tuning of the procedure could produce more precise results: making fresh solutions of TNF-α, CoroNa Green, and A-803467 before each trial may raise the quality of fluorescence measurements. Data collection is ongoing.

Autophagy recycles damaged and misfunctioning organnelles in the cell to preserve and reuse resources. HuR is a RNA binding protein that binds to mRNAs and prevents them from degrading effectively stabilizing them for protein synthesis. column13: Finding a Binding Agent to Neutralize the Echicetin Protein in Echis carinatus Venom Using Molecular Docking
column14: Echis carinatus is a deadly species of viper native to India. Due to high intraspecific variation in the composition of the species’s venom, antivenom is often ineffective at treating victims. Additionally, there are very few hospitals in rural India, and most are underequipped. This means treatment is delayed by long travel times to hospitals which may not have antivenom immediately on hand. Echicetin is a protein unique to Echis carinatus venom which can either cause or prevent blood clotting to lethal degrees. The goal of this project was to find a molecule which could specifically target and neutralize Echicetin. Molecular Docking was done between Echicetin and molecules at two sites on Echicetin, to assess their ability to bind to Echicetin at those sites. The molecules used for this experiment were taken from the World Subset of the ZINC15 database. Prior to Molecular Docking, molecules were sorted into smaller subsets of similar molecules via Chemical Clustering. This allowed us to take a few molecules from each subset for Molecular Docking, which could then be used to predict bond stability for the rest of their subset. Stability of a bond is measured in kcal/mol, with a lower score indicating a more stable bond. The average scores for a molecule at a binding point were compared to each other, with the molecules with lower averages being considered more effective. Currently, for the IgMk binding site, Zotepine has the most stable bonds, with an average score of -20.2886 kcal/mol. For the Glycoprotein lb binding site, Dexbrompheniramine has the most stable bonds, with an average score of -24.25465 kcal/mol. Diamthazole has the second most stable bonds for both sites, with an average score of -19.8512 kcal/mol for the IgMk binding site and -24.02826 kcal/mol for the Glycoprotein lb binding site. These are currently considered the most effective molecules for binding with Echicetin. However, data collection is still ongoing. Additionally, research needs to be done on the molecules considered the most effective to assess which are actually safe for medical use. The hope is that these molecules, or other molecules from their subsets, could be used in a supplemental medicine to lessen the effects of Echis carinatus venom while victims are traveling or waiting for treatment.

column13: Investigating the Effect of Modulating the Hsp90-Aha1 Chaperone System with siRNA by Studying Chloride Ion Flux of Cystic Fibrosis Class II (ΔF508) and III (G551D) CFTR Mutations
column14: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is an autosomal recessive disease caused by mutations in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) gene, which encodes a chloride ion channel essential for normal airway function. Current mutation-specific treatments do not fully restore CFTR activity, highlighting the need for novel therapeutic strategies. Protein folding and trafficking are regulated by cellular chaperones, including Hsp90 and its co-chaperone Aha1; Aha1 accelerates protein triaging, which may lead to premature folding. This study investigated whether silencing Aha1 via siRNA could improve chloride ion flux in Class II (ΔF508) and Class III (G551D) CFTR cell lines. WT, G551D, and ΔF508 cells were seeded into black 96-well plates and treated with Aha1-silencing siRNA at concentrations of 1.88, 3.75, 7.5, 15, and 30 nM. Chloride efflux was then measured using an SPQ fluorophore assay in which nitrate ions replaced chloride to induce efflux, with functional channel activity reported as the t10/t0 ratio and differences across concentrations assessed using the Kruskal-Wallis test.  No statistically significant change in chloride ion flux was observed across any siRNA concentration for ΔF508 (p = 1.00), G551D (p = 0.96), or WT cells (p = 0.68), indicating no measurable rescue of CFTR function. These findings suggest that Aha1 knockdown alone is insufficient to restore CFTR-mediated chloride transport under these conditions, and future work should improve transfection efficiency, evaluate alternate model systems, and explore additional Hsp90 co-chaperones such as p23 as more effective targets for mutation specific CF therapy.

column13: The Effect of Silk Fibroin and Lipopolysaccharides on Wound Healing in Keratinocytes

Lipopolysaccharides (LPS) can be used to induce inflammation in cells to see if AREG can accelerate wound closure alongside inflammation. Silk Fibroin (SF) is a protein found in silk with physicochemical properties that affect wound healing pathways (Indrakumar et al., 2024). The purpose of this study is to determine whether AREG accelerates wound closure and whether SF improves skin graft function through cell migration in a wound model. A scratch assay was used to assess how many HaCaT keratinocyte cells migrated with varying LPS and a single SF dose. A Kruskal-Wallis test indicated that LPS significantly increased HaCaT cell migration compared to controls (p < 0.01). In addition, 0.4% SF significantly increased HaCaT cell migration compared with 0% SF treatment (p < 0.04). Therefore, the cells were more directed to close the wound at LPS concentrations of 3.75 ug/mL, 7.5 ug/mL, and 15 ug/mL, and at the 0.4% SF concentration.

column14: The enzyme PETase, discovered in Ideonella sakaiensis in 2016, is notable for being one of the only known enzymes to hydrolyze plastic, specifically polyethylene terephthalate. column13: The Effect of CHST8 Sulfotransferase Gene Expression on the Glycan Binding Affinity  of IgG Fc Receptors to Effector Cells in Model Autoimmune Response
column14:
In the context of autoimmune disease, the detrimental effect of autoantibodies can be attributed to Antibody-Dependent Cellular Cytotoxicity (ADCC). This reaction is caused by the binding between the Fc region of IgG antibody-antigen complexes and inflammatory effector cells. To facilitate binding, glycosylation modifications on the surface of IgG act as receptors. This study serves to alter the post-glycosylation modification of sulfate in order to inhibit a common ADCC signaling pathway present in all antibodies.
Anti-CD8a were grown from hybridoma cells and treated with an siRNA that blocks the production of the CHST8 sulfate group. Three siRNA concentrations were tested  (12.5, 25, 50 nmol), as well as two controls with no siRNA. A model antibody-antigen complex was established through a Dynabeads protocol, using protein A as the model antigen. The bound IgG complexes were quantified by a nanophotometer in ug/mL. After averaging all IgG concentrations, no correlation was found between siRNA concentrations and the bound antibodies. A Kruskal-Wallis on the data indicated no statistical differences between any of the groups (P-value = 0.32). However, the siRNA concentration of 12.5 had the lowest number of IgG (13.6 ug/mL), suggesting that this level of treatment is optimal for reducing antibody binding affinity. Although these findings signify little reaction to the siRNA treatment, more data is needed to confirm the true effect of sulfate removal, as only one trial was completed.  This focus on a common signaling pathway across all antibodies offers a versatile option for preventing autoimmune pathogenesis.

column13: Investigating the Effect of Different Quantum Dot Frequencies with Luciferin on Luciferase-Expressing Breast Cancer Cells
column14: Breast cancer, the second leading cause of cancer death in women worldwide, is caused when abnormal breast cells grow out of control and form tumors. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a well-established treatment that utilizes light excitation of a photosensitive substance, which produces oxygen-related cytotoxic intermediates called reactive oxygen species (ROS) to kill cancer cells. PDT is non-invasive and exhibits minimal side effects; however, it is limited in its accessibility for endogenous cancerous growth, due to its use of external light sources like lamps, lasers, and LEDs to excite internal photosensitizers (Alizadeh Nobari et al., 2024). To overcome the constraints of PDT, it is necessary to research methods that facilitate effective internal excitation of photosensitizers. Theodossiou et al. attempted to create an intracellular photodynamic therapy through the activation of a photosensitive substance by producing bioluminescence within the cancer cells (2003). Bioluminescence is the emission of light in living organisms, and it can occur through the chemical reaction between luciferin, luciferase, and oxygen (Schramm & Weiß, 2024). The combination of bioluminescent agents (luciferin and luciferase-expressing cancer cells) and a photosensitizer resulted in cancer cell death from the production of ROS (Theodossiou et al., 2003). Quantum dots (QDs) are nanoparticles made from semiconducting materials that release specific frequencies of light corresponding to their size. Additionally, QDs can generate ROS upon excitation, showcasing potential as a photosensitizer. Creating a bioluminescence-activated intracellular PDT, luciferin would be added to luciferase-expressing breast cancer cells to create bioluminescence, and QDs would be added to act as the photosensitizer.

column13: Converting Mechanical Energy to Electrical Energy via Piezoelectric/Electromagnetic Panels

column13: Use of L-Ascorbic Acid to Mitigate Effect of Polystyrene Microplastics on Growth of Brassica rapa
column14: Behind global warming, microplastic contamination is the second most emerging environmental issue. Plastic additive chemicals leach out of microplastics, causing irreversible damage to soil, detrimentally harming crops and produce. Microplastics cause an excess in production of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) in plants, leading to oxidative stress and lowered growth rates. Plants produce antioxidants to mitigate ROS; therefore, L-ascorbic acid, an antioxidant, was applied through drench solution to reduce the negative effects of ROS. Brassica rapa was chosen as a model organism because it is a Wisconsin fast plant with high germination rates and a rapid growth cycle. Groups consisted of one control, one negative, and three positive, each containing 50 pots with three Brassica rapa seeds per pot. The purpose is to test the effectiveness of three concentrations of L-ascorbic acid (0.005%, 0.01%, and 0.015%) in 1mL doses for reducing polystyrene microplastic (PS MP) stress on the growth of Brassica rapa. With effective treatment, the biomass of Brassica rapa varied based on the experimental group, exposure to PS MPs, and concentration of L-ascorbic acid. 300mg of 5mm of styrofoam was incorporated into soilless media of each experimental pot. To quantify growth, biomass in grams of Brassica rapa was measured by cutting at soil line. An ANOVA test was conducted for the data collected, providing a p-value of 0.02, indicating that the median biomass increases as L-ascorbic acid concentration increases. Thus, the 0.015% concentration was the most effective at mitigating the negative effects of PS MPs.

column13: Does Low Sound Frequency Sound Om Affect stress Levels in Daphnia magna
column14:
Does the Low Frequency OM Sound Affect Stress Levels in Daphnia magna?

Abhi Gyanwali

Cardiovascular problems and heart diseases caused by stress affects a lot of people. The Om sound is the universal sound in Hindu scriptures including chanting mantras. It has a deep resonating effect, and it also has a calming effect. The goal of this project was to investigate whether stress can be reduced by being exposed to the Om sound.  Daphnia magna is a model organism because it has stress responses similar to that of humans and was used for this study. If the Om treatment is provided to Daphnia then the heart rate will decrease. For all treatments the 16 Daphnia magna were exposed to stress for one minute then heart beat was measured. For each treatment, 2 days apart, each Daphnia magna was placed under the microscope and then exposed to Om for 20, 15 and 10 seconds respectively. Average heart rate decreased 40% for the 20 second treatment; 27% for 15 second treatment and 29% for the 10 second treatment, indicating that the 20 second Om treatment had the greatest effect on reducing stress. column13: Developing a Plasmonically Enhanced Phone Microscope System and Deconvolution Model to Improve Blood Microscopy Diagnosis in Under-Resourced Areas
column14:
Microscopy is the most common medical diagnostic tool for identifying blood-borne diseases, infections, and hematologic disorders through tests such as Complete Blood Counts (KREND Medical, 2025). In underdeveloped and rural regions, limited access to microscopy and medical laboratories forces samples to travel between 24 and 36 hours for imaging, delaying critical diagnoses (Owusu et al., 2025). For bloodstream infections, each hour without treatment increases mortality by 8% (McGregor, 2014). Smartphone microscopes offer a method for expanding access to diagnostic imaging. However, current systems suffer from low contrast, limited resolution, and optical aberrations that restrict reliable diagnosis (Skandarajah et al., 2014). 
An optical and computational imaging system was developed to enhance smartphone blood smear microscopy. The optical subsystem improves raw signal acquisition using a laser conditioning lens for uniform illumination and a gold nanoparticle–coated plasmonic coverslip to amplify transmitted and scattered light. The resulting images are refined using a blind deconvolution model that estimates the system’s point spread function and removes blur through iterative reconstructions, recovering fine structures and sharpening cell boundaries. Baseline smartphone microscope images, enhanced smartphone microscope images, and lab-grade images were analyzed in ImageJ to extract cell counts, average cell size, and circularity. Measurement results were compared to lab-grade images and percent differences calculated. Preliminary data show the enhanced system achieving on average 2.5% improved counting accuracy, 1.73% improved size measurement accuracy, and 4.2% improved area measurement accuracy. Future improvements include advancing color accuracy of images to expand applicability to color-dependent diagnoses such as anemia and hemolysis.

Dictyostelium discoideum(a BSL-1 organism) was the strain chosen. By analyzing how the tunable properties of the hydrogel influence the behavior of the cells, biomaterial design for guiding cell moment was improved.
Gelatin and agarose(types of hydrogels) were created and tested. The GelMA hydrogels were prepared at different concentrations of photoinitiator and gelatin powder to vary the stiffness. The difference in structure was tested through swelling and deformation tests and in the results it was revealed that increasing the concentration of GelMA powder led to reduced swelling and resistance to deformation. 

          This indicates that Gelatin methacrylate hydrogels can consistently be produced with various physical and chemical properties that are able to replicate the landscape of our bodies. preparation of cells for testing began with waking the cells and placing them in a centrifuge to separate the cells from the medium.

           The results that have been collected so far confirm the hypothesis that hydrogels can be characterized to create various three-dimensional environments. These hydrogels can be used for modeling cellular movement aiding the creation of immune response treatment and wound healing systems. column13: Evaluating The Effects of Magnesium-Enriched Coral Plugs on Growth of Green Star Polyp and Hollywood Stunner Chalice
column14:
Coral reefs are rapidly declining due to climate change and other human impacts, increasing the need for effective restoration methods. Coral transplantation attaches fragments to substrates to rebuild habitat, yet the influence of substrate chemistry on adult fragment growth is not well studied. Lime based substrate with magnesium carbonate (MgCO3) was tested to evaluate if it changes performance of soft coral (Green Star Polyup; GSP) and stony coral (Hollywood Stunner Chalice; HSC) compared to lime controls and conventional coral plugs. 

Three substrate types were compared: NHL2 control disks, NHL2 + 15 wt% MgCO₃ disks, and conventional coral plugs (evaAquatics 1” ceramic plug) (n=10 each; 30 total). Lime disks were cast, CO₂ conditioned for 7 days, then soaked in seawater for 7 days before aquarium exposure. Five fragments of each species were attached per substrate type (one fragment per disk/plug; n=15 per species). Mass and size were recorded weekly in a stabilized aquarium system.

Responses were species-dependent. Surviving GSP fragments on MgCO₃ disks showed the highest growth compared with controls, suggesting MgCO₃ may promote soft-coral growth under stable conditions. HSC growth on MgCO₃ disks was similar to NHL2 controls, while the largest HSC mass gains occurred on conventional plugs, implying other plug properties drive performance. 

Overall, MgCO₃ appears to influence soft coral performance and shows potential as a substrate additive for accelerated restoration, but showed no clear benefit for HSC. Future work will use replicated tanks with standardized algae management, additional soft/stony species, and an extended concentration.

column13: Optimizing Metal-Organic Framework Monoliths for Carbon Dioxide Adsorption Using Computer-Aided Design and Finite Element Analysis
column14: As energy needs increase around the world, carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions rise alongside them, contributing to rapid climate change. Adsorption-based carbon capture aims to create carbon negativity by removing CO2 output from air. Currently, metal-organic framework adsorbents like HKUST-1 and MOF-5 have been promising materials for this task due to their unique cage shape; however, they have not been adequately optimized for use in a monolith structure, which packs the adsorbent into a solid shape with macropores for gas flow. Additionally, the few studies that do attempt to speed up optimization through simulation fail to address economic concerns through both material cost and expenses of the simulation software itself. This study aims to address these issues by creating a free, open-source pipeline to optimize the mass transfer, heat transfer, structural stability, and material cost of a MOF monolith. Four pore shapes and five pore sizes were selected, and the material properties and costs of HKUST-1 and MOF-5 were compared. Several key findings were noted, including a high capability for mass transfer in monoliths with large, square-shaped pores; in addition, HKUST-1 was identified to be more cost-efficient over MOF-5.

column13: Predicting rapid intensification of tropical cyclones 1-2 days in advance in the Intra-Americas Sea using standard signals, diurnal warming, and loop current context
column14: This study examines whether novel ocean predictors—diurnal warm-layer amplitude (DWLA) and Loop Current proximity—can improve 24-hour rapid intensification (RI) prediction for tropical cyclones in the Intra-Americas Sea beyond standard predictors such as sea surface temperature (SST), vertical wind shear, and mid-level humidity. Atlantic basin storms from 2010–2022 were analyzed using HURDAT2 best-track data, with RI defined as an increase of at least 30 knots in maximum sustained winds within 24 hours. Storm-centered environmental variables were derived from OISST, TCHP, and ERA5 datasets, and two machine learning models, logistic regression and calibrated XGBoost, were trained using storm-blocked cross-validation. The calibrated XGBoost model achieved an AUC of approximately 0.68 and a Brier score of 0.059, indicating substantially stronger probability calibration than the logistic regression baseline. In addition, RI cases showed consistently warmer SST values than non-RI cases, supporting the role of upper-ocean heat in storm strengthening. These results suggest that machine learning combined with oceanic context can improve the reliability of RI forecasts and provide a promising framework for future tropical cyclone prediction research.

column13: Design and Implementation of a Residential Air Pollution Detector
column14:
Indoor air pollution can be cause for numerous health concerns such as asthma, fatigue,
and cognitive decline. Pollutants can come from things like paint, cleaning supplies, pet dander, cooking, and skincare substances. Particulate matter (PM) is a type of air pollution that often comes from cooking or pets, and is classified by diameter (in micrometers) as PM1, PM2.5, or PM10. Another group of dangerous air pollutants are chemical gases, which include carbon monoxide (CO) and Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs). Existing solutions to indoor air pollution in residential settings include HEPA filters, which are very effective at removing particulate matter but unable to remove gaseous pollutants. Another existing solution is activated carbon purifiers, which can remove VOCs and gases effectively, though they’re not fully effective with carbon monoxide, ammonia, bacteria, arsenic, nitrates, and some other metals. Infrared Spectroscopy is an effective method for detecting air pollutants, especially carbon oxides, nitrogen compounds, hydrocarbons, ammonia, etc. This can be combined with adsorption, which can be used to remove air pollutants by sticking them with solid surfaces. This project aims to build an air filter that is able to detect air pollutants, specifically Ammonia, present in air. The project will be catered to residential settings, and so it will be cost effective, aiming below $300. It involves shining a 1530 nm laser through air into a photodiode sensor, which sends readings through a transimpedance amplifier and arduino into a computer program, which alerts the user if Ammonia is highly detected. The laser and sensor are contained in a 3D printed box, which includes a fan and tilted vents for air circulation.

column13: Updated Design and Implementation of a High-Performance Handheld Gaming Device Based on Xbox Series S Architecture
column14: Modern handheld gaming devices fail to meet the performance requirements of contemporary AAA gaming, and the few that do come at a prohibitive cost. Popular options such as the Nintendo Switch and Steam Deck rely on mobile-class processors that cannot sustain the 60+ FPS at 1080p expected of modern titles, while higher-performing devices like the ASUS ROG Ally X demand prices approaching $1,000, making console-quality portable gaming inaccessible to most consumers. This project seeks to address this gap by converting an Xbox Series S console into a true handheld system. The solution involves custom hardware integration, including a portable power management system using a trigger board and buck-boost converter, a lithium-ion battery pack with a dedicated battery management system, a compact centrifugal fan and aluminum heat sink for thermal management, and modified Xbox controller PCBs for ergonomic input. A modular development approach was used so that each subsystem could be independently tested and verified before full integration. Cross-platform performance benchmarking using titles such as Fortnite at 1080p with medium graphics presets was conducted to evaluate the prototype against commercial handhelds including the Nintendo Switch 2, Steam Deck OLED, ASUS ROG Ally, ASUS ROG Ally X, and Lenovo Legion Go. Initial benchmark results show the prototype achieving 120 FPS, and further testing including input latency measurement, extended battery endurance evaluation, and full thermal assessment under an enclosed chassis is ongoing as display and controller integration is completed. This research aims to demonstrate that console-level performance is achievable in a portable form factor at a competitive price point, and to provide a documented framework for future portable console development.

column13: Neuroquest
column14:
Neurocognitive diseases and cognitive disorders such as ADHD have a severe impact on an individual’s ability to focus. With almost no treatment options for these disorders available, NeuroQuest focuses on creating a brain-computer interface system to enhance cognitive abilities, specifically focus and attention. By using an electroencephalography (EEG), the signals given by a user's brain will be taken in and converted into video game movements using SSVEP (Steady-State Visually Evoked Potential). The focus levels needed at the beginning of the game will start very low, but as time goes on, the game will encourage the user to focus more on objects to be able to do certain actions, such as picking up a ball or moving an object. Through NeuroQuest, users will be motivated to improve and maintain their focus, potentially mitigating the symptoms that come with these diseases and disorders.
	This project will use two main Machine Learning models: One to take in the EEG signals and interpret them as game inputs, and one to adjust the level of focus and concentration needed to complete a task based on the user’s progress. The models will be created in Python with self-collected data and online datasets. The game will be built in Unity using C#, with custom animations and assets. The initial game for testing of the NeuroQuest will be called FocusFactory, where users will be sorting objects into different containers using a toggle system: One input causes blocks to go one way, and no input causes them to go the other. Final evaluation will be considered using EEG attention scores, and in-game performance. NeuroQuest offers anyone a new tool for cognitive and focus challenges. While brain signal data will be used from healthy patients, NeuroQuest has the potential to transform cognitive therapy with gamified and personalized neurotechnology.

column13: Real-time Detection & Response system for Oidium neolycopersici and similar diseases using Electrochemical and VOC-based sensing.
column14: Plant diseases cause approximately 20-40% of global crop losses annually, valued at around $220 billion. Current treatments for many of these diseases via the application of pesticides are costly ($3000 /hectare/year) and harming both the environment and ourselves. An alternative solution is through targeted application of pesticides and quarantining, which is found to be significantly cheaper, but requires early diagnosis of diseased crops. By the time diseases like Powdery Mildew’s symptoms are shown, it has already spread and lowered yield by up to 40%. Thus, this project proposed a system that aims to detect the diagnosis of Powdery Mildew-like plant diseases early via VOC (Volatile Organic Chemicals) and Electrochemical (EC) sensing in plants. VOC and EC data were collected for mint plants and Fern Neprophilia (both infected & not-infected) & fed into a machine learning pipeline to classify. Both an MQ2 gas sensor and a BME680 sensor were used to collect data on VOC concentrations, gas/particulate matter concentrations, temperature, and humidity. A decision tree was trained to identify between infected and diseased groups, achieving an accuracy of 92%. The final model was deployed on an M5Stack-based sensor to create a real-time interactive dashboard for plant disease tracking and prevention.
