column13: Pulse + Petals
column14: This interdisciplinary project explores fashion technology and the integration of internal responses through the creation of a bio-mimetic and bio-responsive dress. This dress features 3D printed five-petaled flower mechanisms which are controlled by real-time PPG heart rate data. This project interprets the wearer’s physiological state and translates it into expression through the flowers, creating a visual connection between the two. The technical methodology integrates robotic design and electronics. A sensor unit houses a MAX30102 pulse oximeter connected to a ESP32-C3 microcontroller. This microcontroller processes the heart rate data taken from the MAX30102 and transmits it wirelessly through ESP-NOW to another “receiver” ESP32-C3 microcontroller. The receiver is hidden and connected to a Teensy 4.1 within the garment. Two 3D printed flower mechanisms which are controlled by one servo each via a rack and pinion mechanism are embedded into the dress. Teensy 4.1 then coordinates the servos to create a blooming effect in each of the flower mechanisms. When the user’s heart rate is considered low (60-70 BPM), the flower mechanisms bloom in a slow, calmer motion, while higher heart rates (100-120 BPM) produce faster and more energetic motions. This range of excitement creates a representation of the user’s internal state, which demonstrates nonverbal communication through bio-responsive fashion. Overall, this work combines biomimetic mechanical design and interconnected sensor networks that can transform clothing into emotional garments. The project contributes to emerging fields of affective computing and smart textiles while demonstrating technology’s potential to become interconnected with fashion and human connection.

column13: Smart Puck
column14: This project explores the development of an affordable and user-friendly smart home device, referred to as a “smart puck,” designed to make existing “dumb” devices - such as basic doorbells, lights, and switches - smart. Current platforms like Home Assistant and Homebridge offer powerful customization but often require costly hardware, technical networking knowledge, and complex setup. These barriers prevent many everyday users from accessing the benefits of smart home automation. The proposed smart puck addresses these challenges by combining modular hardware with the Matter and Thread protocols to provide reliable, low-power connectivity and simple integration. Research will focus on identifying the minimum hardware requirements for supporting Matter/Thread, selecting microcontrollers capable of efficient device management, and designing flexible GPIO ports for attaching sensors and relays. In addition, user-friendly web-based setup tools will be explored to eliminate the steep learning curve found in existing systems. The expected outcome is a compact, cost-effective prototype that empowers users to customize their smart home systems by upgrading the devices they already own. This research contributes to the smart home ecosystem by bridging the gap between complex open-source platforms and consumer-ready solutions that prioritize accessibility and affordability.

column13: Fit-Net
column14: FitNet aims to improve exercise safety and effectiveness by using machine learning and computer vision to analyze users’ form during workouts. Many gym-goers, especially beginners, struggle to determine if they are performing exercises correctly, often leading to suboptimal results or even injury. This project will develop a downloadable mobile application that takes in a video of a user performing an exercise and returns feedback on their posture and movement. The core of the system will rely on pose estimation technologies to track skeletal keypoints over time and detect form deviations. At first, an initial estimation of shoulder and waist lengths are used to detect an optimal angle for workouts. Then the movement of elbows and shoulders is used to track how good the form of a bicep curl exercise is performed. Current resources provide general information about exercise form but lack personalized, automatic feedback based on an individual’s actual movements. There is no accessible solution that provides customized, pose-based recommendations to help users correct their gym form in real time. As of now, the website works for bicep curls by imputing an mp4 file, while the output is several frames of the video that claim good or bad form.

column13: Improving the Efficacy of VSLAM in Dynamic Environments
column14:
Autonomous robots are increasingly deployed in human-centered environments where reliable localization and navigation are required. Visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (VSLAM) enables autonomous navigation in unknown environments. However, these approaches assume a static environment, degrading performance in dynamic scenes where moving objects create features. This research will investigate whether semantic filtering can improve the efficacy of VSLAM in dynamic environments.
 A lightweight semantic segmentation model, YOLOv8-seg, was integrated into the VSLAM pipeline prior to feature extraction to classify pixels as either dynamic or static. Features detected on these dynamic pixels were excluded from feature extraction for tracking and mapping. The modified algorithm was tested on the TUM-RGBD dataset, a popular VSLAM benchmarking dataset with various static and dynamic scenes. Performance was evaluated through Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) to measure global trajectory error, Relative Pose Error (RPE) to measure local motion consistency, and median tracking time to evaluate computational load. 
The modified algorithm reduced average ATE by 0.048 m, or around 20%, indicating an increase in global localization accuracy. However, the RPE increased by 0.012 m, or 11%, indicating a larger instability in short-term pose consistency.  The median tracking time increased by an average of 0.00138 seconds, showcasing minimal increase in computational complexity.
 These results demonstrate how semantic filtering will improve the global localization accuracy of VSLAM algorithms in dynamic environments. This would enhance the reliability of robots navigating in environments with multiple moving objects.

column13: Virtual Screening to Identify Allelochemicals that Inhibit Cyanobacterial Tubular Pili
column14: Microcystis aeruginosa is a freshwater harmful algal bloom (HAB) species that threatens accessible freshwater that constitutes 0.3% of the world’s water, releasing hepatotoxin microcystin-LR (MC-LR) identified as a potential human carcinogen and contributing to cultural eutrophication (Akinnawo, 2023). Allelochemicals have been identified as eco-friendly promising candidates for mitigating algal blooms due to their natural production via plants; however, there is uncertainty regarding the mechanism of action by which allelochemicals inhibit algal growth, and the large pool of allelochemical deems in-vitro studies ineffective and expensive (Cheng et al., 2024). Thus, our objective was to conduct in-silico screening of allelochemicals (from AlgicideDB) via molecular docking simulations, quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) analysis, and molecular dynamics simulations to identify optimal mitigators of the cyanobacterial tubular (CT) pilus, a newly discovered structure in M. aeruginosa responsible for allowing individual cells to aggregate into large blooms (Zuo et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2023; Ricca et al., 2025). With a predicted binding energy of -8.8 kcal/mol, we have identified Neo-przewaquinone A as a promising candidate for inhibiting the CT pilus. This research field has largely been conducted in-vitro, with a recent in-silico study to predict single allelochemical inhibition rates on M. aeruginosa; therefore, this study demonstrates virtual screening as an efficient method for identifying optimal candidates with a clear mechanism of action, which can then be studied further in-vitro (Khatoon et al., 2025). In-silico methods for screening large databases of allelochemicals will streamline the process of identifying optimal allelochemicals for inhibiting algal growth.

column13: The Effects of Microplastics Exposure from Nylon Tea Bags on the Regenerative Abilities of Dugesia tigrina
column14: Microplastics are an increasingly prevalent environmental pollutant, with studies showing their presence in aquatic ecosystems and even within the human body, raising concerns about their potential biological effects. This study examined how microplastics released from nylon tea bags impact the regenerative abilities of Dugesia tigrina, a model organism known for its stem cell driven tissue regeneration. It was hypothesized that increased microplastic exposure, simulated through longer tea bag steep times, would result in decreased regeneration. To test this, nylon tea bags were steeped in boiling spring water for varying durations (control, 20 minutes, and 2 days) to produce solutions with different microplastic concentrations. Planarians were amputated between the eyes and pharynx, and tail fragments were placed in the respective solutions and observed over a six-day period. Regeneration was documented using microscopy, and growth was measured in millimeters using ImageJ software. The results demonstrated a clear dose-dependent relationship, with planarians in the control group showing the greatest regeneration and those in the longest exposure group exhibiting minimal to no regrowth. Additionally, the increase in length from day 0 to day 6 decreased significantly as exposure time increased, a trend consistent across all trials. These findings suggest that microplastics may disrupt cellular processes involved in tissue repair and regeneration. Overall, this study highlights the potential biological consequences of everyday microplastic exposure and supports the use of planarians as an effective model for studying environmental toxicity and its broader implications for organismal health.

column13: The Early Developmental Effects of Daphnia magna Fed Algae Grown in the Presence of Microplastics
column14: Research indicates that there is an accumulation of 10167 tons of microplastics in the top 20m of lakes and reservoirs worldwide (Dong et al.). Exploring the effects of microplastic integration into these freshwater ecosystems is of great importance to understand the long term effects of plastic pollution. Daphnia magna are widely used as indicators of freshwater toxicity due to their sensitivity to changes in their environment. A simple food chain using green algae and Daphnia magna can evaluate the proliferation of microplastics in an ecosystem and the long term effects of these microplastics. The hypothesis was that microplastics of smaller sizes would have more detrimental effects on the reproductive quality of Daphnia magna due to their ability to permeate through the cell membrane of the algae and, therefore, create longer lasting effects. The green algae, Chlorella pyrenoidosa, was grown in the presence of microplastics and fed to D. magna which were then observed for their egg counts. After the D. magna reproduced, a 30-second locomotion assay was used to evaluate the viability of the offspring. While this research is still preliminary and more trials would need to be run in order to tell what the true effects of microplastics are in a food chain, it helps lay down some of the groundwork necessary for understanding the long-term effects of microplastics in the environment.

column13: Determining the Effect of Erythromycin and Ampicillin on Chlorella pyrenoidosa, and the Macroinvertebrate Daphnia magna
column14: Wastewater and other water sources contain many contaminants, but one of the most worrying of them all is the presence of antibiotic residues (Li et al., 2024). Antibiotic residues in wastewater can cause many detrimental effects on our environment, as the presence of these residues can cause the bacteria in the water to develop Antibiotic Resistance Genes or ARG’s. These residues are difficult to purify with typical wastewater treatments and the development of ARG’s can render antibiotics a useless treatment against sickness and infections. Chlorella pyrenoidosa has been used extensively in a wide range of environmental testing, as a model organism to assess toxicity of pollutants and monitor their effects, due to its prevalence in freshwater, sensitivity, and quick growth rate. Placed in water with antibiotic residues, Chlorella pyrenoidosa has the potential to effectively adsorb the antibiotics, more than traditional methods. Daphnia magna were used to identify how much harm the algae could do to the environment and the organisms that rely on it, as D.magna consumes it and is often used for ecotoxicological testing. This experiment is preliminary and requires further analysis, but the current results indicate that the D.magna are relatively unaffected by presence of antibiotics in the water. Further testing will need to be done in order to deduce how much effect the antibiotics have on the algae and more analysis has the potential to uncover a cost effective and simple solution to antibiotic resistance.

column13: Effects of pH and PET Microplastics on Growth of Scenedesmus quadricauda
column14: Microplastic pollution and acidification are two of the most pressing threats facing aquatic ecosystems today, yet their combined effects on freshwater primary producers remain poorly studied. PET (polyethylene terephthalate) microplastics are among the most commonly detected plastic polymers in freshwater systems, and freshwater pH is projected to decline significantly as atmospheric CO₂ continues to rise. This study investigated how PET microplastics at 100 mg/L affect the growth of the freshwater green alga Scenedesmus quadricauda at pH levels 8.4 and 7.7 over a 14-day period, measured via corrected optical density at 750 nm (OD750). Across two independent trials, PET microplastics reduced algal biomass at both pH levels, with the pH 8.4 + PET group showing approximately 12% lower growth than its control by day 14. The effect was substantially more pronounced at the lower pH: the pH 7.7 + PET group exhibited approximately 23% lower growth compared to its pH 7.7 control, and roughly 38% lower biomass than the pH 8.4 control overall. These results suggest a synergistic interaction between PET microplastics and acidification, consistent with prior findings that lower pH increases microplastic aggregation and surface reactivity with algal cells. Because microalgae like S. quadricauda form the base of aquatic food webs, their inhibited growth under combined stressors could result in reduced oxygen production, disrupted nutrient proliferation, and ecosystem instability. This study provides evidence that risk assessments for freshwater systems should consider the compounding effects of microplastics and pH, especially as both stressors intensify under future climate projections.

column13: Comparing the Effect of Sodium Alginate-Gelatin Hydrogel Dressings on the Wound Closure of Burned G. mellonella Larvae to that of Liquid Bandages
column14: Hydrogels are insoluble three-dimensional networks of polymers. Though they have been established as treatments conducive to wound closure, they are not often put in direct comparison with alternative dressings. Hydrogels made from a combination of sodium alginate-gelatin (GELSA) polymers in particular have been tested in vivo and found to accelerate wound closure. Furthermore, New Skin liquid bandage serves a film dressing that notoriously lacks the absorptive abilities of a hydrogel, making it an excellent positive control treatment to reveal the importance of absorption of fluids for wound healing. Therefore, GELSA hydrogel and New Skin liquid bandage were used to treat burned Galleria mellonella larvae, and the average rate of wound closure was observed based on images from which wound area was measured across a period of ten days. The median average rate of wound closure for the GELSA, New Skin, and negative control groups were calculated as 0.040, 0.061, and -0.160 mm^2 per day respectively. While a Kruskal-Wallis test and post-hocs revealed that both dressings significantly accelerated wound closure compared to an absence of treatment, the rates of wound closure between GELSA- and New Skin-treated larvae were not different. This paper validates Galleria mellonella as models of wound closure in preliminary studies due to the expected results from the positive control group, and its findings suggest that while GELSA and New Skin seem to be equally effective treatments, further research must be conducted to outweigh outliers in the data.

column13: Effects of PLA and ABS Microplastics on The Heart Rate, Reproduction, and Growth of Daphnia Magna
column14: In the past decade, the use of biodegradable plastics as an alternative to non-biodegradable plastics has risen dramatically and the market is projected to rise by 160% over the next 5 years. However, there is very limited research on their harmfulness to the environment compared to non-biodegradable plastics. Both biodegradable and non-biodegradable plastics create microplastics found on land and in water. Microplastics are a danger to ocean and freshwater ecosystems as they negatively affect marine and freshwater organisms. The purpose of this study was to determine the difference between biodegradable and non-biodegradable microplastics. This was done by comparing the effect of biodegradable microplastics made from PLA (Polylactic acid) to non-biodegradable microplastics made from ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene) on the freshwater Macroinvertebrate Daphnia magna, a keystone species in freshwater ecosystems. This study found that there was a statistically significant difference between the effects which microplastics made from PLA and ABS had on the heart rate of Daphnia magna (p < 0.01). ABS microplastics showed greater impact on heart rate compared to PLA microplastics with a far lower average heart rate. Additionally, the study found that microplastics made from PLA and ABS did not have statistically significant differences in their effect towards the number of eggs produced (p = 0.79) and the size of Daphnia magna (p = 0.92). The results of this study contributes to the limited research done comparing the use of biodegradable microplastics to non-biodegradable microplastics on living organisms and paves the way for additional research to be done on this topic.

column13: A Meta-Analysis Between Humans' Biofactors and Circadian Rhythms
column14: Circadian rhythms are biological loops that regulate essential biological processes in organisms, and play key roles in metabolism and overall health. Disruptions to these rhythms are linked to metabolic disorders; however, few meta-analyses have been conducted to investigate this relationship in an overarching study. This study aimed to analyze the association between disruptions in circadian rhythms and metabolic factors through a meta-analysis of recent medical studies. A review of PubMed and ScienceDirect databases identified relevant studies from 2020-2025 using circadian and metabolism keywords. Studies that met criteria after being screened for relevance using the Cochrane’s Risk of Bias Tool were analyzed for means, standard deviations, and sample sizes when available. Effect sizes were calculated using a Hedges’ g, and a random-effects model was utilized to account for variability across study methodologies. A glucose-related outcomes subgroup analysis was conducted as well, with the pooled analysis exhibiting a moderate effect favoring circadian-aligned interventions. However, the result was not statistically significant (g = -0.60, 95% CI [-1.27, 0.06], p = 0.076). Yet the glucose-only subgroup demonstrated significant improvement (g = -1.17, 95% CI [-2.09, -0.24], p = 0.013). The high heterogeneity, I2 = 77.6%, indicates differences in study design, intervention type, and outcome measurements, which limited direct comparisons. These findings suggest circadian timing may be influential in metabolic health, specifically glucose regulation. Future research should aim to address inconsistencies in study design, and investigate more specific disruptions, such as meal timings, to gain a more precise understanding of circadian disruptions.

column13: Evaluating The Cost-Effectiveness Of Chlorella vulgaris Biodiesel Cultivation Methods
column14: As much as 38.11 billion tonnes of carbon dioxide were released into the environment in 2025, mainly due to fossil fuel consumption. Biofuels provide an alternative and sustainable source of hydrocarbon based fuels generated from lipids. The use of algae as the feedstock of choice is based on its biomass production rate, high energy content, and ability to grow in low nutrient water; however, existing approaches for algae cultivation remain too expensive to implement. The two most prevalent growth methodologies include open pond reactors (OPR) and the newer photobioreactors (PBR). In this study, the energy content of biodiesel derived from the algal species Chlorella vulgaris, cultivated under open-pond reactor (OPR) conditions using natural sunlight and photobioreactor (PBR) conditions using artificial sunlight, was determined. After both colonies reached maturity, we utilized a chemical process known as transesterification to turn the biomass into usable biodiesel. The energy of the fuels was measured using a calorimeter to determine the heat released during combustion. From this, we calculated the calorific value of each fuel based on the mass of the biodiesel combusted, resulting in 4.84 kJ/g for the PBR fuel and 4.99 kJ/g for the OPR fuel. Calorific values of biodiesel samples prepared through open-pond reactors were slightly higher compared to biodiesel prepared using artificial UV light, implying that OPR could be cheaper but offer better or similar energy output. The findings of this study reveal that a less costly approach to algae cultivation could be a viable strategy toward the development of more economically feasible biofuels.

column13: Evaluating the Impact Of Microplastic Ingestion and Hydrogel Treatment On the Growth and Regeneration In Blackworms (Lumbriculus variegatus)
column14: Microplastic pollution is accumulating in freshwater ecosystems, yet its effects on aquatic invertebrate regeneration remain poorly understood. Regeneration is critical for blackworm survival following injury. Hydrogels, particularly sodium alginate beads, have been studied for their ability to absorb and trap microplastics from water systems. However, it is not known whether hydrogel beads reduce internal microplastic accumulation and biological impacts when both are present in the same environment. To address this gap, this research investigated how microplastic concentration affects blackworm regeneration and whether sodium alginate hydrogel can mitigate this damage. Blackworms were chosen as the model organism due to their regenerative capabilities and ability to consume and concentrate microplastics in their secretions. The blackworms were divided into eight groups: Control (0 p/mL), Low (10 p/mL), Medium (100 p/mL), and High (1000 p/mL) microplastic concentrations with and without hydrogel. After seven-day exposure, worms were amputated and regeneration was measured over 19 days. Results showed dose-dependent impairment: control worms regenerated 8.15 mm while Low, Medium, and High microplastic groups regenerated 7.20 mm, 6.07 mm, and 4.90 mm respectively, representing 12%, 26%, and 40% reductions. Sodium alginate hydrogel significantly improved regeneration at all concentrations, with the strongest effect at high microplastic levels (27.8% improvement). High microplastic exposure also reduced segment formation by 46% and caused 20% mortality. This study demonstrates that microplastic concentrations significantly impair regeneration in freshwater invertebrates and that sodium alginate hydrogel offers a potential mitigation strategy for protecting aquatic ecosystems.

column13: Effects of Oral Exposure to PET Microplastics on Short-Term Memory and Motor Function in Drosophila melanogaster
column14: Microplastics (MPs) are widespread environmental pollutants, with humans consuming approximately 0.71 grams daily. Previous studies have shown that MPs can cross the blood-brain barrier and potentially disrupt neurotransmitter activity, however, their effects on memory and motility. This study investigates the effects of oral exposure to polyethylene terephthalate (PET) microplastics on the short-term memory and motor function of Drosophila melanogaster (fruit flies). Fruit flies were used because they share 70% genetic identity with humans and reproduce quickly, allowing for more data to be collected. Drosophila larvae were divided into four groups and fed food containing varying concentrations of PET microplastics. Through a Y-maze assay, flies learned to associate specific odors with a correct and wrong path. The percentage of flies making the correct choice (memory retention) and the percentage of flies completing the maze (motor function) were recorded. The results showed that a higher microplastic concentration affected both memory retention and motor function. While these results are specific to a model organism, they provide insight into how microplastic consumption may disrupt cognitive and physical functions in more complex biological systems.

column13: Circadian Phase-Dependent Effects of Zeitgeber Time Disruption on Functional Mobility in Drosophila melanogaster
column14: Circadian rhythms are internal 24-hour cycles that regulate essential biological functions such as sleep, metabolism, and behavior. Modern lifestyle factors, including artificial light exposure and irregular sleep schedules, can disrupt circadian rhythms. Researchers use Zeitgeber Time (ZT), a standardized system based on light cues, to study phases of the circadian cycle. However, most prior work has focused on molecular rather than behavioral outcomes. This study investigated whether disruptions at specific circadian phases affect functional mobility in Drosophila melanogaster. Flies were first entrained to a 12:12 light-dark cycle, then exposed to high-intensity light disruptions at defined Zeitgeber times (ZT0, ZT6, ZT12, ZT18) over five days. Functional mobility was assessed using negative geotaxis and RING assays, and data were analyzed using an ANOVA statistical test. Results showed a statistically significant effect of disruption timing on mobility at the ZT12 phase. Flies exposed at ZT12 exhibited a greater decline in functional mobility compared to controls (p < 0.01), while ZT0 and ZT6 showed no significant differences, and ZT18 showed a slight, non-significant trend toward impairment. These findings support the hypothesis that ZT12 is a particularly vulnerable phase for circadian disruption. Overall, this study demonstrates that the impact of circadian disruption depends strongly on timing, with certain phases being more sensitive than others. This highlights the importance of considering circadian phase in studies of behavioral and physiological health. The results provide a foundation for future research that can examine underlying molecular mechanisms and broader functional outcomes associated with circadian misalignment.

column13: Fashion to Fuel: Evaluating Different Cotton Blends as Viable Feedstocks in Bioethanol Production
column14: 92 million tons of textile waste are produced annually, accumulating in landfills where they are slow to decompose, while incineration remains unviable due to the production of harmful gases. This issue is exacerbated by fast fashion, which produces large amounts of clothing to meet trends while usage duration declines, requiring a more cyclical approach. At the same time, continued reliance on fossil fuels contributes to greenhouse gas emissions such as CO₂, fueling climate change. A potential solution to both issues is converting textile waste into bioethanol. Current literature focuses on optimizing pretreatment to break down cellulose crystallinity in pure cotton; however, this is unrealistic given the increasing presence of synthetic fibers in modern textiles. To address this gap, this study evaluates the viability of different cotton blends, including rayon, nylon, and polyester, as feedstocks for bioethanol production. Textile samples were shredded and underwent pretreatment using 3 M NaOH, 0.5 M HCl, and autoclaving, followed by 48-hour hydrolysis and 3-hour fermentation. Glucose and bioethanol concentrations were measured across three trials per material and compared to pure cotton as a control. A Kruskal–Wallis test indicated no statistically significant differences in glucose or bioethanol concentrations among the groups (p = 0.09 and p = 0.08). These findings suggest that cotton blends may perform comparably to pure cotton as bioethanol feedstocks. This has implications for improving the economic feasibility of textile-based bioethanol production by expanding the range of usable materials.

column13: Metacognitive Decision-making and Uncertainty Monitoring in Drosophila melanogaster
column14: In recent years, there has been increasing interest in how organisms make decisions under uncertain conditions. Metacognition, particularly uncertainty monitoring, enables the evaluation of decision reliability; however, it remains unclear whether such processes require large cortical systems or can emerge from compact neural circuits, as animal studies are often confounded by associative learning explanations. To address this, behavioral experiments were conducted using Drosophila melanogaster, a model organism that allows precise genetic manipulation of neural circuits. Mushroom body neurons were selectively silenced using the GAL4/UAS system (29BD-GAL4 × UAS-Kir2.1) to test their role in uncertainty-dependent behavior. Flies were tested in a negative geotaxis assay in which visual uncertainty was manipulated through contrast conditions (high, medium, and low). Behavioral measures included decision latency, checking latency, abort rate, and success rate, and baseline locomotion assays confirmed intact motor function. Data were analyzed using linear mixed-effects models for latency variables and generalized linear mixed models for binary outcomes. Increasing visual uncertainty significantly elevated both decision and checking latencies. Linear mixed-effects models revealed a significant effect of contrast on decision and checking latency, and a significant genotype × contrast interaction for checking latency. Mushroom body–silenced flies exhibited reduced modulation of checking behavior across contrast conditions, while locomotion remained unaffected. These findings provide behavioral evidence consistent with uncertainty monitoring in Drosophila and demonstrate that mushroom body activity contributes to uncertainty-dependent behavioral regulation. More broadly, this work supports the idea that core components of uncertainty-sensitive decision making can emerge from compact neural circuits.

column13: Testing the Effects of Rosmarinic Acid from Rosemary Herb on the Visual and Neural Impairment of Drosophila Melanogaster Induced by a High Sucrose Diet
column14: Diabetic retinopathy is a complication of diabetes mellitus that affects millions nationwide, and leads to vision blurriness, photophobia, or potentially blindness. The current treatments are limited, creating a need for therapeutic options. The study explores whether rosmarinic acid, a compound found in rosemary herb, with known antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, can reduce visual and neural impairment when induced by a high sucrose diet in Drosophila melanogaster. To test this, flies were divided into treatment groups including a normal diet, high sucrose diet (HSD), low rosmarinic acid diet (Low RSAD), high rosmarinic acid diet (High RSAD),  Low RSAD + HSD, and High RSAD + HSD. Neural function was assessed using a climbing (negative geotaxis) assay, which measured the time required for flies to climb 9 cm. A climbing assay was also conducted to measure vertical climbing. Visual function was evaluated using a phototaxis assay. Results from the climbing and phototaxis assays show that flies in the RSAD + HSD group generally climbed faster and had a higher success rate compared to flies in the HSD group. The flies in the RSAD + HSD generally climbed similarly to the control group. These findings suggest that rosmarinic acid may help reduce sucrose-induced neural impairment, likely due to its antioxidant properties. This study highlights the potential of natural antioxidants as protective agents against diet-induced neural and visual dysfunction.

column13: Effects of Blue Light on the Growth Progression of Drosophila melanogaster
column14: In recent years, there has been an exponential rise in the consumption of technology. As a result, exposure to blue light has become an increasingly pressing issue due to its adverse effects on organism health when overly exposed including disrupted circadian rhythm, neurodegeneration, and reduced lifespan. However, current research has only studied the effects of blue light on a particular stage of growth. This experiment attempts to determine how early-onset exposure to blue light will affect their livelihoods as adults. Drosophila, a model organism for behavioral studies, were exposed to several light cycles including blue light to determine their effects on their growth progression and courtship behaviors. Growth progression was determined through microscope imaging and then analysis through ImageJ and courtship behaviors were measured through the DANCE fly-behavior model. Through a Kruskal-Wallis statistical analysis, it was found that overexposure to blue light did not have a significant effect on the growth of Drosophila. However, it was found that in the 12 hour blue light-night and 12 hour day-blue light cycles that there were less attempts at courtship and shorter durations of courtship-related actions than compared to the control group of a standard 12 hour day-night cycle, suggesting that courtship was negatively impacted due to the period of stress. These results provide insight into the risks of blue light exposure and how it has the capability of affecting one’s life throughout.

column13: Observing the Effect on Plant Growth of Spinach with the Inoculation of Priestia Megaterium under Abiotic Stressors
column14: This study investigates the effect of inoculating spinach seeds with the bacterial endophyte Priestia megaterium on plant growth under abiotic stressors, specifically drought and overwatering conditions. As climate change increases the frequency of extreme weather events such as flooding and prolonged drought, developing resilient crops capable of withstanding these conditions is critical to ensuring global food security. As global population growth simultaneously increases demand for sustainable agricultural practices, endophytes, which are microorganisms that form mutualistic relationships with host plants, present a promising alternative to conventional fertilizers and pesticides. To test this, spinach seeds were inoculated with a liquid culture of Priestia megaterium prepared from a propagated agar plate, with 20 mL of the solution applied directly to seeds prior to germination. Seeds were germinated for two weeks before being transferred into a growing system consisting of 40 plants divided across four groups: inoculated drought, inoculated overwatered, control drought, and control overwatered, each receiving either 30 mL or 150 mL of water per plant every two days respectively. Data were collected across three dependent variables: a visual stress score (0–3 scale), plant height percent change, and dry mass measured at the conclusion of the four-week growing period.Results were largely inconsistent across measures, while dry mass showed a statistically significant difference between groups (p < 0.01), height percent change and visual scores did not, suggesting that the effect of Priestia megaterium inoculation on stress tolerance in spinach remains inconclusive and warrants further investigation with more controlled trials.

column13: Testing Moringa oleifera as a Potential Treatment in Combatting the Negative Effect of Zinc in Soil on the Mobility of Lumbricus terrestris
column14: Soil contamination with heavy metals such as zinc is a growing environmental concern, causing harm to organisms essential in maintaining healthy ecosystems. Earthworms, including Lumbricus terrestris, play an important role in soil aeration and nutrient cycling, but their movement can be reduced when exposed to toxic substances. This project investigated whether Moringa oleifera, a fast-growing rich plant in antioxidants, could help restore earthworm mobility after zinc exposure. The hypothesis formed was that if Lumbricus terrestris is exposed to Moringa oleifera after being exposed to zinc oxide, then the earthworms would exhibit an increased mobility (higher speed, distance, and max burrowing depth) as the anti-inflammatory properties in Moringa can potentially help restore earthworm movement. Earthworms were placed in soil containing zinc and then treated with three different concentrations of moringa (0.1%, 0.5%, and 1%), along with a zinc-only control group. Mobility was measured by recording the distance traveled, speed, and maximum burrowing depth over a one-hour period using a thin container and tracing movement patterns. ANOVA statistical tests showed strong evidence that moringa treatment increased both distance traveled and speed compared to the control group. However, there was little to no difference in maximum burrowing depth between groups. These results suggest that moringa may help reduce some negative effects of zinc on earthworm movement. This is important because improving earthworm mobility can support soil health and overall ecosystem stability. This project ultimately highlights the potential of a low-cost, plant-based solution in addressing the impact of soil pollution.

column13: Examining the effects of food dyes on chromosomal aberrations and survival rate in Lymnaea stagnalis
column14: Synthetic food dyes are widely used in food and cosmetic products, despite evidence of cytotoxic effects in various organisms. Previous studies have demonstrated that artificial dyes such as Brilliant Blue FCF (Blue 1), Indigo Carmine (Blue 2), and Sunset Yellow FCF (Yellow 6) can cause chromosomal aberrations, developmental delays, and cellular damage in multiple species. This study investigates which of these three synthetic dyes causes the greatest developmental impairments in Lymnaea stagnalis (great pond snails), serving as a model for human exposure during development. Three-month-old juvenile snails were exposed to 29 μM concentrations of each dye for 30 days, with concentrations scaled from human acceptable daily intake values. Developmental impairments were assessed through survival rate monitoring and DNA damage analysis using the comet assay on extracted central ring ganglia tissue. Preliminary results suggest that Blue 1 caused the most severe effects, resulting in a 0% survival rate after just four days when compared to Blue 2 and Yellow 6. Blue 2 also had a 0% survival rate, yet it took around 25 days for the entire population to expire. Yellow 6 had a 25% survival rate, which was surprising as much literature named it to be the most harmful. Ongoing investigation aims to conclude imaging of comet assay slides to analyse the DNA damage present.

column13: The Effect of Proteasome Inhibitors on the HSF1 Stress Response of the Sea Anemone Nematostella vectensis when Introduced to Heat Shock
column14: Global warming has caused ocean temperatures to rise and coral bleaching events kill coral ecosystems. This impacts the marine ecosystem’s health and decreases global biodiversity. Sea Anemones are commonly used experimental models for coral and research has focused on ways to analyze the thermal stress response pathways when introduced to heat shock. HSF1 genes control the thermal stress response in organisms by signaling Heat Shock Proteins (HSPs). Proteasome inhibitors, such as MG-132, block pathways that cause protein breakdown. In this study, the sea anemone Nematostella vectensis was exposed to MG-132 and to heat shock to determine the molecular and physical changes in stress response. qPCR analysis was used to quantify the genetic change in HSF1 as the proteasome inhibitor blocked the breakdown of HSPs. In conjunction with qPCR, the visual stress signs of the N. vectensis were recorded to identify an increase in heat tolerance. The results from this test have not been completed, but it is expected that the expression of HSF1 will increase in the presence of MG-132. The significance of this experiment is that by exposing sea anemones to proteasome inhibitors, then the stress response pathway for heat shock will be more controlled and the organism will be less susceptible to the thermal stress. This has potential to translate to coral projects and prevent coral bleaching.

column13: Determining the Impact of Freshwater Acidification on Long-Term Memory Retention in the Great Pond Snail, Lymnaea stagnalis
column14: Acidification is a type of pollution that affects large bodies of water by CO2 dissolving at the surface of water bodies and forming a carbonic acid. This is better understood in oceanic environments, because freshwater environments couple CO2 dissociation with factors like decomposing organic matter, acid rain, and complex biogeochemistries making the process more complicated. Prior research suggests that the acidification of water bodies can have adverse impacts on the neurological wellbeing of aquatic organisms, which can damage ecosystems and impact biodiversity. The great pond snail, Lymnaea stagnalis, was used as an experimental organism due to their established presence in the field as a model for freshwater molluscs in memory experiments and their ease of care. The effects of hydrochloric acid as a form of acidification on memory formation was observed using memory training (operant conditioning aerial respiration) and memory testing. Four levels of acidification were tested, ranging from zero acidification (pH = 7), to high acidification, (pH = 4.9). Our results supported the conclusion: zero acidification allowed the snails to form memories and the low and high levels of acidification inhibited the snail memory formation, the intermediate level of acidification enhanced the snail's memory. Given these results, there may be a threshold in the environment where combined causes of acidification can in turn enhance memory formation and possibly benefit aquatic organisms. However, this experiment also provides evidence that organisms are more frequently harmed by acidification than benefited, and attempting to reach a specific pH to benefit organisms may be redundant.

column13: The Effect of the Common Food Preservatives Citric and Benzoic Acid on the Social Impairment Exhibited by a Lead-Induced Drosophila Model of Autism Spectrum Disorder
column14: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a neurological and developmental disorder that can significantly impact the learning, behavior and social skills of individuals. Diet is thought to have a significant effect on the severity of ASD symptoms displayed by an individual. Food preservative acids (FPAs) are a common ingredient in many foods today and are capable of impacting the gut-brain axis. This experiment serves to determine if the FPAs citric acid and benzoic acid have an effect on the social impairment exhibited by a lead-induced Drosophila melanogaster model of ASD using the Social Space behavioral assay. The Social Space assay involves placing ten flies into a vertical triangular chamber and giving them twenty minutes to settle down. After the twenty minute period the distance between each fly and their nearest neighbor is measured. The farther the distance between each fly and their nearest neighbor, the greater the level of social impairment. Results on the citric acid experimental groups suggest that citric acid is beneficial in alleviating social impairment caused by ASD. Regarding the benzoic experimental groups, the results suggest that benzoic acid plays a part in worsening social impairment resulting from ASD.

column13: Investigating the Effect of Manuka Honey Components Methylglyoxal (MGO) and Methyl Syringate on Bacillus Subtilis Biofilm Inhibition
column14: Chronic wound infections caused by biofilms affect millions of people each year and are often highly resistant to traditional antibiotics. This has led researchers to look for more natural approaches to inhibit and eradicate them. This project aims to determine the combinational effects of two major components of manuka honey, methylglyoxal (MGO) and methyl syringate, against a Bacillus subtilis bacterial biofilm, which is an ideal model for biofilm formation. Honey has been known to have healing properties for thousands of years, and manuka honey especially is known for having a significantly higher MGO content, which correlates to higher overall antimicrobial properties. Methyl syringate is known for its high anti-inflammatory and other unknown properties, which suggests that a combination of the two compounds could yield higher antimicrobial effects. A checkerboard microdilution assay was used in this study, in which decreasing concentrations of MGO and methyl syringate were placed along the rows and columns of a 96-well plate along with bacterial culture. The incubated plate was then stained with a crystal violet dye to determine the biofilm mass of each well, as well as the percent biofilm inhibition of each combination of compound concentrations. Data collection is still ongoing and preliminary results have yet to be analyzed. This project is significant because the determination of a synergistic combination of naturally occurring compounds in manuka honey could serve as a potential treatment for antibiotic resistant biofilms and serve as aid for those suffering from these infections.

column13: Determining the Relationship Between Ingested Microplastic Particles in Artemia salina (Brine Shrimp) to Microplastic Concentration, Microplastic Exposure Duration, and the Point in Lifecycle when Exposed to Microplastics.
column14: Eight million tons of plastic waste are added into our oceans annually; these plastic pollutants break down and form microplastics. Microplastic ingestion causes problems in a multitude of organisms; leading to laceration, inflammation, damage to the digestive tract, and disruption of reproductive behavior. In this experiment, the amount of ingested fluorescent microplastics in Artemia salina (Brine Shrimp) were investigated based off of the independent variables of microplastic concentration (0.002, 0.02, and 0.2 grams / 200 mL), microplastic exposure duration (1, 2, 3, 6, 48, and 96 hours), and the point in lifecycle when the Artemia salina are exposed to microplastics (hatched in microplastic solution versus exposed as nauplii). There were six groups of Artemia salina tested for microplastic ingestion based off of these variables; they were sampled using a glutaraldehyde fixation protocol, a fluorescent stereomicroscope, and ImageJ software. Additionally, a seventh group was not exposed to microplastics and used as a control. Preliminary results suggest that there is a direct relationship between the amount of microplastics ingested and the microplastic relationship as well as a direct relationship between the amount of ingested microplastics and the exposure duration. Additional work is needed to identify if the amount of ingested microplastics changes in relationship to the point in lifecycle when Artemia salina are exposed. Artemia salina are a food source for many organisms, so these findings can later be applied to research on microplastic transfer in the food chain.

column13: Investigating the Effects of Chinese Traditional Medicine (Xiao Yao San) on Generalized Anxiety Disorder in Drosophila melanogaster Through Wall-Following Assay and Gut Permeability
column14: Generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) affects millions of adults worldwide and results in trouble relaxing, difficulty concentrating, and fatigue. Current treatments have side-effects such as withdrawal, dependence, and cognitive issues. The herb blend Xiao Yao San (XYS) has been used to treat anxiety, but there is a knowledge gap on how its effects on gut health relate to how it ameliorates anxiety levels. The gut-brain axis (GBA) is the bidirectional communication system between gut microbiota and the central nervous system, which plays an important role in regulating stress and anxiety levels. This experiment aims to study the therapeutic effects of XYS on GAD by determining effects on the health of the GBA in a stress-induced model of Drosophila melanogaster (fruit fly). The Wall-following assay, which involves placing a fly in an open arena and measuring the amount of time spent inside and outside a set radius; and the SMURF assay, which involves placing the fly into blue fly food and sorting based on whether or not the fly is a SMURF (the fly has blue dye present throughout its body), measures anxiety levels of the flies and the health of their gut, respectively. It is predicted that groups of stressed flies will spend longer times outside the radius and will have more SMURFS. Also, it’s hypothesized that treatment groups will spend more time inside the radius and will have less SMURFS. Data analysis is ongoing; if these hypotheses are supported, it shows that XYS is a potential treatment for GAD.

column13: Determining the Effect of the Antioxidant Geraniol on the Motor Dysfunction and Oxidative Stress in the Drosophila melanogaster Model of Huntington’s Disease
column14: Huntington’s disease (HD) is a neurodegenerative disorder that presents with motor symptoms and increased oxidative stress (OS). HD is caused by expanded CAG repeats in the Huntingtin gene, with higher repeats being associated with motor decline and oxidative damage. This study investigated geraniol, an antioxidant, as a potential treatment for these symptoms of HD in varying CAG repeat length variants. Drosophila melanogaster stocks were crossed to create 96Q and 72Q repeat variants, which were grown under geraniol diets of 0mM or 40mM. The locomotion of the D. melanogaster was measured using the climbing assay. The OS levels in the D. melanogaster groups were quantified by measuring the concentration of Malondialdehyde species in a tissue homogenate of fly heads using the Thiobarbituric Reactive Substances (TBARS) assay. The climbing abilities of D. melanogaster were compared using the ANOVA test (p<0.05), and a statistically significant increase was found in 96Q HD and 72Q HD groups treated with geraniol compared to no treatment. The 72Q HD group showed a higher increase in locomotion when compared to the 96Q HD group after treatment. A Kruskal-Wallis test found no statistically significant decrease in TBARS in the 96Q group and the 72Q group after treatment. Results indicate that geraniol is an effective antioxidant treatment in reducing HD motor symptoms, but more data is required to determine if there is an effect on OS. Future studies are necessary to conduct more trials on a variety of geraniol concentrations and HD strains in more advanced animal models.

column13: Maze Runners: Analyzing efficiency of slime mold Physarum polycephalum as a model for generating evacuation routes in the Academies of Loudoun building
column14: The slime mold Physarum polycephalum has been well-studied for applications of its foraging capabilities in pathfinding. Applied to modern building planning, these abilities can be used to create the most efficient evacuation routes in a building. To address this safety issue, the research project expands on the viability of the slime mold Physarum polycephalum as a model for generating evacuation routes. The pathmaking abilities of slime mold are similar to that of the A* algorithm, which uses a Python program made to yield the most efficient route between any start and end point. The research project outlined involves the use of slime molds in a 3D-printed model of the Academies of Loudoun building as a model for generating routes. Inoculated at a certain start point, the slime mold would have access to oat flakes placed at endpoints so it could forage and generate a path. The path created by slime mold would be compared with an A* algorithm to determine how efficient the slime mold’s path was, depending on the specific start and end points used. Though experimental phases of the project have stalled due to frequent contamination of the slime mold, it was predicted that the slime mold would have a similar path to the A* algorithm. The results from this project, if expanded, could be used to create new and improved algorithms to create safer and more efficient evacuation routes in buildings.

column13: Observing the Attractional Effect of Vibrational Frequencies between 2700-2900 Hz on the Eastern Subterranean Termite for Better Capture and Control
column14: The eastern subterranean termite is a common termite variety that infests millions of buildings per year. This pest feeds on cellulose, a compound found in organic materials such as wood. Termite damage can cost thousands of dollars to repair, and worldwide, over $5 billion annually is spent fixing termite-induced damage to buildings. One non-invasive control method for termites is the bait trap, in which poisoned cellulose bait is planted near termite colonies, poisoning the termites once distributed amongst the colony. However, this method can take weeks or even months to go into effect, since termites forage for food randomly and may not come across bait traps for some time. In this study, researchers plan to expose termites to a range of different vibrational frequencies from 2700-2900hZ in order to determine which frequency results in the most consistent attraction of the eastern subterranean termite. This assessment of a "most attractive" frequency will be done through a series of behavioral tests in which the behavior of termites is observed in response to different vibrational frequencies. After data is collected, a V-test will be used to determine which frequency most consistently results in termite attraction. Once found, this attractive frequency could be played near a bait trap, making it more efficient by stimulating natural attraction of termites to draw them towards the trap. Additional testing is needed to draw significant conclusions from preliminary data.

column13: Combatting Lassa Fever: Investigating Pathogenesis and Constructing a Preliminary Vaccine Candidate
column14: Lassa Fever is a hemorrhagic fever caused by Lassa virus (LASV) with an estimated >100,000 human infections per year in Western Africa. Currently, there are no approved vaccines or therapeutics against LASV. Additionally, the development process for LASV vaccines and treatments is inhibited by the necessity for high containment laboratory space and funding neglect. This research aims to characterize the first full LASV ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complex, predict potential immunogenic epitopes in silico, and validate those peptides in vitro. Here, we constructed the first full RNP complex for LASV, validating plausibility through Ramachandran analysis and molecular dynamics simulations. We also identified several T cell and B cell epitopes using advanced bioinformatic analysis. Several predicted immunogenic peptides were confirmed to be antigenic in vitro. Furthermore, an analysis of the sera/survival data of guinea pigs vaccinated with LASV glycoprotein DNA produced no neutralizing antibody titer, however, were protected from lethal challenge with LASV, suggesting that T-cell immunity and/or non-neutralizing antibody responses are the true drivers of successful LASV vaccination and protection. These peptides are promising candidates for future LASV vaccine construction.

column13: Evaluating the Impact of Chronic Juvenile Caffeine Exposure on Learning and Memory using RT-qPCR analysis of CREB1 and MAO Gene Expression in Lymnaea stagnalis
column14: Caffeine is a psychoactive substance that affects the central nervous system, influencing mood, awareness, and behavior. Its growing prevalence among children and adolescents has raised concerns regarding its potential impacts on brain development. Prior research suggests that adolescent caffeine consumption may impair the development of the cerebral cortex, a region critical to learning and memory. To investigate the effects of chronic juvenile caffeine exposure on neurodevelopment, Lymnaea stagnalis, a pond snail widely used in behavioral and molecular research, was selected as a model organism. RT-qPCR was used to analyze the expression of two target genes, CREB1 and MAO, relative to the housekeeping gene EF1-a in L. stagnalis. CREB1, a cAMP response element binding protein, is critical to long term memory formation, while MAO, a monoamine oxidase, regulates the degradation of key neurotransmitters including dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin. It was hypothesized that higher caffeine concentrations would result in the downregulation of both genes. Four groups of Lymnaea stagnalis were exposed to 0, 100, 250, and 500 microgram per liter concentrations of caffeine ranging from days 15 to 40 of their lifecycle, with dissections were performed at day 20, 25, 30, and 40. Preliminary results from each checkpoint suggest that experimental caffeine concentrations result in the upregulation of CREB1 and MAO, but statistical tests are ongoing. Continued analysis is necessary in order to draw definitive conclusions about the relationship between caffeine concentration and gene expression, with future investigation aimed at examining how these pathways are altered over the course of development.

column13: Snail Detox: Investigating how Green Tea affects the Reproductive Ability and Expression of the CDCH-I Reproductive Gene in Great Pond Snails exposed to either Zinc or Copper
column14: Anthropogenic, or human, activity exacerbates heavy metal pollution, threatening aquatic environments and water quality. Few studies have addressed how the heavy metals zinc and copper impact the fecundity (reproductive ability) and expression of reproductive genes in freshwater organisms. Green tea, which can bind and mitigate heavy metals through its antioxidant properties, has not been tested on freshwater organism reproduction. This experiment aimed to investigate how green tea could alleviate any detrimental reproductive effects caused by heavy metal pollution by immersing Lymnaea stagnalis (great pond snail), a vital member of aquatic ecosystems, in six aquatic tanks containing no exposure, green tea, zinc chloride or copper chloride, or a combination of either zinc or copper with green tea. Over 21-day trials, fecundity was measured through the number of egg clutches and eggs in each clutch laid by the snails in each tank were counted weekly. After the 21-days, the cerebral ganglia (“snail brain”) of each snail was dissected and its RNA extracted. Their relative expression of CDCH-I, a reproductive gene that creates egg-laying proteins, was quantified through reverse transcription-quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) and analyzed using the Delta-Delta Ct method. Findings indicate decreased fecundity in the “copper” tank compared to the “green tea and copper" tank. Conversely, RT-qPCR findings indicate a downregulation of CDCH-I in snails exposed to green tea + zinc and green tea + copper compared to snails exposed to green tea. Recognizing the effect of heavy metals and green tea on freshwater snail reproduction can illustrate both the long-term heavy metal impact on aquatic environments and how green tea may serve as a "detox solution” to pollution.

column13: Investigating the Transgenerational Effects of Methyl 4-hydroxybenzoate exposure on the Water Flea, Daphnia magna
column14: Parabens are common synthetic preservatives and emerging environmental contaminants that end up in lakes, rivers, and other freshwater systems due to their global mass production. Methyl 4-Hydroxybenzoate (methylparaben) is one of the most frequently detected parabens in water, raising concerns about its effects on aquatic ecosystems. While past studies have shown that acute exposure to parabens can induce short-term physiological changes in organisms, less is known about the effects of chronic exposure on future generations. Therefore, this project investigated whether exposing the parent generation (F0) of the water flea Daphnia magna to methylparaben could cause immediate health effects and long-term epigenetic changes in later generations. Daphnia magna were chosen as a model because they are commonly used in ecotoxicology projects and have short reproductive cycles. A preliminary range-finding test was used to determine safe sublethal concentrations of methylparaben. Parent organisms were then exposed to these concentrations, and heart rate was measured as a marker of physiological stress. To examine possible inherited effects, later generations were raised in clean water, and genomic DNA from the F3 generation was analyzed using Nanopore sequencing to measure global DNA methylation levels, a marker for inherited epigenetic changes. Heart rate data suggested a non-linear dose dependent relationship with methylparaben exposure, with all heart rates elevated above typical levels. Ongoing investigation aims to extract DNA from samples and perform DNA sequencing to collect methylation data to compare with controls.

column13: Reef Relief: The Effects of UV, Chemical, and Thermal Stressors on the Ability of the Antioxidant Mannitol to Mitigate Bleaching in the Sea Anemone Exaiptasia pallida
column14: Coral reefs are essential to marine ecosystems, providing habitat and food sources for millions of species. However, rising ocean temperatures have led to widespread coral bleaching, a process in which corals expel their symbiotic algae. This algae, most commonly zooxanthellae, provides food through photosynthesis. Reactive oxygen species accumulate during thermal stress, causing oxidative damage. Previous studies suggest that the antioxidant mannitol may mitigate this damage. However, its effectiveness across different bleaching conditions remains unclear. This project investigates whether mannitol’s protective effects vary when anemones are bleached by elevated temperature, ultraviolet (UV) exposure, or menthol-induced stress. Sea anemones were acclimated, then exposed to one of three bleaching conditions with or without 10 mM mannitol. A control group with no bleaching treatment was also tested. Symbiont density was quantified at the end of each treatment using hemocytometer counts normalized to protein concentration. Anemones treated with mannitol during the elevated‑temperature trial showed significantly higher symbiont densities than untreated anemones, demonstrating a meaningful protective effect. In contrast, mannitol provided no measurable benefit in the UV‑bleaching trial, consistent with findings that UV stress produces different reactive oxygen species. Future analysis of the menthol‑bleaching and control groups will help clarify the conditions under which mannitol is most effective. Understanding when antioxidant treatments are most effective can help refine future strategies for mitigating bleaching in corals. However, further research is needed to explore long-term effects and potential applications in coral conservation efforts.

column13: Effect of Dietary Glucose Concentration on the Transcriptome of a Type 2 Diabetes Model of Drosophila melanogaster
column14: Type 2 diabetes is a metabolic condition and an incapacitating disease that hundreds of millions of people around the globe suffer from. To gain a comprehensive understanding of gene expression changes associated with type 2 diabetes, researchers often employ high-throughput sequencing to profile the transcriptome, or the complete set of RNA expressed in a tissue at a given time. Unfortunately, how varying glucose concentration levels influences gene expression in insulin-resistant organisms remains unclear. Therefore, this study uses Drosophila melanogaster as a model organism for type 2 diabetes to compare the transcriptomes of insulin-resistant D. melanogaster fed low-, high-, and higher-glucose diets (~4%, ~12%, and ~16% weight/volume). To generate these data, RNA was extracted from each group; mRNA was isolated from the total RNA; and mRNA was reverse-transcribed into cDNA. Ongoing investigations will prepare and sequence these samples to identify the diet-dependent changes in gene expression. The data are expected to show significant differences in gene expression between diets as increased glucose is expected to induce a genetic response to lipid accumulation. Overall, this study addresses a gap in current research, which has primarily focused on the phenotypic  outcomes rather than genetic changes.

column13: The Comparative Analysis of Compounds Found in a Lavandula angustifolia Essential Oil on the Anxiety of Caenorhabditis elegans
column14: Anxiety is caused by the dysregulation of neurotransmitters. This overactivity alters brain chemical signals, reducing the effectiveness of serotonin and dopamine. Severe anxiety is treated with anxiolytics such as Ativan or Xanax. Short-term prescribed treatments often come at an expensive cost, with side effects. Aromatherapy is being researched as a potential alternative to synthetic drugs; Studies done on humans have not been reliable due to the possibility of the placebo effect being in effect. The purpose of this experiment was to see if natural compounds could reduce anxiety with fewer side effects. Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) was used as a model organism due to similarities in neurological pathways and the lack of human bias, reducing the risk of the placebo effect. The physical reactions of C. elegans have indicated biological activity of the compounds. The lavender essential oil was broken down by its natural compounds using a gas chromatography mass spectrometry machine (GC-MS). The GC-MS machine identified linalool, linalyl acetate, and alpha-terpineol. Those compounds were used as the three experimental groups, alongside a positive and a negative control group. A water bath set at 30 degrees celsius was used to induce heat stress on the worms. The stressed worms were then chunked into plates containing the compounds. Thrashing motions were recorded in the span of one minute to indicate stress. Data analysis was conducted for trial one and two, giving p-values of <0.01, meaning the compounds had a significant effect in reducing the stress of the worms.

column13: Microplastic Fragmentation by Eisenia fetida and Its Effect on Nitrite Content in Worm Castings
column14:
Microplastic pollution in the environment has become a major issue due to its persistence with a potential impact on the soil ecosystem. Earthworm species, like Eisenia fetida, ingest and fragment microplastics in the soil. This process could have a negative impact on the chemical properties of vermicast (earthworm excrement). When microplastics are fragmented by worms, their surface area increases, enhancing chemical absorption. This research determines whether nitrite concentration increased in vermicast exposed to styrofoam, expanded polystyrene (EPS), microplastics. The experiment involved two independent groups (no EPS microplastics, and with EPS microplastics), each consisting of 25 units. After 12 days, nitrite was extracted from each sample and underwent the Griess reaction, quantifying nitrite levels. The dependent variable was the measured nitrite absorbance (AU) at 540.1 nm using a Vernier VIS Spectrophotometer. Using DataClassroom, a Mann-Whitney U test calculated a p-value less than 0.01, indicating strong confidence of a shift in nitrite concentration in the EPS test group. The mean of the control group was 0.766 AU, while the EPS group mean was 1.25 AU, a 63% increase. The variability of both groups was low, a standard deviation of 0.249 for control and 0.3512 for EPS group. Because nitrite is a sensitive indicator of nitrification, this shift suggests EPS exposure disrupts nutrient cycling in vermicast. This may reduce soil fertility and long-term agricultural health in microplastic contaminated environments.

Chen, Yuling, et al. “Defense Responses in Earthworms (Eisenia Fetida) Exposed to Low-Density Polyethylene Microplastics in Soils.” Ecotoxicology and Environmental Safety, vol. 187, Jan. 2020, p. 109788. ScienceDirect, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecoenv.2019.109788.

column13: Dose-Dependent Effects of Curcumin on Heat Shock Protein Function and Heat Stress Resistance on Caenorhabditis elegans
column14: Neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson’s are characterized by progressive neural damage resulting from protein misfolding and aggregation. Heat shock proteins (HSPs) act as molecular chaperones that maintain protein homeostasis by preventing misfolding and stabilizing protein structure. However, reduced HSP activity causes increased aggregation leading to the amplification of neural impairment. Recent studies suggest that natural compounds contain antioxidant and anti-aggression properties that may reduce protein misfolding and support HSP function. The objective of this study was to evaluate the dose-dependent effects of curcumin on heat-induced protein damage and stress resistance in Caenorhabditis elegans. The worms were pre-treated with varying volumes of curcumin prior to induced heat shock. Experimental groups included untreated controls, heat-stressed controls, and curcumin-treated groups under heat stress. Stress resistance and protein damage were evaluated through survival-based assessments and lipofuscin autofluorescence imaging as a marker of oxidative protein damage through a fluorescence microscope. Data collection is ongoing, but initial findings of control data indicate significant differences between heat-stressed and non-stressed groups, confirming the effects of heat-induced protein aggregation and supporting the investigation of natural compounds as modulators of heat shock protein function. These findings aim to further characterize the role of curcumin and its potential role in contributing to the border development of natural therapies targeting protein misfolding in neurodegenerative diseases.

column13: The Effect of Multispecies Biofilms on Rust Prevention on Mild Steel in a Simulated Maritime Environment
column14:
Rust prevention in maritime environments presents a challenge due to saltwater spray, abrasive sand, and
heavy moisture, which degrades synthetic rust preventatives. Abrasion compromises protectants, releasing
toxins in water. Steel remains indispensable for applications like storm shelters, ship hulls, and underwater
pipes due to its tensile strength and hardness. Previous research indicates that Bacillus subtilis forms biofilms
on steel surfaces. The extracellular polymers and calcite produced by Bacillus subtilis create an organic
protective barrier that provides a scaffold for other microbes. The hypothesis states a multispecies biofilm
implementing Bacillus subtilis would effectively inhibit rust formation on mild steel under simulated maritime
conditions. Experiments involved preparing one-inch by one-inch metal squares, divided into two groups:
Bacillus only, and bare mild steel. Following biofilm development, the squares were placed on a drying rack
suspended above a saltwater solution (1:10 ratio). These were housed in a greenhouse to facilitate repeated
evaporation and condensation, mimicking a humid, corrosive environment. Findings indicate that Bacillus
subtilis formed magnetite on the steel, an oxide known to prevent other rust types. A control group using
Bacillus coagulans had the reaction at a slower rate, with less hematite.

column13: The Effects of Prolotherapy on the Regenerative Process of Lumbriculus variegatus
column14:
Have you ever wondered why more than 100 kids get injured everyday from sports and can’t participate in them due to the injuries not healing fast enough? Lumbriculus variegatus (California blackworm) is a highly regenerative annelid capable of regrowing head or tail segments after injury, making it a model for studying tissue regeneration. Prolotherapy, a technique using irritant injections like hypertonic dextrose to trigger a wound-healing cascade, stimulates cellular proliferation and collagen deposition. Research combining these areas typically examines if these proliferative agents accelerate, inhibit, or modify the natural regeneration process in this organism. The purpose of this research is to test a novel prolotherapy solution consisting of glucose powder, 4% lidocaine, and reverse osmosis water. Each of the 30 blackworms in the treatment group were weighed, dissected, and injected with this treatment, then transferred to individual petri dishes, which contained 12 mL of spring water, and repeated for the control group without the treatment solution. The blackworms were weighed every 2 weeks to see the progressive growth. It is predicted that there will be a positive increase in the regeneration rate on the Lumbriculus variegatus. Data collection is ongoing.

column13: Does Fertilizer Reduce Stress-Related Pigments in Plants Exposed to Air Pollution
column14: Cement dust pollution, aggravated by the rapid increase in construction for infrastructure such as data centers, poses a significant threat to agricultural productivity. Exposure to this particulate matter can reduce photosynthesis rates by up to 73%, leading to crop instability and major crop waste. This study investigates the effectiveness of general-purpose 20-10-20 fertilizer as a mitigation strategy against cement dust-induced stress in Mizuna plants. The experimental design consisted of three groups: a positive control (no dust or fertilizer), a negative control (6.67 g cement dust), and a treatment group (6.67 g dust plus 100 mL fertilizer). Chlorophyll absorption was quantified using an Atleaf SPAD meter, and stress-related anthocyanin levels were analyzed via spectrophotometry at 530 nm. Data collection occurred biweekly over a four-week period. Initial baseline recordings indicated average SPAD values of 33.35 (positive), 34.64 (negative), and 32.66 (treatment) ; these results were recorded right after the treatment started in early February 2025. It is predicted that over a four week period that the negative control will exhibit a decline in chlorophyll absorption and increased anthocyanin pigmentation, while the treatment will sustain photosynthetic activity, or greatly reduced effects. Data collection is ongoing and will end in March 2025. The findings of this study aim to identify a cost-effective method to alleviate environmental stress on crops, potentially reducing agricultural waste caused by construction-related pollution.

column13: Effect of Oxidative Stress on Surface Degradation and Hydroxyapatite Coating Stability of  3D Printed PLA Bone Scaffolds
column14:
Bone scaffolds used in tissue engineering must remain stable under oxidative stress conditions commonly present in aging, inflammation, and injury environments. Reactive oxygen species can alter polymer surfaces, accelerate degradation, and reduce coating stability, potentially compromising implant performance (Li et al., 2022). This study investigated the effect of hydrogen-peroxide-induced oxidative stress on surface degradation and hydroxyapatite (HA) coating retention of 3D-printed polylactic acid (PLA) scaffolds during dry aging. Solid cylindrical PLA scaffolds (10 mm × 5 mm) were fabricated using fused deposition modeling at 100% infill to isolate surface-level changes. Samples were divided into four groups: untreated PLA control, HA-coated PLA, PLA exposed to 0.01% hydrogen peroxide, and HA-coated PLA exposed to 0.01% hydrogen peroxide. HA coatings were applied using a gelatin-based suspension and dried prior to oxidative exposure. All samples were aged under controlled dry conditions at 37 °C for four weeks. Surface morphology was analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to quantify microcrack density, surface roughness indicators, and HA particle retention. Results showed that HA-coated scaffolds exhibited the greatest mineral surface coverage (65–75%), while untreated PLA and peroxide-treated PLA showed minimal mineral presence (0–5%). Hydrogen peroxide exposure significantly increased microcrack density (9–12 cracks/mm²) compared with controls (1–3 cracks/mm²). The combined HA + peroxide group displayed moderate mineral coverage (40–45%) but the highest crack density (13–16 cracks/mm²) and surface roughness (8–9), indicating simultaneous mineral clustering and oxidative degradation. These findings suggest oxidative stress increases structural degradation and partially reduces coating stability.

Rezwan, K., Chen, Q. Z., Blaker, J. J., & Boccaccini, A. R. (2006). Biodegradable and bioactive porous polymer/inorganic composite scaffolds for bone tissue engineering. Biomaterials, 27(18), 3413–3431. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2006.01.039

column13: Does Low Sound Frequency Om Affect Stress Levels in Daphnia magna
column14:
Does the Low Frequency OM Sound Affect Stress Levels in Daphnia magna?

Abhi Gyanwali

Cardiovascular problems and heart diseases caused by stress affects a lot of people. The Om sound is the universal sound in Hindu scriptures including chanting mantras. It has a deep resonating effect, and it also has a calming effect. The goal of this project was to investigate whether stress can be reduced by being exposed to the Om sound.  Daphnia magna is a model organism because it has stress responses similar to that of humans and was used for this study. If the Om treatment is provided to Daphnia then the heart rate will decrease. For all treatments the 16 Daphnia magna were exposed to stress for one minute then heart beat was measured. For each treatment, 2 days apart, each Daphnia magna was placed under the microscope and then exposed to Om for 20, 15 and 10 seconds respectively. Average heart rate decreased 40% for the 20 second treatment; 27% for 15 second treatment and 29% for the 10 second treatment, indicating that the 20 second Om treatment had the greatest effect on reducing stress. Research is ongoing.
