column13: Distribution and Habitat Associations of Asian Jumping Worms (Amynthas agrestis):  A Soil-Sampling Survey
column14: The Asian jumping worm, specifically Amynthas agrestis, is an invasive species of worm that is a major ecological threat to forests. They have become an increasing ecological problem across the eastern United States. Unlike most earth worms being good for the environment, Amynthas agrestis breaks that conception by causing many problems to the ecosystem. They quickly eat leaf litter on the forest floor, changing the structure of soil and stunts plant regeneration causing forest shrinkage. This project aims to investigate the presence of Asian jumping worms, environmental factors and ecological impacts in local forested and high human disturbance areas. 4 samples were collected from 5 locations across Loudoun county. Each sample was chosen based on certain characteristics of the location: Heavy foot traffic, forest, lake, heavy human disturbance and river. These were the independent variables while the amount of egg or cocoons found in each sample were the dependent variables for the experiment. Field sampling methods were used to collect 500 grams of soil for each sample at five different locations across Loudoun county, while environmental variables such as soil temperature, moisture, pH and sunlight were recorded using a probe at each sample site. The samples were sifted using three different sifters, all being a different size of mesh to find possible eggs and cocoons of Asian jumping worms. All possible eggs and cocoons were put into an incubator at 50°F for 2-3 days to verify the egg authenticity and data collection is ongoing.

column13: The Effect of Temperature on the Enzymatic Activity of Saccharomyces cerevisiae During Glucose Fermentation
column14:
The Effect of Temperature on the Enzymatic Activity of Saccharomyces cerevisiae During Glucose Fermentation
Jon Jesuraj

Saccharomyces cerevisiae is a fungus known for having unique enzymatic functions. It is known to undergo fermentation when exposed to heat. Denaturation is known to occur in similar fungi when enough heat is applied. The purpose of this study is to find the point of heat at which enzymatic activity decreases, and denaturation begins to occur. Identifying this point can help industries that utilize yeast optimize outputs and experience greater yield. In this experiment, yeast was combined with a glucose starch solution and exposed to five different temperature conditions (15°C, 25°C, 35°C, 45°C, and 55°C). Carbon dioxide production, measured through balloon expansion, served as an indicator of enzymatic activity during fermentation. Results showed that CO₂ production increased steadily up to approximately 35°C, indicating optimal enzyme function, and then declined sharply at higher temperatures, indicating denaturation and loss of metabolic efficiency. These findings support the established principles of enzyme denaturation and demonstrate how temperature directly influences fermentation rate and yeast viability.

    Saccharomyces cerevisiae. Microbiology and Molecular Biology Reviews.

column13: Transformation of Group B Blood to Group O Blood Through the Use of Plasma Transfers and Enzymatic Processes
column14:
Transformation of Group B Blood to Group O Through the Use of Plasma Transfers and Enzymatic Processes

Sophie Leftwich 
 
Blood transfusions save around 118.5 million lives a year worldwide. The availability of O blood impacts this statistic. The world population blood supply is split up into four groups; A, B, AB, and O. 35.2%, 16%, 1% (rarest), and 7% respectively. For blood transfusions, O negative is the most useful due to its lack of an antigen layer. An antigen layer is unique to each blood group as they detect foreign substances in the blood stream. O negative is used in emergencies due to limited time when a patient is in critical condition. Since, O negative is the universal donor and is compatible with all other blood types, no prior test is needed. Unfortunately there is a shortage of O blood and donors. The purpose of this research was to investigate a procedure that can potentially transform any blood type into O negative. B plasma sheep’s blood was centrifuged and B negative blood was added to it so the final mix contained both A and B plasma. No separation in the vials was noted indicating that this part of the procedure was successful. Next the antigen layer was removed, using a sugar digesting enzyme (alpha galactosidase). The blood and enzymes incubated for an hour at 20C. Data indicated that 19 of the 20 samples were O negative, indicating 95% successful transformation. This experiment may help pave the way for ongoing research to be continued so that O negative blood becomes readily available around the world.

column13: The Comparative Effects of Ethyl Pyruvate on Drosophila melanogaster CO2 Levels
column14: Utilizing their olfactory system, Drosophila melanogaster (fruit flies) are responsible for destroying 30% of crops across the country each year. This occurs when the females lay eggs on the skin of ripe and decaying fruit, allowing larvae to feed on the produce, leading to crop deterioration and spread of pathogens. With their antenna olfactory system, fruit flies detect CO2 to determine where food, mates, breeding grounds, and potential threats are. Ethyl pyruvate, commonly utilized for its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, was tested on mosquitoes that possess similar olfactory systems to those of fruit flies. When treated with ethyl pyruvate, mosquitoes lost their sense of smell to find food. The purpose of this experiment was to test the effects of ethyl pyruvate on the olfactory system of a fruit fly. Fruit flies were divided by sex into sixty vials with ten flies in each. In a y-maze, a piece of Musa acuminata (banana) was placed in each arm. In the top left arm an untreated piece of banana was placed and in the right arm a piece of banana was treated with 1% ethyl pyruvate. The flies were monitored on which arm they entered into and documented in a spreadsheet. The p-value was found by using a Kruskal-Wallis test. The value was less than 0.01, proving that the ethyl pyruvate worked and was not based on chance.

column13: Utilizing Acoustic Vibrations To Non-Invasively Analyze Structural Deformation In Buildings
column14:
Hollow metal pipes are used in countless scenarios, such as the foundation of structures for support through columns and beams. In more pressing situations, such as natural disasters, buildings are put at severe risk. Earthquakes are a pertinent example, with 75% of deaths following earthquakes resulting from structural collapses, a lot of which are due to misidentifying which structures are internally critical.

This research project uses an array of metal pipes that deform to various deformation intensities. Each deformation level will have 100 samples tested with an accelerometer and a mass dropping apparatus to generate vibration readings. These varied readings will be used to create a machine learning model that can identify the deformation level of a pipe given the vibration graph.

So far, this study contains roughly 150 vibration images across the 0% to 30% deformity range. Even in the small change, it is apparent that there are changes in 3 key components: Z-Axis position, Z-Axis Amplitude, and X-Axis amplitude. These changes are expected to become more apparent as higher levels of deformity are approached. Once the full dataset of 1000 images is complete, it will be split 70,20,10 train, validation, and test to optimize predictions. It will then be learned by a 2D CNN, which will make predictions on the level of deformity based on the prior 3 markers mentioned. Future goals include filling out the rest of the dataset to make accurate predictions and tuning the model for higher levels of accuracy once tested.

column13: Utilizing a Peltier Module in a Portable Refrigeration Unit for Off Grid Vaccine Transport and Last Mile Cold Chain Sustainability
column14: Approximately 20% of African children still lack access to routine immunizations, which contributes to highly preventable diseases and increased child mortality rates (Endalamew, 2025). On top of that, currently, only 5% of total people in Africa have received the Malaria vaccine. Administration of the vaccine is hindered by limited electricity access and isolation, which reduces coverage potential and creates a risk of vaccine spoilage. Developing and testing a compact, low-energy thermoelectric refrigerator system intended for last-mile vaccine storage and transport, can ensure that vaccines remain within recommended range to prevent spoilage.The built system uses a Peltier module integrated into a cooler box to maintain stable storage temperatures. Unlike conventional vaccine refrigerators, which rely on compressors or chemical-based systems, this design prioritizes affordability and simplicity by using thermoelectric cooling. Internal temperature data was collected using sensors under varying conditions representing use in a real-world environment. Preliminary results showcased that the product can maintain an internal temperature within the 2-5° recommended range for the vaccine over a 24 hour period. This indicates a strong cooling effect and suggests that design improvements will allow it to meet the World Health Organization’s vaccine storage requirements. Reducing cost through specialization of parts and mass production will make the product more effective per unit cost spent by humane organizations.

column13: Extraction and Comparison of Anthocyanin Rich Plant Dyes as Cleaner Solar Cell Photosynthesizers
column14:
Over sixty percent of American energy is currently sourced from fossil fuels with approximately nine percent sourced from renewable energy (Transformative Power Systems, Energy.gov). Solar panels have evolved through three generations, from the silicon-based cell, toxic in production due to carcinogens released in silicon processing, to the new third generation Dye Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC), which mainly uses chemical dyes containing rare earth metals as photosynthesizers. Dye from crops containing anthocyanins may be harvested to replace chemical dyes. Further research is required to prove what plants perform best as photosynthesizers.
After creating the chemical components (TiO2 paste and Lugol’s iodine), plants were heated in absolute ethanol to extract their dye. Two plates of ITO glass were annealed, the cathode coated in graphite powder, the anode in TiO2 paste, then sandwiched together with a dye and Lugol’s iodine suspended between the plates through capillary action.
 The cells were charged under a halogen lamp and tested for voltage, amperage, and resistance. The plates must be annealed in order to produce a resistance reading and single cell amperage is read in micro-amps. Eggplant dye has produced the highest current at 60µA. Having a baseline procedure and understanding of what plants are viable as photosynthesizers is crucial to advancing DSSC performance for future applications. 
Utilizing plant dyes are a cleaner, cheaper alternative than chemical dyes. Moving forward, multiple cells will be connected in a parallel circuit to maximize energy output, working towards powering small appliances such as heating platforms, LEDs, or small fans.

column14:
Researchers say that the earth has already or will soon reach a point of “no return” for air pollution, meaning irreparable damage especially in cities (Pearce, 2026). Along with that risk, people in cities are constantly being put at health risks from sound pollution due to the busyness that happens right outside their doors. One solution that is being researched to mitigate this is the replication of owl feather structure as they are known for their silent flight. Additionally, when it comes to addressing air pollution, a very strong candidate for this is microalgae which can absorb carbon dioxide. 
Methodology consisted of four phases: finding efficient algae conditions, creating owl feathers, creating a surrounding base for the prototype, and testing both air and sound pollution. Data from algae was collected to find the best growth conditions, demonstrating the positive effects of using a photobioreactor with more medium. Alongside this process, images and sketches are being created on Desmos to import into Inventor by converting them into Excel points to accurately 3D print owl feathers with polylactic acid - carbon fiber (PLA-CF) filament. 
After these two phases are completed, the next steps involve creating external components. The bottom base is made of concrete and the top segment is made of plastic. A decibel meter will be used to test for sound pollution and an air quality sensor will be used for air pollution. Further extension of this project could include testing out different structure designs and a different microalgae, such as Schedesmus sp. and Spirulina sp.

column13: Evaluating the Antibacterial Efficacy of Streptomyces Extracts Against Antibiotic-Resistant Bacteria
column14:
Antibiotic resistance is a growing global health concern, with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) posing a significant threat due to limited treatment options and increasing clinical prevalence. Many effective antibiotics originate from natural sources, particularly fungi, yet a large proportion of fungal metabolites remain unexplored. The long-term goal of this research is to contribute to early-stage antibiotic discovery by evaluating the antibacterial potential of fungal-derived compounds. This phase of the study focused on validating an experimental framework necessary to achieve that goal.
A standardized disk diffusion assay was used to establish reliable control data in a school laboratory setting. Mueller-Hinton agar plates were inoculated with Escherichia coli as a safe surrogate organism. Sterile paper disks containing an antibiotic control and a solvent control were placed on the bacterial lawn, and plates were incubated at 35–37°C for 18–24 hours. Zones of inhibition were measured to assess antibacterial response.
The antibiotic control produced consistent zones of inhibition across three trials, with an average diameter of 22 mm, while the solvent control showed no inhibition. Minimal variation between trials confirmed the 
reproducibility and reliability of the method.
By validating the disk diffusion procedure under controlled conditions, this testing moved the project closer to its goal by establishing a dependable screening system. This ensures that any future inhibition observed from fungal or Streptomyces extracts can be confidently attributed to biological activity rather than experimental error. The validated framework now supports progression to extract screening and, ultimately, research relevant to antibiotic-resistant pathogens such as MRSA in appropriately equipped laboratories.

column13: Reducing Biofilms on Surgical Mesh Through Controlled Electrochemical Current Treatment as an Alternative Approach to Combating Antimicrobial Resistance
column14:
Hospital waste systems, particularly contaminated surgical mesh and single-use plastics, pose a significant public health risk. About 15% of hospital waste is hazardous and often contains infectious microbes (World Health Organization, 2018). Biofilms complicate remediation because their extracellular polymeric substance (EPS) matrix increases tolerance to conventional sterilization methods. While initial trials confirmed voltage-dependent reductions in planktonic bacterial cultures, the primary focus of this study was the application of pulsed electrical fields to established biofilms on surgical mesh within a sealed electrochemical chamber.

In Phase II, a custom acrylic chamber with graphite electrode plates and 3D-printed mesh holders was engineered to simulate controlled treatment conditions. Phase III evaluated biofilm-forming polymicrobial communities of Escherichia coli K-12, Pseudomonas fluorescens, and Staphylococcus epidermidis cultured directly on polypropylene surgical mesh. Mesh samples were exposed to controlled pulsed electrical treatments (20 kV or 25 kV, 180 seconds) using a DC power supply, while untreated samples served as controls. Post-treatment viability was quantified through colony-forming unit (CFU) analysis and microscopic imaging.

Results demonstrated significant reductions in bacterial viability following high-voltage pulsed treatment, with polymicrobial biofilms exhibiting greater resistance than single-species systems. The sealed chamber design improved experimental reproducibility and containment, highlighting its feasibility for safe decontamination research.

Pulsed electrochemical treatment demonstrates strong potential as a scalable, non-antibiotic strategy for reducing biofilm-associated pathogens on contaminated surgical materials prior to disposal, with future research focused on optimizing electrical parameters and evaluating integration into hospital wastewater and medical waste management systems. 

Kim, Y. W., Subramanian, S., Gerasopoulos, K., Ben-Yoav, H., Wu, H., Quan, D., Carter, K., Meyer, M. T., Bentley, W. E., & Ghodssi, R. (2015). Effect of electrical energy on the efficacy of biofilm treatment using the bioelectric effect. NPJ Biofilms and Microbiomes, 1(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/npjbiofilms.2015.16

column13: Developing a Method of Upcycling Waste Polylactic Acid Into Thermoplastic Adhesives With Citric Acid, Polyethylene Glycol, and Monobutyltin oxide
column14:
According to the National Geographic Society, there is an estimated 91% of plastic globally not recycled. Plastic waste poses significant dangers to the environment and human health. A large portion of plastic waste, around 79% to 81%, ends up in landfills or is incinerated. It pollutes land and water, harms wildlife, and contributes to climate change. 

To combat this issue, upcycles waste plastics like PLA can be turned into thermoplastic adhesives using citric acid (CA), polyethylene glycol (PEG), and monobutyltin oxide as catalyst. The main research focus would be on demonstrating that waste plastics can be turned into functioning adhesive materials with the use of environmentally friendly materials. The process utilizes reactive melt blending to break the PLA down into fragments. Heating the PEG and MBTO breaks the ester bonds within PLA. The citric acid is then added to crosslink the chains as an esterification process. The resulting adhesive already demonstrates adhesive properties at room temperature. 

The adhesive will proceed to be analyzed by a lap-shear test and a SAFT test all done in accordance with ASTM D 1875 and ASTM D-4498 standards to be compared with other adhesives like White Glue, Super Glue, and Hot Glue. The current adhesive has high viscosity, which causes issues in the testing phase, thus this project is still on-going. The future steps for this project will be to optimize the recipe and procedure of making the adhesive and test its properties and strength to compare it with other adhesives on the market.

column13: Assessing the Impact of the Dual-inhibition of DDR1 and FYN on Cell Viability in Human Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia
column14: Chronic myelogenous leukemia(CML) is a type of blood cancer driven by dysregulated tyrosine kinase signaling that allows it to often develop resistance to standard therapies. Its compensatory nature creates a need for therapeutic strategies that address its various pathways. This study investigated whether the dual inhibition of Discoidin Domain Receptor 1(DDR1) and FYN reduces CML viability more effectively than their monotherapies. K562 CML cells were treated with DDR1-IN-1(1μM and 10μM), a small molecule inhibitor  and FYN-targeting siRNA(10 nM and 40 nM) in monotherapy and in combination. Cell viability was measured using a Celltiter-Glo luminescence assay which measures ATP, a measure of metabolically alive cells. Each experimental condition was tested in 10 replicates across three trials. DDR1 inhibition produced the greatest reduction in viability with 1 μM DDR1-I-1 resulting in the lowest mean luminescence(~113,589 RLU). In contrast, siRNA only treatment increased luminescence relative to the negative control(~231,703 vs ~157,254 RLU) suggesting a potential compensatory cellular response. Combination treatments reduced viability compared to siRNA alone but did not consistently outperform DDR1 only treatments. A two way ANOVA demonstrated a significant treatment effect(= 0 0.019) and a strong inter-trial variability(p < 0.0001). These results indicate that the DDR1 kinase is a primary driver of CML cell viability while FYN inhibition may result in a more complex, hormetic response. This study demonstrates the therapeutic promises of inhibiting singular kinases such as DDR1, but highlights the need for further optimizing monotherapies through dual inhibition.

column13: Combined effect of serotonin and dopamine receptor-targeting drugs on hyperactivity in Drosophila melanogaster model of ADHD
column14: ADHD is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. Approximately 5% of children and 2.5% of adults have ADHD (American Psychiatric Association, 2013) and show deficits in working memory and response inhibition compared to neurotypical peers. In ADHD, dopaminergic signaling of frontostriatal, mesolimbic, and mesocortical circuitry is dysregulated in both baseline (tonic) and superimposed transient (phasic) patterns (Véronneau-Veilleux et al., 2022). Along with dopamine, an overall deficit in serotonin has been observed in ADHD (Jackson et al., 2025), strongly suggesting serotonergic involvement within ADHD. However, serotonin has been underappreciated in ADHD research. We investigated the individual and combined effects of dopaminergic signaling and serotonin biosynthesis on hyperactivity in Drosophila melanogaster (D. melanogaster; fruit flies) model of ADHD.

column13: The Effect of Cinnamaldehyde on FoxO Expression and Muscle Wasting in a Drosophila melanogaster Model of Muscle Atrophy
column14: Muscle atrophy is a condition categorized by the loss of muscle mass and strength and is a common symptom in diseases like cancer and kidney disease. Muscle atrophy occurs when protein degradation exceeds protein synthesis in the PI3K/Akt pathway, which is the main regulator of skeletal muscle mass. In a body experiencing muscle atrophy, low insulin is produced and binds to the surface of the cell membrane, leading to the improper phosphorylation of the pathway. This results in the overexpression of FoxO proteins in the nucleus, where they bind to ligases that mark healthy muscle proteins for protein degradation. This research focuses on using cinnamaldehyde as a potential treatment, due to its insulin-mimicking abilities through increasing glucose uptake, on the mobility in a Drosophila melanogaster model of muscle atrophy. The flies were supplemented with cinnamaldehyde and their mobility was assessed by a climbing assay. In this assay, the number of flies that crossed a 15 cm mark after a 10 second testing period was recorded, and flies that passed the mark were considered successful. After data was collected, a Kruskal-Wallis test was performed and the p-value was <0.01, indicating that the groups are different. A Post-hoc Dunn’s test was performed; when comparing the crossed flies on standard food against the crossed flies on drug food, the results were different. In conclusion, the data supports that the hypothesis was partially supported indicating that cinnamaldehyde had a positive impact on the mobility in flies that exhibited muscle atrophy.

column13: The Effect of Curcumin on Filamentous actin Levels and Memory on Aging Drosophila melanogaster
column14: 58 million people suffer from neurodegenerative disease and two in five adults experience age-associated memory impairments. Recent studies attribute memory loss to a natural age-associated increase in F-actin levels in the brains of Drosophila melanogaster. As flies age, the protein structures become more rigid and form aggregates that disrupt autophagic activity and weaken synapses, leading to memory loss with significant implications for neurodegenerative disease such as Alzheimer's and dementia. Curcumin, the yellow pigment found in turmeric spice, regulates protein signalling pathways by modifying structures involved in actin polymerization, strengthens synapses, and improves memory in vitro and mouse models. Through an aversive phototaxic suppression assay to measure memory retention and fluorescent microscopy to quantify F-actin concentrations in the brain, this study aims to establish whether curcumin is a potential preventative treatment to reduce age-associated memory loss by reducing F-actin levels. This study offers insight into an unexplored area of research concerning the correlation between curcumin and F-actin levels, the extent to which protein levels may disrupt memory in humans, and its potential applications in future treatments. Currently, results reveal a significant improvement in memory with curcumin supplementation in the flies. However, data collection is ongoing to determine whether curcumin resulted in a significant reduction in F-actin levels. Therefore, the question of whether curcumin improved memory by reducing F-actin levels cannot yet be answered.

column13: The Effect of an Ultrasound-Activated Electrospun Piezoelectric Hydrogel Scaffold on Post-Traumatic Brain Injury Motor Function in Drosophila melanogaster
column14: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) is a leading cause of long-term neurological disability, affecting 50-60 million people annually. Cascading secondary issues, including oxidative stress, impair motor and cognitive function. Most current TBI therapies are too slow-acting or only manage symptoms rather than providing restorative effects. While ultrasound-activated piezoelectric barium titanate nanoparticles (BTNPs) can accelerate cellular regeneration, BTNPs are prone to cytotoxic agglomeration and lack the environment of biocompatible hydrogels that can reduce oxidative stress. This study investigates a scaffold created by embedding BTNPs within an electrospun sodium alginate (SA) and polyethylene oxide (PEO) hydrogel. TBI was induced in Drosophila melanogaster with a high-impact trauma (HIT) apparatus. Treatments were applied with a procedure to cut open fly cuticles followed by ultrasound stimulation. Motor recovery was quantified with a climbing assay across four groups (N=10 flies per group). Assay validity was supported by a statistically significant difference in climbing assay scores between flies without TBI and those with it (p=0.0035). While the large variance resulted in no statistical significance among the TBI treatment groups (p>0.05), the median climbing assay pass rate in flies with TBI and the scaffold (50%) was greater than the medians in flies given TBI and just BTNPs (45%) and flies with TBI (35%). The trend found suggests that piezoelectric electrospun hydrogel scaffolds may be a pathway for safer and more biocompatible restoration after traumatic brain injury, but data collection is ongoing and the long term effects of the treatment are still unexplored.

column13: The Effects of Lavender Oil on Hyperactivity, Inattention, and Sleep Quality in Drosophila melanogaster models of ADHD
column14: Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder affecting millions of people worldwide. Aside from hyperactivity and inattention, one common symptom of ADHD is poor sleep quality. Not only is poor sleep associated with ADHD, but it also seems to exacerbate ADHD symptoms. Lavender essential oil has improved sleep quality in humans. Drosophila and human neuronal networks are similar, making Drosophila a common model for human behavior and diseases. To model ADHD, radish1 mutant flies will be used as they exhibit hyperactivity and inattention, in addition to wild type flies fed lead, as they too will be more hyperactive when given low doses of lead. Each of the six fly groups will go through the Drosophila Activity Monitor (DAM) or Optomotor Maze, which measure activity and inattention, respectively. The DAM counts the number of times an individual fly breaks a laser beam and the optomotor maze measures flies’ behavior in response to a distraction pattern. Flies will be put in the DAM or undergo the maze 3 days post-eclosion. Although the difference is not statistically significant, the radish1 flies have a lower median number beam breaks than wild-type flies in the DAM, indicating that the model is not appropriate. This could be due to limited sample sizes, however, more testing must be done on an appropriate model.

column13: The Effect of Zingiber Officinale on Oxidative Damage through Malondialdehyde Expression and Aggression in Drosophila melanogaster​
column14: Contributing to 80–90% of human disease, oxidative stress (OS) occurs when reactive oxygen species overwhelm antioxidant defenses, driving lipid peroxidation and producing malondialdehyde (MDA), a biomarker of oxidative damage. Current treatments often show limited efficacy, require monitoring, and carry serious risks, emphasizing the need for safer interventions. Since elevated oxidative damage is associated with maladaptive aggression, this study tested whether ginger essential oil (GEO) from Zingiber officinale reduces both MDA expression and aggressive behavior under different housing conditions. Male Drosophila melanogaster (~5 days old) were maintained as group-housed or socially isolated cohorts and fed vehicle, low, medium, or high GEO diets. Oxidative damage was quantified through a TBARS colorimetric assay at OD535, using an MDA standard curve (R² = 0.992) across 10 biological replicates/group. Aggression was measured through 10-minute paired male assays, with lunging, boxing, and chasing events recorded across the same eight treatment groups. Isolation increased baseline oxidative stress and aggression, with isolated controls showing higher mean MDA and aggressive-event frequency than group-housed controls. GEO reduced MDA in both housing conditions, with the strongest reduction at the medium dose. Mean MDA fell from 17.9 to 12.2 µM in group-housed flies and from 27.4 to 20.0 µM in isolated flies, corresponding to 32% and 27% reductions. Aggression data also showed strong significance across groups (p<0.01), matching TBARS trends across all 128 pairwise comparisons. Overall, GEO showed significant biochemical and behavioral effects, supporting its promise as a safer in vivo candidate for oxidative-stress-linked aggression.

column13: The Effect of Demethoxycurcumin Supplementation on Motor Function and Memory in Drosophila melanogaster Overexpressing ALS-Linked TDP-43 Q331K Mutation
column14: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a rapidly progressing neurodegenerative disease characterized by oxidative stress and mislocation in the TDP-43 protein, leading to increased vulnerability in both motor and dopaminergic neurons. This study investigated whether demethoxycurcumin (DMC), a curcumin derivative with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, can improve motor function and short-term memory in Drosophila melanogaster overexpressing the ALS-linked mutant form of TDP-43 (Q331K) in which the substitution of glutamine to lysine at position 331 leads to protein misfolding. Using the UAS-GAL4 system, flies were generated to target expression of the mutation in motor and dopaminergic neurons and were divided into control and DMC-supplemented groups. Motor function was assessed using a negative geotaxis (climbing) assay, while short-term memory was measured using an aversive phototaxis suppression (APS) assay. Results showed that DMC-treated ALS flies demonstrated improved motor performance in comparison to untreated ALS flies, suggesting a partial restoration of motor function. However, APS assay results indicated that DMC treatment did not significantly improve short-term memory. Statistical analysis using Kruskal-Wallis and post hoc Dunn’s tests confirmed that DMC had a significant effect on motor function but not on memory recovery.These findings suggest that DMC may alleviate motor deficits associated with mutant TDP-43, but is less effective in restoring memory function. Future work should investigate the specific mechanisms of DMC’s effects and determine optimal dosing concentrations.

column13: Revealing how dust grain properties and temperature distributions in high redshift galaxies correlate with star formation activity using Machine Learning
column14: Accurately measuring star formation rates (SFRs) in high-redshift galaxies (z > 5) remains a major challenge because interstellar dust absorbs ultraviolet light from young stars and re-emits it in the infrared, skewing spectral energy distribution observations. Traditional dust attenuation laws, such as the Calzetti and Small Magellanic Cloud laws, are derived from nearby galaxies and have been shown to fail at high redshift, where dust composition, temperature, and geometry evolve rapidly. As a result, SFR estimates in the early universe carry significant systematic uncertainties. This study addresses that gap by training three supervised machine learning models on publicly available JWST CEERS DR1 photometric data (Cox et al. 2025) to predict SFR without assuming a fixed attenuation law. Features were extracted from 87,345 galaxies and filtered to a high-redshift subset (z > 5), including UV continuum slope beta computed from NIRCam F115W and F150W fluxes, stellar mass, E(B-V) dust attenuation from LePHARE SED fitting, an infrared excess proxy derived via the Meurer et al. (1999) relation, and pairwise interaction terms. Three models were trained and compared: Linear Regression as a fixed-law baseline, Random Forest (400 trees), and Gradient Boosting (300 estimators). Performance was evaluated using MAE, RMSE, and R-squared on an 80/20 train-test split with 5-fold cross-validation. Gradient Boosting achieved the strongest performance (R-squared = 0.926, RMSE = 0.319), outperforming the linear baseline (R-squared = 0.900), demonstrating that dust-SFR relationships at high redshift are non-linear and benefit from flexible, data-driven modeling approaches.

column13: Computational Design of Protein Inhibitors Targeting Aβ42 Aggregation in Alzheimer's Disease Using Molecular Docking
column14: Alzheimer's disease pathology is heavily driven by the aggregation of amyloid-beta 42 (AB42) proteins into neurotoxic plaques. The KLVFFA and IATVIV motifs serve as critical structural hotspots for this self-assembly process. While current treatments include antibody therapies, they face significant delivery hurdles. This study presents a computational approach to design de novo peptide inhibitors intended to physically cap these motifs and arrest AB42 fibril growth. A bioinformatic pipeline was developed, utilizing AlphaFold2 to predict the 3D structures of motif-targeted sequences. These inhibitors were docked to the AB42 fibril and subjected to a 100-picosecond molecular dynamics simulation using OpenMM in a solvated physiological environment. Candidate efficacy was evaluated using a composite scoring matrix that balanced root mean square deviation (RMSD) for structural stability and intermolecular hydrogen bond counts for binding specificity. The lead candidate, targeting the KLVFFA core, achieved the highest overall score, maintaining a stable RMSD plateau of 0.22 nm and averaging over four persistent hydrogen bonds. This indicated successful, specific binding compared to a structurally simple negative control and a literature-derived sequence that experienced simulated steric failure. Although the performance difference compared to the negative baseline did not reach strict statistical significance, the lead design's targeted hydrophobic interactions demonstrated functional capping capabilities. This computational framework successfully identifies thermodynamically stable peptide candidates, establishing a strong foundation for future in vitro validation and the development of alternative therapeutics.

column13: Machine Learning Driven Inverse Design of Photonic Metamaterials
column14: Designing photonic metamaterials with targeted optical properties is challenging due to the complex, non-linear relationship between geometry and electromagnetic response, making traditional trial-and-error simulations inefficient. This study presents a machine learning–driven inverse design framework that accelerates the discovery of metamaterial geometries with desired energy loss characteristics. A parametric dataset of unit-cell geometries was generated and evaluated using finite element method (FEM) simulations across multiple frequencies. A variational autoencoder (VAE) encoded geometries into a low-dimensional latent space, while a neural network surrogate model predicted spectral responses from latent variables. Inverse design was performed through gradient-based optimization in latent space, followed by decoding into physical geometries. The models achieved strong performance (VAE R² ≈ 0.91, surrogate R² ≈ 0.90), and FEM verification showed high agreement with predictions (R² ≈ 0.998). Overall, this approach significantly reduces computational cost while maintaining accuracy, demonstrating the potential of machine learning to enable rapid and scalable metamaterial design.

column13: EpiSight: A Graph Convolutional Network for Epileptogenic Zone Localization in Drug-Resistant Epilepsy Using Phase-Amplitude Coupling
column14: Resective surgery is the primary treatment for 15 million people worldwide with drug-resistant epilepsy, yet it suffers from a 30% failure rate due to inaccurate localization of the epileptogenic zone (EZ). This study presents EpiSight, a novel computational tool using a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) to automate 3D EZ localization. To do this, the model extracts Phase-Amplitude Coupling (PAC), an underutilized biomarker representing the relationship between low-frequency phase and high-frequency amplitude, from intracranial EEG (iEEG) recordings from the Children’s Hospital of Michigan. Brain regions of interest were modeled as nodes in patient-specific k-nearest neighbor (kNN) graphs (k=6), where each node was initialized with PAC summary statistics. A custom GCN architecture was then used to train the model and update brain region features. On a separate test set at the UCLA Mattel Children’s Hospital, EpiSight achieves a state-of-the-art AUC of 88.6%, an accuracy of 82.2%, and a Hit@1 of 99%, indicating that the top-predicted region was the correct EZ in nearly all test cases. This performance significantly outperforms current fMRI-based research benchmarks by over 20%. Furthermore, this project engineered the first end-to-end clinical pipeline capable of translating raw iEEG data into interactive 3D brain maps of likely epileptogenic regions in under three minutes, overcoming the technical hurdles that traditionally delay surgical planning. By providing surgeons with a high-precision and efficient diagnostic tool, EpiSight offers an accurate engineering solution to reduce surgical failures and significantly improve long-term patient outcomes for those suffering from drug-resistant epilepsy.

column13: siRNA Mediated Knockdown of FUT7 to Attenuate Sialyl Lewis X Driven Triple-Negative Breast Cancer Metastasis
column14:
Triple-negative breast cancer is associated with a disproportionately high mortality rate, potentially because current therapeutic strategies primarily target tumor proliferation. Despite this, non-proliferating cancerous cells are able to disseminate by exploiting the transendothelial migration (TEM) cascade, a series of molecular interactions between endothelial and cancer cells. The first interaction in the cascade involves binding between E-selectin, expressed on endothelial cells, and sialyl-Lewis X (sLeX), a glycan frequently overexpressed on cancer cells.

The current study investigated whether downregulating sLeX through inhibition of its key biosynthetic enzyme, FUT7, could reduce invasive behavior in a murine TNBC model. 4T1 cells were seeded and transfected with 20 nM FUT7 siRNA. A scratch assay was performed to assess local invasion, and an in-cell western blot was used to quantify the functional sLeX expression. An ANOVA test demonstrated that FUT7-silenced cells exhibited significantly reduced local invasion compared to the control groups. These findings support the role of sLeX-mediated adhesion in promoting metastatic behavior and suggest that FUT7 inhibition may impair early steps of the TEM cascade. However, contrary to the hypothesis, the FUT7 inhibition led to an increase in functional sLeX and e-selectin binding, suggesting the activation of other compensatory fucosyltransferase pathways. To explore potential therapeutic strategies targeting this pathway, FUT7-targeting PROTAC molecules were designed and evaluated using molecular docking simulations, which demonstrated favorable binding within the FUT7 catalytic pocket. These results provide preliminary computational support for the development of FUT7-targeted inhibitors aimed at limiting metastatic adhesion.

column13: The Effect of Ascorbyl Palmitate on PI3K-AKT Pathway Activity in Burkitt's Lymphoma Cells
column14: Burkitt's Lymphoma (BL) is a highly aggressive B-cell lymphoma with an average survival rate of 2.8 months upon recurrence. Current intensive chemotherapy treatments carry severe side effects, therefore requiring novel therapeutic strategies. Ascorbyl Palmitate(AP), a lipophilic vitamin C derivative, has demonstrated the ability to reduce cell viability in cancer cells by increasing reactive oxygen species(ROS), however its effect on BL has not been studied. This study investigated whether AP inhibits the PI3K-AKT signaling pathway in Raji cells (human Burkitt's lymphoma B lymphocytes) in a dose dependent manner. Raji cells were treated with 0µM, 50µM, 100µM,and 200µM of AP for 48 hours across three independent trials. Cell viability was quantified utilizing a Cell Titer Glo 2.0 Assay which detects the relative amount of ATP which can be used as a marker for cell viability. The p-AKT protein expressed within the PI3K -AKT pathway was assessed by measuring the phosphorylation of AKT protein through western blot. A one way ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test was used to analyze Cell Titer Glo data, and a Kruskal Wallis test with a Dunn's post Hoc test was used for western blot data. It was hypothesized that increasing AP concentrations would decrease phosphorylation of AKT protein and reduce cell viability. These findings are crucial to novel non-toxic therapies and may establish AP as a targeted therapeutic candidate for Burkitt's Lymphoma.

column13: Using SAMSON molecular docking to identify novel FGFR1 inhibitors from Human Endogenous Metabolites in-silico
column14: Around 3.5% of all cancer patients have an overexpression of the Fibroblast growth factor receptor protein 1, (FGFR1). The presence of this protein and its signaling pathway dramatically increases the mortality rate, metastasis, and angiogenesis. Past research has identified that compounds that bind to the FGFR1 protein can inhibit the protein signaling and FGFR1’s effects on cancer. In this study, the researchers aimed to predict an inhibitor to the FGFR1 protein using molecular docking, a novel, in-silico approach that predicts a compound's inhibitory effects by calculating the amount of energy released from the binding between proteins and compound The more energy that is released, measured in -kCal/mol, the stronger the predicted inhibitory effect. This study screened over 50 compounds from the human endogenous metabolome with the binding affinity results being compared to the binding affinity of Pemigatinib, a known medication for FGFR1 cancers. Results have indicated that Sanguinarine, a benzophenanthridine alkaloid commonly found in the Sangunaria Cadensis plant to have a higher average binding affinity than Pemigatinib (-9.243 to -9.2066), a T test was performed which yielded a p value of 0.93, suggesting that there is no significant difference between the binding affinities of the two compounds. This shows Sanguinarine as a possible inhibitor of the FGFR1 with a comparable effectiveness to a current FGFR1 cancer medication.

column13: Investigating the Effect of Natural Treatments Quercetin and Resveratrol on Cell Viability in BxPC-3 Pancreatic Cancer Cells
column14:
Pancreatic cancer is one of the deadliest cancers in the United States, with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma being the most common form. Hyperactivation of the PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling pathway promotes tumor proliferation and therapeutic resistance, yet single-agent therapies targeting this pathway have limited efficacy. Natural compounds quercetin and resveratrol each modulate this pathway through distinct mechanisms, suggesting increased effects when combined. This study investigated which dosage combination of quercetin and resveratrol most significantly reduces cell viability in BxPC-3 pancreatic cancer cells. 
BxPC-3 cells were seeded and treated with four combination groups: 10µM resveratrol + 10µM quercetin, 10µM resveratrol + 40µM quercetin, 30µM resveratrol + 10µM quercetin, and 30µM resveratrol + 40µM quercetin. Single-compound, vehicle, and negative controls were also tested. Cell viability was measured using an acid phosphatase assay across three trials with eight replicates per condition. 
Results varied across trials. In Trial 1, combinations with higher quercetin concentrations showed slightly more noticeable decreases in cell viability compared to other combinations. In Trial 2, the 30µM resveratrol + 40µM quercetin combination showed the lowest viability values, near zero, while single-compound treatments had little to no effect. Trial 3 did not show a consistent decrease in viability with increasing concentrations, and no strong optimal ratio was clearly identified, as high variability among the replicates occurred.
Overall, the data suggests that specific combinations, particularly at higher concentrations, may be more effective at reducing cell viability than single-compound treatments, though further trials with reduced variability are needed to confirm this.

column13: Investgiating  Human Luekmia when Exposed to Nrf2-siRNA and ZnPP  Treatments to Increase Oxidative  Stress
column14:
Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia remains a disease where a significant minority of patients struggle to find effective treatment. To address this, cells were treated with ZnPP inhibitors to maximize oxidative stress and destroy cancer cells. The CellTiter-Glo assay measured luminescence (ATP levels) of Jurkat T cells at ZnPP concentrations of 0, 2.5, 5, 10, and 20 μM. Luminescence values showed substantial variability and overlap across all groups, with no clear trend as concentration increased. One notable outlier was observed around 5 μM, which displayed a much higher luminescence value than the rest of the data. To further analyze these differences, a Kruskal–Wallis test was performed, yielding an H-statistic of 10.74 with 4 degrees of freedom and a p-value of 0.03, confirming a statistically significant difference in luminescence among at least one of the ZnPP concentration groups.
These findings indicate that ZnPP has a measurable effect on Jurkat cell activity; however, the significant overlap between groups and the absence of a clear dose-dependent trend suggest that this effect is inconsistent across concentrations. While ZnPP may influence cell viability through increased oxidative stress, it does not reliably reduce cancer cell activity in a predictable manner at the tested doses. Overall, ZnPP shows potential as a therapeutic for Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia, but further investigation is required to determine the optimal concentration and confirm its effectiveness and consistency.

column13: Investigating the Effect of Valerenic Acid on Inhibiting Calcium Deposition in Vascular Smooth Muscle Cells
column14: The primary purpose of the following research is to determine the effect of Valerenic acid on inhibiting calcium deposition in Vascular Smooth Muscle Cells (VSMC's). The research models atherosclerosis and Generalized Arterial Calcification of Infancy (GACI) by inducing calcification build up on A7r5 cells derived from a rat's aorta. In order to fully evaluate effects, an acid phosphatase assay would be used along with a stain to reveal calcification levels before and after treatment. The conclusions would be analyzed using a non-parametric ANOVA test and could support a difference in the values of cell survival and calcification with and without the treatment. column13: Comparing the Role of Vitamin B7 and Vitamin B12: Impact on IL-6 Cytokine Expression by 4T1 Breast Cancer Cells Inflamed by Lipopolysaccharides
column14: Breast cancer, the second deadliest cancer in the world, affects roughly 1 in 8 women in the U.S., with chemotherapy resistance often caused by IL-6 overexpression, causing side effects on patients. In this study, a novel dual treatment using Vitamin B7 and Vitamin B12  is investigated in its effects on IL-6 expression by Lipopolysaccharide inflamed 4T1 cells. It was hypothesized that if 4T1 cells are treated with a dual treatment of Vitamin B7 and Vitamin B12, then IL-6 expression will decrease due to Vitamin B7 targeting overexpressed SMVTs, allowing Vitamin B12 to scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) via antioxidant properties. Sequential analysis using a Western Blot for testing IL-6 expression, and Acid Phosphatase for treatment toxicity, determined through cell viability. This was done for Vitamin B7, Vitamin B12, and Dual treatments at 125uM, 250uM, and 500uM. Acid Phosphatase trials found no significant reduction in cell viability, indicating the treatments were not cytotoxic. However, 500uM Vitamin B12 treatment potentially influenced IL-6 expression due to statistically significant increase in cell viability compared to a control (p = 0.09). Furthermore, the 500uM dual treatments were found to be most effective in maintaining stable cell viability (p = 0.14). While B12 showed some positive effect on viability, the study indicates that combined B7 and B12 treatment shows potential for mitigating the inflammatory response that drives chemotherapy resistance. By regulating overexpressed IL-6, this dual treatment could improve cancer treatment efficacy, allowing chemotherapy to target cancer cells more effectively and improving patient outcome.

column13: The Effect of Medioresinol on the Induction of Cell Death of Breast Cancer Cells Through the Activation of the p53 Protein.
column14: In 2022 alone, over 2.3 million women were diagnosed with breast cancer (WHO, 2025). Breast cancer is a highly invasive disease characterized by the uncontrolled growth of malignant cells. While treatments are currently available, many are expensive and associated with harsh side effects, creating a need for more natural and accessible alternatives. Lignans, a subset of phytoestrogens, have emerged as potential treatment options due to their natural origin and possible apoptotic effects. Medioresinol, a lignan found in lentils, has been shown to induce oxidative stress in cells (Hwang et al., 2012), which induces oxidative stress. Oxidative stress can activate the p53 protein and trigger apoptosis (Liu & Xu, 2011). In this study, MCF-7 human breast cancer cells were cultured and treated with medioresinol at concentrations of 25µM and 50µM, dissolved in DMSO, alongside a 0µM negative control. Two 96-well plates were used to conduct acid phosphatase assays measuring cell viability, with results analyzed using a 2-way ANOVA. Additionally, a 24-well plate was used for western blot analysis to measure p53 protein expression, with data analyzed via a Kruskal-Wallis test. Results from the acid phosphatase assays showed an increase in cell viability with higher medioresinol concentrations, with corrected values rising from 62.54 (control) to 195.91 (50µM). Western blot analysis confirmed p53 expression in treated cells. Although medioresinol increased cell viability, the presence of p53 suggests it may serve as a natural complementary treatment, potentially contributing to future breast cancer therapies that promote tumor suppression.

column13: Analyzing the effect of Eucalyptol on a skeletal muscle atrophy model of C2C12 myotubes by measuring the expression of atrophy indicators FBXO32 and TRIM63
column14:
Skeletal muscle atrophy (SMA) is a muscle disease that occurs when protein breakdown exceeds protein synthesis. Oxidative stress is a key driver of SMA, triggering the Ubiquitin Proteasome System (UPS), where E3 ubiquitin ligases Atrogin-1 and MuRF1, encoded by genes FBXO32 and TRIM63, tag skeletal muscle proteins for degradation. Eucalyptol, a natural compound with strong antioxidant properties, was investigated as a potential alleviant of SMA due to its promising combative effects against oxidative stress with its antioxidant properties. This was modeled in C2C12 mouse myotubes due to their structural and functional similarities with human skeletal muscle.

C2C12 myotubes were differentiated into myotubes and induced with atrophy using a hypoxia chamber. Cells were then treated with varying concentrations of Eucalyptol. Cell viability was determined using an Acid Phosphatase Assay, and gene expression of the atrophy indicators FBXO32 and TRIM63 were intended to be quantified via PCR analysis.

Due to timeframe constraints, only one trial of the acid phosphatase was completed and PCR has not been started. Additionally, seeding errors resulted in an insufficient amount of cells in each plate. This created an unreliable assessment of Eucalyptol’s effect on the cell line. As a result, no statistically significant conclusions can be made from the current data.

Despite these limitations, Eucalyptol remains a promising alleviant for SMA. If future results support the original hypothesis, this will lay the foundation for studies examining Eucalyptol in synergy with other antioxidant compounds to determine whether a combined effect produces a stronger alleviation of SMA.

column13: Comparative Analysis of the IL-6 Cytokine Protein Expression through Protein-Corona Modified vs. Pristine Microplastics in SHSY-5Y Cells.
column14: Microplastics are small pieces of plastics that are found in almost every man-made object used today. These plastics, although useful for everyday life, can penetrate into the body and interfere with cell function. This study aims to investigate whether or not protein corona-coated microplastics cause a stronger harmful effect on neuronal cells compared to normal, pristine microplastics. SHSY-5Y human neuroblastoma cells were exposed to four treatments: protein corona-coated microplastics formed using fetal bovine serum (FBS), pristine (uncoated) microplastics, lipopolysaccharide (LPS) as a positive control, and untreated cells (negative control). Cells were incubated under controlled conditions and treated for 48 hours. Cell viability was measured using an acid phosphatase assay, detecting enzyme activity in living cells, while inflammatory response will be assessed by measuring IL-6 protein expression using Western blot analysis. The results of the acid phosphatase showed that cells exposed to uncoated microplastics had higher viability, while protein corona-coated microplastics reduce cell viability, which suggests that there is increased cellular stress. These findings suggest that the presence of a protein corona changes how microplastics interact with cells, potentially increasing their harmful effects. Overall, this study aims to highlight the link between environmental pollution and neuronal health.

column13: MT1 Melatonin Receptor Signaling and ERK-Mediated Stress Responses in MCF-7 Cells
column14: Melatonin signaling influences cancer cell growth, oxidative stress responses, and circadian regulation of many cellular functions, but the effect of melatonin receptor inhibition on oxidative stress responses remains unclear. This study investigated whether blocking the melatonin receptor 1 (MT1) with luzindole has an impact on the viability of MCF-7 breast cancer cells and downstream ERK signaling under H2O2-induced oxidative stress. ERK signaling was used as an indicator of repair effort post-oxidative damage due to its role in the regulation of the cell cycle and control over damage response. In this experiment, MCF-7 cells were treated with 10 μM or 30 μM luzindole, with and without 100 μM H2O2. Cell viability was measured using an acid phosphatase assay, and p-ERK protein expression was evaluated using a Western blot. Results showed dose-dependent effects of luzindole. 30uM luzindole showed reduced viability in comparison to controls. Under oxidative stress, 10uM luzindole showed increased viability in comparison to 10uM without H2O2 exposure and H2O2 alone, while 30uM luzindole + H2O2 showed a significant decrease in viability. Western blot analysis of p-ERK showed that there was an impact on downstream signaling post-MT1 inhibition. These findings suggest that melatonin receptor signaling has a biphasic impact on survival and stress-response pathways in MCF-7 breast cancer cells. In future research and treatments, MT1 signaling may be a useful tool in modifying tumor sensitivity to DNA damage.

column13: Investigation of the Learning Process Through Game Development
column14: This project aims to investigate the process of meta-learning by developing a game from scratch. Meta-learning is, at its core, learning about how to learn. It is a skill that can dramatically improve every aspect of one’s life because of its versatility, broad application, and transferability to whatever the person wishes. Game development is a large field that encompasses many different disciplines that are not necessarily connected to one another, so interacting with and learning about all these disciplines can lead to a more broad and overarching understanding of not only game development in this case, but how learning happens, what affects it, and how to better the learning process. The end product of this research would be a documentation of developing a game and its adjacent fields to then observe any long-term themes, lessons, problems, and conclusions in regards to how the learning occurred.

column13: Exploring Innovative Solutions For Reliable Energy Storage to Empower Communities in Nigeria Through Sustainable External Energy Sources.
column14:
This research examines the development of an affordable, environmentally friendly gravity energy storage technology to address energy insecurity in rural areas of Northern Nigeria. This area has an unreliable electricity supply due to inadequate infrastructure, high fossil fuel costs, and limited solar energy availability. Despite the increasing use of solar cells, their efficiency is constrained by their availability and by their ability to provide adequate energy at specific times of day.

The procedure suggested involves using solar energy to run a peristaltic pump that pumps water into a storage reservoir, where it accumulates energy as gravitational potential. When energy is required, the water is released to generate electricity. This technology avoids the use of chemical batteries, which are expensive, have a limited shelf life, and pose a challenge for safe disposal in rural areas.

Using readily available materials such as water, tubing, basic electronic components, and open-source microcontrollers, this project aims to create a model that is not only reproducible but also sustainable for local communities. Initial experiments confirm the technical feasibility of this design, thus enabling future improvements. This paper provides an overview of the system concept, the design process, early evaluations, and its potential as a sustainable alternative to conventional chemical and magnetic energy storage techniques.

column13: Advanced Orbit Calculator: Non-Uniform Density of Asteroids and their Satellite Parameters
column14: Space agencies such as the National Aeronautics Space Administration (NASA) and European Space Agency (ESA) have hundreds of failed deployments due to gravitational variations that could destabilize satellite paths, leading to collision risks, communication loss, and premature re-entry into the Earth Atmosphere without the completion of the mission (McInnes & Mcdonald, 2025). This issue highlights the necessity for a calculator that can account for the varying densities and accurately predict satellite parameters including the orbital period and orbital velocity. Future satellite missions can benefit from a calculator which can account for different internal compositions of celestial bodies and asteroids such as ice, rock, metal, and gas (Barnett, 2025). A study in Caltech used a wide range of observational methods to refine the 3-D shape of 16 Psyche and produce a global surface map (Cambioni et al., 2022). This model allowed them to identify several different regions where there is enhanced radar albedo, indicating that there are possible surface irregularities on 16Psyche (Cambioni et al., 2022). This can help understand the surface of 16Psyche to model it accurately in the MATLAB simulation conducted and correctly plant the metal-rich patches in the correct area. This highlights the nonuniformity of the planet and the importance of understanding the changes that a satellite may undergo while conducting important experiments like these. If results show that slight changes are made to the satellite’s path, this experiment can highlight the length of these changes for future missions to bypass this issue.

column13: Developing an App to Investigate Whether an Automatically Adjusting Active Filter can Mitigate CVD
column14: Color Vision Deficiency (CVD) affects roughly 4.5% of the world population, with red-green, green deficient CVD being by far the most common of those. This type of colorblindness, also known as Deuteranomaly, has by far the most extensive research done on it, which has lead to extensive work being done on mitigation techniques. Most of the research that has been done so far has mainly focused on physical filters and lenses, but more recently work has been done with digital techniques. One such idea is to have a digital filter applied through an app that can mitigate CVD in the same way as a physical filter. Our hypothesis is that by combining a CVD filter with a mosaic CVD test, the effects of deuteranomaly can be fully or partially mitigated. The mosaic test consists of 16 filters, and the test was taken 10 times each time it was tested. The filter was designed in such a way that it analysed the results from the test and adjusted itself accordingly to improve accuracy. The results from the test were very promising, showing a 15.67% improvement on score averages, from 80.60% to 96.27%. This indicates that the app design works, and could be used in real world applications. Additionally, the app could be adjusted for other forms of colorblindness simply by adjusting the colors that the mosaic test tests for and the colors that the filter uses.

column13: Revolutionizing low orbital rockets: understanding if fiberglass is more efficient that carbon fiber for rocket fins to yield higher acceleration.
column14: Low-orbital rockets require fin materials that balance strength, weight, and aerodynamic stability to optimize flight performance. This study examines whether fiberglass or carbon fiber produces higher peak acceleration in model rockets, with the goal of identifying the more effective material for rocket fins. Trapezoidal fins were designed using Fusion 360 and fabricated from fiberglass and carbon fiber sheets in 1 mm, 3 mm, and 5 mm thicknesses using a water jet. Three fins of each material and thickness were attached to model rocket bodies. Each rocket was launched using a C6-5 motor and an altimeter for data collection. Peak acceleration (m/s2) was recorded across multiple trials. A two-way ANOVA test was used to analyze the effects of material type and fin thickness on peak acceleration. Results indicated that fiberglass generally produced higher peak acceleration values than carbon fiber across most thicknesses. Carbon fiber trials ranged from 73.5 to 82.4 m/s2, while fiberglass ranged from 76.4 to 97.1 m/s², with the highest values observed in thinner fiberglass configurations. However, statistical analysis showed no significant effect of material (p = 0.43) or thickness (p = 0.38) on peak acceleration. Although fiberglass showed a trend toward higher peak acceleration, the differences between materials were not statistically significant. This suggests that fiberglass and carbon fiber perform similarly under the tested conditions. The findings implied that material choice alone may not strongly influence acceleration, and future research should investigate fin geometry, composite layering, and aerodynamic interactions to better optimize rocket fin design for performance improvement.

column13: Uncovering the Secrets of the Universe: Hubble Constant Found from High-Redshift Baryon Acoustic Oscillations
column14: The existence of a large discrepancy between early and late-Universe measurements of the Hubble constant (H0) is currently one of the most significant challenges in modern cosmology. Baryon acoustic oscillations (BAOs) serve as a key cosmological probe in the effort to resolve this tension by providing a baseline for the measurement of H0. We were able to successfully detect the baryon acoustic oscillation peak at high redshift from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) Data Release 1 (DR1) based on data collected between redshifts of 2 < z < 3 as well as measure the Hubble constant based on BAO data. We combine existing methods of computing the two-point correlation function with a novel conversion from redshift space to real space and detect a BAO peak at roughly 110 h-1 Mpc. We computed the value of various cosmological parameters by combining n-body simulations used for mock catalog generation and CosmoMC analysis to obtain values of H0 = 67.8 ± 1.2 km s-1 Mpc-1, Ωm = 0.31 ± 0.084, ΩΛ = 0.69 ± 0.084. This result indicates that the BAO data collected from our selected redshift range are in agreement with other early universe results, demonstrating the potential of DESI as a tool to measure cosmological parameters throughout its survey volume.

column13: Surviving Stellar Fury: A Million Year Analysis of Atmospheric Escape of Exoplanets caused by Interplanetary Shocks
column14:
This project investigates how interplanetary shocks (IPS) caused by coronal mass ejections affect atmospheric loss on Earth-like exoplanets orbiting G-type stars over a timescale of 1,000,000 years. Since atmospheric stability is a key factor in planetary habitability, understanding how space weather contributes to long-term atmospheric erosion is important. The simulation is divided into three parts: modeling CME generation and solar wind, simulating IPS propagation through space, and analyzing interactions with a planet’s magnetosphere and upper atmosphere. Data including pressure, velocity, density, and magnetic field are passed between each stage and converted into atmospheric loss over time.

A control simulation with no CME activity was used to establish baseline atmospheric loss. This was compared to multiple exoplanets, including Kepler-68 b. Results show that while the general trend of atmospheric loss over time is similar between planets, exoplanets exposed to IPS experience significantly greater total loss compared to the control. For example, Kepler-68 b followed a similar curve to the control but lost a much higher percentage of its atmosphere due to repeated shock interactions. This suggests that even planets with Earth-like properties can experience substantial atmospheric erosion under stronger space weather conditions.

Overall, the results indicate that interplanetary shocks play a major role in long-term atmospheric loss and should be considered when evaluating exoplanet habitability.

column13: Engineering roofing granules with reactive mineral coatings to capture atmospheric CO₂
column14: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) levels have risen from about 280 ppm prior to the industrial revolution to over 420 ppm in 2023, marking the highest concentrations in at least two million years. This project explores the potential of carbon capture roofing granules as a passive and scalable method to reduce atmospheric CO₂. Roofing materials coated with reactive minerals may capture carbon dioxide while maintaining durability, offering a new approach that has not yet been widely studied or implemented. An ANOVA test was performed to compare the variation between group means. There was a statistically significant result (p < 0.05) indicating that at least one group differs significantly from the others. A post-HOC test was performed to compare the control and each group produced a significant result of (p < 0.05) indicating that the control group differs significantly from the others. The consistent mass gain trend in the Ca(OH)₂ and Mg(OH)₂ samples provides strong preliminary evidence that mineral-coated roofing granules can function as a passive carbon capture material. The main limitations of this study were sample size and exposure duration. This approach is promising because if roofing granules are engineered to capture carbon, roofs could function as passive, maintenance-free carbon sinks without requiring additional land use or energy input. Future work consists of testing this material against various environmental conditions. In conclusion, this project demonstrates that engineered roofing granules can potentially offer a novel, scalable pathway for climate mitigation.

column13: Testing the Combined Effects of Berberine and Cynadin-3-glucoside Chloride on Insulin Regulation in Drosophila Melanogaster
column14: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) affects over 483 million people globally. Biologically, it is mainly due to insulin resistance which is the process in which the body’s cells become less sensitive to insulin. In turn, glucose is insufficiently regulated. Over time, it causes hyperglycemic conditions, oxidative stress, and damaged beta cells. Even though there is no cure, some prescription drugs provide alleviation treatment, but they often have a high cost and many side effects. Bioactive compounds have been found to have the potential to do this. These are natural substances that have a positive effect on a living organism. Many bioactive compounds have been identified that can improve glucose homeostasis and insulin signaling. A 2025 study conducted by Ye et al. found these characteristics within cyanidin-3-glucoside chloride. Similarly, a 2024 study conducted by Arjun et al. found the same with berberine. The problem is that the effects aren’t strong enough for clinical application or human testing. This reveals the possibility of combining bioactive compounds for a stronger result, but it remains unexplored. Foundational studies have shown that different bioactive compounds including alkaloids and flavonoids have distinct metabolic mechanisms during insulin secretion. C3G enhances endocrine signals upstream of insulin releases. On the other hand, berberine improves insulin pathway efficacy which affects the downstream release. This difference suggests they would have a more powerful effect on reversing insulin resistance if combined. Can berberine and cyanidin 3-glucoside chloride regulate insulin expression in Drosophila melanogaster in a high-sugar diet?

column13: Validating the Existence of Quasi-Periodic Oscillations in Astronomical Objects Using Synthetic Waveforms
column14: Quasi-Periodic Oscillations (QPOs) are becoming increasingly important for astronomers interested in understanding the origins of black holes. Current analysis methods are unable to generalize to multiple types of QPOs and thus case studies are required to individually analyze each body. This study investigates the ability of a three-pronged synthetic waveform recreation approach to validate the existence of QPOs at certain frequencies in multiple types of bodies. A dataset of photon emissions over time from NASA’s Rossi X-ray Timing Explorer were analyzed using Fast Fourier Transform and Lorentzian model fitting. Then, a skeleton of the expected QPO power spectrum was createdgenerated, followed by the random superimposition of light curves and the implementation of Poisson noise and cross-phase power spectrum lag.

column13: Finding Optimal Coil Shape for Use in Wireless Resonant Inductive Power Transfer EV Charging Systems
column14: This study investigates the power transfer efficiency (PTE) of wireless resonant inductive power transfer (WRIPT) under varying coil alignment conditions and geometries to determine the most optimal coil shape for use in dynamic wireless EV charging. The main purpose was to analyze how latitudinal and longitudinal misalignment and coil shape affect PTE in a multi-coil system. Three coil shapes (circular, rectangular, and square) were tested using copper coils and capacitors in a resonant circuit. Input and output voltages were measured across a resistor, and PTE was calculated for each configuration. Under controlled conditions (0% misalignment), rectangular coils achieved the greatest efficiency, reaching ~71.8% PTE, circular coils second, reaching ~70.6% PTE, and square coils last, reaching ~60.8% PTE. Regardless of coil shape, efficiency decreased as misalignment increased, supporting that coupling between coils is highly impacted by latitudinal and longitudinal misalignment. Rectangular coils proved least susceptible to varying alignment conditions, seeing the lowest decrease in PTE under 10% and 20% misalignment. These results are consistent with the expected result that reduced magnetic field overlap lowers overall PTE. This experiment demonstrates that both coil geometry and alignment are important factors in optimizing dynamic wireless power transfer systems. The results suggest that minimizing misalignment is fundamental in improving system efficiency, and the use of rectangular coil designs is most optimal in achieving a greater PTE under normal and misaligned conditions.

column13: Advanced Orbit Calculator: Non-Uniform Density of Asteroids and their Satellite Parameters
column14: Space agencies such as the National Aeronautics Space Administration (NASA) and European Space Agency (ESA) have hundreds of failed deployments due to gravitational variations that could destabilize satellite paths, leading to collision risks, communication loss, and premature re-entry into the Earth Atmosphere without the completion of the mission (McInnes & Mcdonald, 2025). This issue highlights the necessity for a calculator that can account for the varying densities and accurately predict satellite parameters including the orbital period and orbital velocity. Future satellite missions can benefit from a calculator which can account for different internal compositions of celestial bodies and asteroids such as ice, rock, metal, and gas (Barnett, 2025). Using a MATLAB simulation and the Runge Kutta Method, researchers understood the relationship between the non-uniform density and the satellite parameters, including orbital velocity and orbital period, closely following the constants used by the recent NASA 16 Psyche mission. 200 data points were used in a density range of 3,400-4,100 kg/m^3 to approximate the change in satellite parameters within this wide range. Specifically, varied altitudes were produced using the four altitudes stated in the NASA mission (709, 303, 190, and 75 kilometers). The satellite parameters were measured through two equations: the vis viva equation for orbital velocity and elliptical orbital period equation, both manipulated to include density within it. Then, the code was thoroughly analyzed and graphs were made, highlighting the changes in density and its impact on the satellite parameters.

column13: Combating Space Debris: Modeling Laser Ablation to Minimize Plasma Shielding through Changing the Pulse Shape of a High-Power High-Frequency Laser
column14: One of the main methods being developed for mitigating space debris is the employment of space-based lasers to vaporize pieces of debris. This study aimed to determine whether changing the pulse shape of a high-frequency, high-power laser affected the plasma shielding observed on a target material and, if so, which pulse shape most effectively limited plasma shielding. The pulse shapes tested were Gaussian, rectangular, exponential, and sawtooth. A MATLAB model was created to determine the total ablation depth and deflected energy during laser ablation of an aluminum cube with a high-power, high-frequency laser. A verification study was carried out to determine the accuracy of the code’s results. The results of this study supported the conclusion that the laser pulse shape did affect plasma shielding and that the sawtooth pulse had the lowest deflected energy, while the exponential pulse had the highest. The pulse shape that was deemed the most effective for laser ablation was the exponential pulse shape because it had the highest ablated material to deflected energy ratio.

column13: Optimizing the Structure of Synthetically Replicated Shark Denticles to Reduce Drag on High Speed Aircraft
column14: Denticles are small enamel structures embedded in the dermal layer of sharks. Denticles allow sharks to glide through the water, reducing drag and allowing the sharks to expend less energy while swimming. Both water and air are fluids, so researchers began applying artificial denticles in the field of aerodynamics. The effects of artificial denticles on the drag of aircraft has been tested, showing that they can have drag reducing effects. Synthetic denticles are difficult and costly to apply to the exterior of aircraft. Synthetic denticles function, but their cost currently outweighs their benefits. My research aimed to determine the optimal denticle dimensions for high speed aircraft in order to render denticles beneficial enough for their benefit to outweigh their costs. I established my independent variables by choosing dimensions from the 3d model of a Mako denticle to modify. After choosing my dimensions, I modeled the mako denticles in Blender and chose to affix them to an airfoil to test them. Each denticle was changed separately and tested in batches, with each batch being different but all denticles in a batch being the same. The arrays of artificial denticles were affixed to an airfoil in Blender, then printed using a resin printer. The airfoils were tested in a wind tunnel, measuring the drag in g’s of the airfoil and its respective denticles. I was unable to test all models, but gathered enough data to determine the effect of a single dimension of the denticles.
