column13: Rebuild, Renew, Recover: Testing the Overexpression of RAC1 in Wound Regeneration of Drosophila Melanogaster
column14:
Wound regeneration is a complex biological process that relies on coordinated cellular movement and cytoskeletal remodeling. This study investigates the role of the small GTPase Rac1 in epithelial wound healing using Drosophila melanogaster as a model organism. Utilizing the UAS/GAL4 system, Rac1 was overexpressed in larval tissues to examine its effects on actin dynamics and wound closure.

Standardized puncture wounds (20–30 µm) were introduced into the larval abdomen, and wound healing was monitored over defined time intervals. Wound width was quantified using microscopy and image analysis to assess the rate of closure. Comparisons were made between Rac1-overexpressing larvae and control groups to determine differences in regeneration efficiency.

Preliminary findings suggest that altered Rac1 activity impacts cytoskeletal organization, influencing the rate and coordination of wound closure. These results support the hypothesis that Rac1 plays a critical regulatory role in actin-mediated repair mechanisms.

This study contributes to a broader understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying tissue regeneration and highlights the importance of cytoskeletal regulation in wound healing, with potential implications for regenerative biology and medical research.

column13: Analyzing Solar Modulation of Galactic Cosmic Rays Using Fermi‑LAT Observations
column14: Galactic cosmic rays (GCRs) are high-energy charged particles from deep space. Their flux near Earth, often in the form of gamma rays. This poses one of the greatest obstacles in space exploration as radiation exposure increases drastically beyond the Earth’s atmosphere. It is known that GCRs are impacted by solar magnetic activity. This study investigates how the two different phases of the solar cycle influence GCR intensity. Gamma-ray light curve (LC) data from the Fermi Large Area Telescope were analyzed for selected dates during solar minimum (December 2019) and solar maximum (October 2024). Daily gamma-ray flux values were computed from LC files. To correlate solar activity, coronal mass ejection (CME) from the SOHO/LASCO catalog was recorded. For each date, CME count was defined as the product of count and speed. Results show that gamma-ray flux values were generally higher during solar minimum than solar maximum. Additionally, an inverse relationship was observed between CME activity and gamma-ray flux, suggesting that increased solar activity suppresses GCR intensity. However, the correlation was weak, likely due to limited sample size. These findings support the hypothesis that the increased solar magnetic activity during solar maximum reduces GCR flux near Earth.

column13: Self-powered textiles: enhancements to the piezoelectric properties of polyvinylidene fluoride and quartz on fabric using copper nanoparticles
column14: Piezoelectric materials are materials that can create electrical pulses from crystal disslocation, which is why they are often applied in converting kinetic energy into electrical. Such materials include quartz, polymers, and lead zirconate titanate, the latter of which is the most commonly used in microsensors but is also brittle, toxic and expensive. Quartz and polymers, specifically polyvinylidene fluoride, have been researched in flexible environments, which is why this research focused on applying such materials onto cotton textiles through spin coating. Two base solutions, one containing just polyvinylidene fluoride and the other containing polyvinylidene fluoride and quartz, and dissolved using dimethyl sulfoxide, were created. Conductive materials have been shown to improve the electrical output of such materials, so copper nanoparticles were added in different concentrations to the textiles. The copper concentrations ranged from 0.1% w/v to 0.5% w/v, with each solution being five milliliters. One milliliter was spin coated on a 2 cm length square piece of 100% cotton fabric using a stepper motor. A tesile tester was used to apply a force from 0-12 N and create electrical pulses. It was found that copper nanoparticles increased the electrical output of the textiles in for both solutions, though the initial electrical output and increase by the copper nanoparticles was much smaller in the polyvinylidene fluoride/quartz solution than the polyvinylidene solution.

column13: Reducing Nail Waste: Evaluating the Applicability of Treated Wood and Polymer in Fastener Free Joints
column14: Almost everywhere a person looks inside a house, office, or any other building there is one common denominator: Nails & Screws. Nails & Screws used to join together countless materials and make furniture. While relatively simple and reliable these fastener joints use nails and screws that create millions in tons of annual metal waste. Thus, to minimize this metal and economic waste while maintaining compressive strength, this study aims to examine the applicability of fastener free joints in comparison with the conventional end to end joint. The joints in question are Japanese Sashimono joints which are renowned for not using any metal fasteners. A sashimono joint known for its compressive strength is the mortise-tenon while a commonly found furniture joint also known for its compressive strength is the end to end joint. Hence, an experimental group of three mortise-tenon compression joints will be made and tested on a testing apparatus against three screwed end to end joints which are commonly found in furniture. After data is collected it will be subsequently extracted from the testing apparatus and analyzed via a Kruskal-Wallis test to determine statistically significant differences between the fastener and fastener free joints relative to compressive strength in newtons. The conclusions from this statistical analysis will be used to ultimately determine if fastener free joints have similar if not higher compressive strength than their fastener counterparts.

column13: Determining the Influence of Black Hole Merging Events on Local Space-Time Fabric Through the usage of Lagrange Point Stability
column14: Understanding the intricacies regarding the developments of space-time fabric and the forthcoming of the universe can be of investigation in the topic of black holes. Through the utilization of a N-body simulation template, Schwarzschild black hole merging’s impact on local space-time fabric was analyzed through the stability of their Lagrange Points L1-L5. Different characteristics of the black holes, such as accretion rate and hardness (the distance between the outer layer of black holes at the timeframe of initialization), were examined with respect to their influence on Lagrange Point stability. This stability was quantified by relative displacement, essentially analyzing the percent change of the displacement from the calculation at a point from one time step in comparison to the previous time step. Results have supported the claims of a “glitch” or “gravitational burst” event for hardness’s influence on stability, characterized by a single point in the timeframe in which stability breaks off. Accretion rate results have been shown to be inconsistent, with some Lagrange Points showcasing sinusoidal-like graphs in comparison to other ones having single-point breakoffs.

column13: Cleaning our water: Filtering aqueous pet microplastics using hematite-based electrospun fibers
column14: Microplastics (MPs) are widely defined as fragments of plastic that are less than five millimeters (< 5mm) in diameter. MPs have various adverse effects on the health of living organisms. In plants, they can cause reduced shoot and root growth, as well as reduced photosynthetic ability. In mammals and humans, they can give rise to various cancers, ulcers, and neurodegenerative diseases. Due to the polar nature of microplastics, heavy metals have been found to adsorb onto their surface. The heavy metal hematite (Fe2O3) was integrated into electrospun fibers in increasing concentrations (0g, 0.02g, 0.04g, 0.06g, 0.08, 0.1g) to serve as a filter for aqueous polyethylene terephthalate (PET) microplastics. The final PET microplastic mass after filter exposure was recorded. After removing outliers, it was found that the R^2 value of the graph was 0.914, the graph being a logarithmic decline. This supports the claim that an increase in hematite (Fe2O3) in electrospun fibers does indeed increase microplastic filtration efficiency.

column13: Applying Pre-Stretching to Conductive Electrode Layer in DEAs for Better Anisotropic Dielectric Elastomers
column14: Dielectric elastomer actuators (DEAs) are an important technology in soft robotics, comprised of electrode layers that are both highly conductive and deform under voltage sandwiching a non-conductive elastomer layer. A specialized variant, the anistropic dielectric elastomer actuator (ADE), is able to achieve directionally dependent actuation through mechanical reinforcement, often through pre-stretching. This experiment investigates the effect of pre-stretching directly on a carbon black-Ecoflex electrode layer, rather than the dielectric layer, on the performance of directional actuation. Five electrode layers were created through the combination of a 5 wt% carbon black-IPA slurry and Ecoflex 00-10, along with the usage of doctor-blade for layer creation, before cutting the layer into 50x50 mm samples. Each sample was prestretched using a Mark-10 F305 at increments of 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mm to introduce directional mechanical reinforcement on the materials. Mechanical testing includes the Young's Modulus, while ADE performance was evaluated through the application of voltages from 0 to 14 kV, along with the measurement of the respective displacement in both the prestretched and perpendicular directions. Results were compared through use of a Kruskal-Wallis and one-way ANOVA, with each sample being compared to a typical, non-stretched ADE.

column13: Novel Neuroevolution to Train Neuromorphic AI using Co-Adversidal RL, P-bits, and Optimizations
column14: Artificial neural networks (ANN’s) have an issue with the von Neumann bottleneck where the processing and storage is separate, requiring much of the power is spent to accommodate this. However, training spiking neural networks (SNNs) remains challenging due to non-differentiable discrete outputs that prevent conventional backpropagation. Current approaches like surrogate gradients, ANN-to-SNN conversion and synaptic plasticity-based methods (STDP) fail to learn discriminative features necessary for complex pattern recognition and sacrifice efficiency. This research proposes a novel approach combining neuroevolution and neuromorphic hardware to overcome these limitations. We implement NEAT (Neuroevolution of Augmenting Topologies) using Compositional Pattern Producing Networks (CPPN) to automatically discover optimal SNN topologies without gradient computation. The algorithm runs on a Jetson Nano and interfaces with an FPGA-based reconfigurable neuromorphic architecture (RANC), which executes evolved networks on specialized hardware. P-bit computing and cost-constrained fitness functions guide evolution toward architectures balancing performance and efficiency. Optimal configurations are subsequently deployed on Akida neuromorphic processors for final deployment. We benchmark evolved SNNs against CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet datasets, comparing results to ANN2SNN conversion, surrogate gradient methods, and conventional HyperNEAT baselines. However, we can not make any conclusions since we have collected no data and would need more time to collect data and form conclusions.

column13: Investigating the Effects of Satellites’ Eccentricity, Inclination, and Mass Ratios on Their Orbits with Applications to Uranus’ Moons
column14: This study focuses on the three-dimensional close encounter subclass of the three body problem, which involves a close encounter between two massive satellites orbiting a more massive central body. Specifically, this study aimed to determine the effect of varying initial eccentricities and inclinations of the two satellites’ orbits as well as the mass ratio of the two satellites on satellite eccentricities and inclinations after an encounter. N-body integrators in the REBOUND package was used to simulate a deterministic sweep of these initial conditions. It was observed that for greater time periods such as 100 orbits, the system became increasingly chaotic. As a general trend, it was found that greater outer satellite eccentricities and inclinations resulted in a smaller change in inner satellite eccentricities and inclinations, respectively. When simulating 100 orbits with varying mass ratios, it was observed that as a satellite became relatively less massive, there was a greater variability in its final eccentricity. This is likely due to a combination of chaos and greater energy transfer relative to the less massive satellite. In addition to deterministic sweeps, the Portia group of Uranus’ moons was simulated due to its potential for close encounters. It was found that, on the order of 1000 years, close encounters frequently reached distances less than 0.5 of the mutual Hill’s radius of the moons, with the closest of these being encounters between Desdemona and either Juliet or Cressida.

column13: Evaluation of Carbon Fiber and Aerogel for Increased Dexterity in Firefighter Turnout Gloves
column14:
Conventional firefighter gloves rely on thick insulating layers for protection and studies have shown that they reduce manual dexterity by over 50% compared to bare hands during task performance (McLellan & Selkirk 2004). This significantly hinders critical fine motor tasks such as tying knots and tool utilization in emergency operations. Redesigning the glove composition to incorporate a carbon fiber layer within a fire-resistant fabric system can help solve this problem. The created design was tested for its ability to reduce heat transfer while retaining flexibility. Outcomes of this research contribute to the advancement of lighter, higher-dexterity protective gloves for improved firefighter performance in high-temperature environments.

A multilayer composite sample consisting of Nomex, aerogel fabric, and a carbon fiber weave was fabricated and compared to a control sample of the current generation glove construction. Both samples were exposed to a standardized enclosed heat source, and interior temperatures were recorded over time. Thermal performance was analyzed by comparing interior temperatures and rates of temperature rise between the samples.

Results indicate that the experimental composite sample exhibited faster heat dissipation than the control and a similar increase in interior temperature, demonstrating improved thermal resistance. Additionally, qualitative flexibility testing suggested that the thin construction decreased stiffness. These findings suggest that this new formula for firefighter glove materials has the possibility of enhancing thermal protection without sacrificing dexterity, allowing for application to full turnout gear. Future work will involve optimizing layer thickness, conducting mechanical durability testing, and exploring scalable manufacturing methods for full glove prototypes.

column13: Investigating The Viability of In Situ Resource Utilization for Moon Habitation Construction
column14:
Transporting construction materials from Earth to the Moon is extremely costly, averaging approximately $10,000 per kilogram, making traditional building methods impractical. However, as NASA’s Artemis missions aim to establish a long-term human presence on the Moon, there is a critical need for sustained lunar habitats constructed using in situ resource utilization (ISRU). ISRU uses rocks and debris called regolith already present on the Moon; this project investigates the feasibility of 3D printing structural components using lunar regolith–based geopolymer concrete, eliminating the need to transport large quantities of materials.

A lunar south pole regolith simulant was combined with sodium hydroxide and sodium silicate alkaline activators, water, and polypropylene fibers to form a printable geopolymer concrete. Activator ratios of 1:3 and 2:5 sodium-silicate to sodium-hydroxide were tested to evaluate printability and mechanical performance. An Ender 3 V3 KE 3D printer was modified into a concrete extrusion system by replacing the filament nozzle with a lead-screw-driven plunger attached to a 60 mL syringe, enabling controlled printing of thick cement. Printing parameters were calibrated using conventional concrete as a control.

Ring-shaped samples were printed, oven-cured at 60°C for 72 hours, and evaluated for dimensional consistency and compressive strength using a universal testing machine. The results of this study will compare the structural performance of different lunar concrete formulations and assess their practical use in additive manufacturing applications. This research demonstrates a scalable, low-cost approach to lunar construction and supports the potential of 3D-printed regolith-based materials for future planetary habitats.

column13: Limiting Battery Production by Storage of Variable Energy via Capillary-Fed Electrolysis
column14:
As the world continues to shift to renewable energy, the need for batteries to store variable energy has increased dramatically. As of 2025, battery production has hit an all-time high, and consequently, the greenhouse emissions from battery production has as well, accounting for approximately one million metric tons of carbon dioxide. Hydrogen, for many decades, has been used as a fuel source, which is then converted back into electricity via combustion or by the use of fuel cells. With a sufficiently efficient electrolytic cell, hydrogen could theoretically be produced to supplement batteries and therefore lower battery production.

Capillary-Fed Electrolysis (CFE) has been demonstrated to have ultra-high efficiencies for producing hydrogen — upwards of 98%. CFE cells have a zero-gap cell architecture which limits bubble formation, this vastly increases efficiency. Currently, CFE proves to be one of the most efficient means of production of hydrogen, beating all other types of electrolytic cells, like Photon Exchange Membrane (PEM) cells, and other industrial processes like steam methane reforming. A CFE cell will be manufactured and tested on efficiency and cost to test for its viability to supplement batteries.

Although CFE is proven to have high efficiencies, the rate and cost of production for CFE is most likely insufficient for supplementing batteries for storage of variable energy. These findings indicate that future research into the cost reduction and durability of these cells could potentially enable their deployment to supplement batteries in the push for renewable energy.

column13: Investigating Bacteria Incorporation into Concrete as a Potential Self-Healing Mechanism for Crack Restoration
column14:
Concrete is one of the most widely used construction materials, but it commonly develops cracks that reduce safety, increase maintenance costs, and contribute to environmental harm. This project explored the development of self-healing concrete by incorporating Bacillus megaterium, a bacterium capable of producing calcium carbonate when activated by moisture. The purpose of the experiment was to determine whether bacteria-infused concrete could autonomously repair cracks, improve durability, and provide a more sustainable alternative to traditional concrete in real-world infrastructure such as bridges, roads, and buildings.

The methodology involves cultivating Bacillus megaterium in nutrient broth, harvesting bacterial spores, and mixing them into concrete alongside a calcium-based nutrient source. A total of sixteen concrete specimens are to be cast, including both bacterial and non-bacterial control samples in cylindrical and slab molds. After curing, controlled cracks will be introduced using a hydraulic press. The cracked bacterial specimens are then exposed to water and warmth to activate the bacteria, and all samples are to be retested after a 56-day healing period.

So far we have collected 5 samples of bacteria in 30ml tubes which we have confirmed to have bacterial growth. This gives us 110ml of bacteria suspended in nutrient broth. We are incorporating the bacteria at 1-2% of the total concrete volume, which is expected to yield a strength increase of 10-30% and give the concrete properties of self-repair.

Extensions to the project include testing performance under varying environments, and investigating different methods to enhance bacterial longevity and scalability for large scale infrastructure applications.

column13: Implementing a Digital Design Framework to Reduce the Embodied Carbon of Transitional Humanitarian Shelters
column14:
Transitional humanitarian shelters, structures erected after the lifespan of emergency shelters yet before permanent rebuilding, are essential for disaster response but traditionally carry high embodied carbon from material production and transport. Implementing a climate and local skill-centered digital design methodology (CLCD) reduces embodied carbon in transition shelters for flood-prone regions, using Bangladesh’s Haor region as a case study.
A shelter was digitally prototyped in Autodesk Revit using Building Information Modeling (BIM). The 18 m² design utilizes locally sourced, low-carbon materials: homogenized bamboo (Bambusa vulgaris) for walls and thatch for the roof. A carbon life cycle assessment (LCA) was conducted to quantify the cradle-to-gate embodied carbon, accounting for biogenic carbon storage. Results were compared to conventional imported prefabricated and timber shelters.
The LCA found the bamboo-thatch shelter acts as a strong carbon sink at construction, storing approximately -2,260 kg CO2e when sourced locally. Even with a conservative end-of-life assumption, its full lifecycle impact is near-neutral, a reduction of over 2,000 kg CO2e compared to a typical prefabricated steel shelter. This demonstrates that locally sourced bio-based materials can dramatically lower embodied carbon in the transitional rebuilding process of post-disaster areas.
In conclusion, a CLCD process integrating BIM and LCA can effectively minimize the carbon footprint of transitional shelters. Going forward, completing thermal comfort simulations and iterating the design to extend the LCA to operational impacts, forming a replicable framework for sustainable humanitarian construction which can be applied to other case-study locations, will serve to prove CLCD viability for transitional shelters.

column13: Mechanizing Automatic Spray Pollination in Vertical Farming Systems
column14: Vertical farming is typically done in a greenhouse environment, rendering traditional pollination methods such as insect and natural wind pollination unviable for the plants. Forced to implement manual pollination, workers are hired to pollinate the blooming flowers by hand, typically with a paint brush. This is a labor-intensive method of pollination, that is associated with increased costs with having to pay for wages. The methodology of this research is building a prototype that mechanizes the wind pollination, making it able to be implemented in vertical farming environments. The mechanism should make the suck-and-release motion of a syringe bulb connected to an automatic motion that can be coded through a servomotor and Ardunio. The prototype has been created, with a total motion of 15 seconds to make the entire process of pollination.  Data will be collected by measuring the ejection the prototype provides for a pollen-like substance, like bee feed or flour, optimally 9-12mm away from the injection point. Final data collection will be done with self-pollinating cherry tomato plants in the blooming stage. The yield rate, whether the plant produces fruit, and the quality and weight of the fruit produced will be measured and compared with the control group of grocery store cherry tomatoes and vibration-pollinated fruit findings of a previous study. This senior project is done with the support of the Academies of Loudoun and mentored by Mr. Kitteleson.

column13: Optimizing Aero-Acoustic and Aerodynamic Characteristics of Toroidal Drone Propellers
column14:
Propeller-generated noise and efficiency losses present a significant design challenge across quadcopter applications in noise-sensitive applications. Conventional open-bladed propellers produce strong tip votives and unsteady loading that increases acoustic emissions. Furthermore, Grand View Research expects the drone industry to grow to over $182 billion by the year 2033. Toroidal propellers have been a proposed solution for mitigating these effects through a closed-loop geometry. A lack of quantified evaluations have prevented clear identification of optimal designs for real-world applications. Experimentally assessing the influence of geometric parameters governing toroidal propeller performance will improve flight conditions.
	Combined computational and empirical experimentation are employed throughout this study. A parametric grid of sealed toroidal propellers with varying blade angle of attack and outward inclination was designed using CAD and fabricated through additive manufacturing with resin. Transient CFD simulations incorporate a hybrid turbulence model and Ffowcs Williams-Hawkings acoustic analysis to predict aerodynamic behavior and preliminary aeroacoustic trends. Empirical testing was conducted using a thrust stand with calibrated load cells and sensors, alongside sound pressure level measurements in a semi-anechoic environment.
	Analyzing thrust curves and SPL data reveals clear relationships between geometry, aerodynamic efficiency, and aeroacoustic output. Several configurations demonstrated reduced tonal noise while maintaining thrust comparable to baseline designs, while extreme geometric variations resulted in diminished efficiency and increased flow unsteadiness. The study suggests that toroidal propeller performance encapsulates a multitude of various application-specific regions for further targeting. Future research will expand the parameter space, incorporate higher-fidelity acoustic measurements, and evaluate performance under dynamic flight conditions.

column13: Prescribing Pesto: Preventing Degenerative Motor Function in Parkinson's Patients with Basil
column14: Parkinson’s disease is a devastating chronic neurodegenerative disorder characterized by symptoms such as impaired motor function and cognitive decline. Already affecting millions, Parkinson’s is the fastest growing neurological disorder in the world, particularly in low income countries. The dopaminergic neuron loss associated with Parkinson’s has been linked with neuroinflammation and oxidative stress, findings which have influenced the search for potential treatments. Ociumum basilicum, commonly known as sweet basil, is an herb that has been used in traditional medicine systems across Eurasia for millennia. Compounds (such as phenolic acids and flavonoids) found in basil have been associated with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. An MPTP-induced Parkinson’s model of Drosophila melanogaster was chosen as a model organism, as they are known for short lifespans and preservation of human disease genes. It was hypothesized that, due to the previously described properties, sweet basil would have an ameliorative effect on the motor dysfunction of a fly model of Parkinson’s, as tested by a negative geotaxis assay. Four groups of D. melanogaster were prepared in order to test the effectiveness of basil on the Parkinson’s model: an experimental group raised on basil and exposed to MPTP, a control raised on basil and not exposed to MPTP, and two controls raised on normal fly medium, one exposed to MPTP and one not. Current data is preliminary, but trends show potential error in the MPTP mechanism, as the affected controls do not show significant motor dysfunction compared to the groups raised on normal fly medium. Conclusions are forthcoming.

column13: Pain to Positivity; Post-Injury Healing and Cell Wall Strength of Sunflower (Helianthus annuus) Wounds from Indole 3-Butyric Acid and Snail Mucin Application
column14:
Sunflowers are important in agriculture for producing oil and seeds with an approximate industry of over 32 billion. Snails and slugs are major pests causing over 1.3 billion in annual losses. The goal of the experiment is to determine if compounds applied on sunflowers can activate the natural defence prior to actual herbivore attack. The experiment measured the effect of Indole-3-butyric acid (IBA) and snail mucin (from Lymnaea stagnalis) on mammoth sunflowers (Helianthus annuus). Four variables were used: IBA, Mucin, IBA+Mucin, and nothing (control). Sunflower plants were wounded, exposed to variables, then measured for healing and stress.   IBA+Mucin was hypothesized to result in most improved healing and reduced stress. Healing was measured by wound dimensions. Larger dimensions indicating poor healing. IBA+Mucin had the most increase in wound healing averaging 47% and 56.67% wound width and depth contraction, respectively. Meanwhile, IBA and Mucin variables performed poorly. Mucin performed worst with an 75% and 137.288% increase in wound depth and wound width, respectively. Plant stress was measured using chlorophyll fluorescence (Ft), where decreases indicate higher stress.  Over 16 days, all treatments showed increased fluorescence except the control, which decreased. IBA had the highest increase ( 47.88%), followed by mucin (27.12%), and IBA+mucin (26.94%).
 These results suggest a trade-off: the IBA+Mucin improved healing but was less effective at reducing stress, while individual treatments reduced stress more but healed wounds less effectively.

column13: The Effect of Epicatechin on Long Term Memory in Drosophila melanogaster Expressing the HTT Exon 1-Q93 gene
column14: Huntington's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by a CAG repeat in the HTT gene that affects motor  and cognitive function. This project investigated whether epicatechin, a natural flavonoid found in cocoa and tea, could improve long term memory in Drosophila melanogaster expressing a human huntingtin protein fragment. Drosophila were used in this research because many gene, cellular stress pathways, and learning processes are conserved across species. Although Drosophila does not fully replicate human Huntington’s disease, their shared oxidative stress pathways and memory structures, such as the mushroom body, make them a valuable model for studying human neurodegenerative disease. Epicatechin has the potential to improve memory because its hydroxyl groups allow it to neutralize reactive oxygen species, reducing oxidative stress caused by Huntington's disease that damages neurons and contributes to cognitive decline. Flies were raised on food containing 0, 2, 6, or 10 µL/mL of epicatechin and tested using an Aversive Phototaxis System to measure memory retention after training. Trials containing the control flies have been completed, while HTT expressing fly trials are still ongoing. Driver line data were analyzed using a Kruskal-Wallis test, which suggests that memory performance differed between non-treatment groups and treatment groups. 6 µL/mL and 10 µL/mL groups showed the highest memory scores, suggesting that moderate to higher epicatechin exposure may support long term memory compared to lower epicatechin treatment. Overall, these preliminary results support further investigation into epicatechin as a potential dietary compound for reducing Huntington’s disease related cognitive decline.

column13: The Effects of Santalum album and Cedrus atlantica Essential Oils on Stress Behavior in Drosophila melanogaster
column14: Essential oils are used for stress reduction; however, their biological effects are not fully understood. This study investigated whether a 1:1 mixture of Santalum album (sandalwood) and Cedrus atlantica (cedarwood) essential oils alters stress-related behavior in Drosophila melanogaster, a model organism with an olfactory system (odors are processed by circuits in the brain that influence stress-related behavior and connect smell to brain regions involved in stress), similar to olfaction in humans. Flies were assigned to four groups under two stress conditions (heat shock and oxygen deprivation): negative control (no stress, no oil), positive control (stress, no oil), scent control (no stress, oil), and experimental (stress, oil). Flies were pre-exposed to essential oils for 15 minutes before stress exposure. Heat shock involved 10 minutes at 36゜Celsius, while oxygen deprivation involved 15 minutes in an anoxia chamber. Behavior was recorded in an arena and stress response was measured by counting center entries, indicating reduced anxious wall-following behavior. Under heat shock, flies exposed to essential oils showed higher center entries than stressed flies without oil, indicating reduced stress-related behavior. A Kruskal-Wallis test confirmed significant differences among all 4 treatment groups, indicating that treatment type affected behavior. This suggests that essential oil exposure reduces stress-related behavior. Oxygen deprivation trials showed a similar pattern, with oil-exposed flies demonstrating increased center entries compared to stressed controls. These findings suggest that S. album and C. atlantica essential oils may reduce stress-related behavior in D. melanogaster, providing evidence that aromatherapy could influence stress-related neural processes across species.

column13: Getting A grip; Magnesium Carbonate on Galleria Mellonella For Wound Healing
column14: Gymnasts and rock climbers frequently endured hand injuries; recurring callus tears were persistent, painful, and often necessitated prolonged recovery periods. A common variable among athletes experiencing these dermal tears was the constant application of MgCO3. While some forms of magnesium were known to help cells grow and heal, there was not much research out there on what actually happened when insoluble MgCO3 chalk got directly into open wounds. This study looked into how MgCO3 affected skin regeneration by using Galleria mellonella (Greater wax moth) larvae as a skin proxy for their fundamental skin healing pathways. After running a toxicity test to make sure the treatments were safe, the larvae were placed in a cold environment at 4 C to safely slow them down. A standardized cut was then made on each insect. The cuts were treated with three differing concentrations of MgCO3 mixed into olive oil, while two control groups received plain olive oil and no treatment. To see how well they were recovering, I visually tracked how fast the wounds darkened, a natural process called melanization that happened when their cells gathered at the cut, and watched for the outer skin to rebuild. Data collection was completed. A Chi-squared test was conducted to determine if there were statistically significant differences in healing outcomes between the treatment cohorts and the control. Understanding this relationship provided crucial insight into athletic wound care and whether standard gym chalk applications inhibited or supported the natural tissue regeneration process.

column13: Predicting X-Class Solar Flares Using Hourly Sunspot Area Changes
column14: This study investigated whether sunspot data can improve the prediction of extreme solar events, specifically X-class solar flares. Solar flares cause satellite disruptions, radio blackouts, power grid fluctuations and more. Larger flares, such as X-class flares, have more devastating and longer lasting effects. Traditional forecasting models rely on daily or weekly observations of sunspot characteristics, which may overlook rapid changes that occur within shorter time intervals. This research explores the use of hourly sunspot area to determine whether more frequent measurements improve predictive accuracy. A machine learning approach was used to analyze historical solar data from  the NOAA. A timespan of eleven years, the length of a solar cycle, was used to train the models. Multiple models, including Random Forest and LSTM, were trained and evaluated to compare performance between hourly versus daily datasets. Random Forest builds decision trees whose outputs are combined while LSTM is a neural network designed to learn long-term dependencies in sequential data. Metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score were used to evaluate each model’s effectiveness. An accuracy of 0.977 was found for Random Forest and 0.895 for LSTM. The results of this study aimed to determine whether adding hourly solar flare data resulted in better predictions. Improved forecasting of solar flares has important implications for space weather monitoring, since these events can affect important systems on Earth. By comparing daily and hourly approaches, this research adds to space weather prediction and shows the potential of machine learning in improving solar activity prediction.

column13: Early Warning Prediction of Zooxanthellae Expulsion From Coral Systems Using Multimodal Deep Learning
column14: Corals contribute $150 billion to various industries while housing 25% of marine species and serving as a keystone species. Zooxanthellae expulsion weakens corals by disrupting symbiotic algal relationships supporting coral energy production and algal habitats. Current models have been unable to predict coral health degradation until an event has already occurred. An early warning model estimating zooxanthellae expulsion serves as a potential way to take preventative measures at an earlier stage. A time series was trained on several photophysiological variables from the South China Sea’s coral systems. Satellite drivers were mapped along corresponding time frames to build a CNN. The model predicted a continuous expulsion stress score ranging from 0-1 on a 28 day time horizon. Training consisted of a 70/20/10 split with probabilistic calibration applied to improve reliability of the predicted stress scores. Exploratory analysis supported zooxanthellae covarying with multiple environmental predictors such as thermal stress and reduced dissolved oxygen to varying levels. Clear seasonal cycles were prevalent in zooxanthellae density throughout the year. The trained RNN outperformed the persistence baseline (ROC-AUC = 0.851, PR-AUC = 0.843) with ROC-AUC = 0.906 and PR-AUC = 0.897. DIIs (equation for water column correction) were used to preprocess CNN data, with the architecture of the CNN and concatenation of networks is pending. Overall, the model aimed to establish a system to take early preventive action on coral health degradation. Future work could broaden the time-horizon of prediction and expand to a more generalizable framework to adapt to any coral system.

column13: Death By Doomscrolling: Researching if our Unhealthy Screen-time Habits Affect Future Generations
column14: This experiment aimed to investigate the transgenerational effects of extended blue-light exposure to better understand the long-term effects of escalating artificial blue-light exposure. Drosophila melanogaster were used as a model organism as they have a short lifespan and react to light in a similar way to humans. As technology develops and more people are exposed to blue light for longer periods of time, it is crucial that we investigate the potential negative impact blue-light could have on future generations. Past research has shown that blue-light exposure causes stress and has effects on the circadian rhythm in humans and causes accelerated aging and increased locomotion in D. melanogaster. Two groups of 3 vials of flies were tested: one group exposed to 24 hours of darkness, the other exposed to 12 hours of blue light and 12 hours of darkness. Using a startle induced negative geotaxis (SING) assay, locomotor performance and muscle degeneration in the parental generation and unexposed offspring were quantified. It was observed that flies parented by flies exposed to blue light radiation took longer to develop and had three times as much decreased locomotor performance upon reaching adulthood. This has distinct implications for the growing exposure to blue light and suggests that modern screen time habits extend beyond the individual and that our current influx of screen time could impact the development of future generations.

column13: Flora vs. Flame Retardant: Mitigating the Impact of TBBPA on Associative Memory Using Bacopa Monnieri
column14: Tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA) is a brominated flame retardant found in a variety of electronic equipment including televisions, computer boards, phones, etc. Due to its growing presence, fetal exposure to TBBPA can occur through the placenta, umbilical cord, and consumption of breast milk. This is a pressing problem as TBBPA has been shown to harm neurodevelopment (e.g. memory) and increase AcHE activity. Bacopa monnieri (BM) is a nootropic herb which has been shown to decrease AcHE activity which generally correlates to improved associative learning and memory capabilities. This experiment sought to investigate the effect of various concentrations of BM in mitigating the effects of TBBPA on AcHE activity and associative memory capabilities using Drosophila Melanogaster. To conduct this experiment, flies were tested with an aversive phototaxis suppression (APS) and AChE assay before and 6 days after an exposure to treatment food. This process was repeated for 3 trials. The data was analyzed using a Kruskal Wallis test and a Sigma Aldrich CS0003 calculator which constructed a kinetic curve that, with minor calculations, revealed AChE activity. The results of this experiment sought to understand whether BM serves as an effective treatment to combat TBBPA exposure. This research adds to the growing field of neurotoxicology in regards to environmental toxicants and seeks to show how in addition to finding ways of limiting the spread, finding a treatment could be a potential way of combating these toxicants.

column13: Generalizability and Calibration of Deep Learning Models for PDAC Detection on Contrast-Enhanced CT
column14: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) carries one of the poorest prognoses among solid malignancies, underscoring the urgency of early detection. Deep learning models trained on CT imaging have shown promise for early PDAC detection, yet their generalizability to unseen institutions remains poorly characterized, limiting utility for early diagnosis across clinical settings. This study investigates whether a single-institution 2.5D ResNet18 model trained on CE-CT imaging can generalize via zero-shot external validation, and whether preprocessing interventions reduce the cross-institutional domain gap without retraining. The model was trained on ~180 scans from CPTAC-PDA and NIH Pancreas-CT and externally validated on ~420 scans from the MSD Pancreas Tumor dataset under three conditions: no adaptation, input harmonization using CPTAC source-domain statistics and histogram landmark matching, and post-hoc calibration via temperature scaling to correct anticipated model overconfidence on out-of-distribution data. Performance was assessed using ROC-AUC, sensitivity/specificity, and Brier/Expected Calibration Error scores. Internal validation yielded AUC 1.0, 96.4% accuracy on the held-out validation set, and 98.2% on the internal test set, reflecting strong within-distribution discrimination. External validation is expected to decline substantially, as the model likely learned acquisition-level domain features rather than generalizable cancer morphology. Input harmonization is expected to partially recover external performance by reducing Hounsfield Unit (HU)-level distributional shift, while temperature scaling is expected to improve calibration without altering rank ordering. These findings aim to quantify the cross-institutional performance gap in PDAC detection and evaluate whether training-free interventions can meaningfully reduce it, with implications for deployment and multicenter generalizability.

column13: The Lasting Effect of Loneliness:  Early Social Isolation Disrupts Stress Recovery
column14: Social environments can affect stress levels, but the relationship between early-life isolation and the effectiveness of social behavior in stress recovery, social buffering, is unclear. This study tested how developmental isolation and stress exposure affect social buffering in Drosophila melanogaster. It also tested whether 5-hydroxytryptophan (5-HTP), a supplement shown to increase social behavior, could reverse these effects. The hypothesis was that stressed flies isolated during development would show slower recovery post-stress, while 5-HTP would partially restore recovery. Stress was quantified using a climbing assay. Larvae were assigned to four groups: A grouped, B grouped stressed, C isolated stressed, and D isolated stressed +HTP. Post-eclosion, a baseline climbing assay measured initial stress level. Flies in stress groups were exposed to repeated mechanical vibration during class periods for one week. A second climbing assay measured the immediate effect of stress. Isolated flies were then grouped, and 5-HTP was added to the treatment group’s food. After 48 hours, a final climbing assay measured recovery. Data collection is ongoing, but preliminary results showed B was stressed 14% compared to A and showed 0% recovery. C flies showed 18% stress and -43% recovery, while D showed 0% recovery. Preliminary results suggest that, although grouped controls showed no recovery, developmentally isolated flies showed significantly more stress after grouping, but 5-HTP prevented this increase. These findings suggest that developmental environments and chemical treatments affect social buffering, meaning social buffering should only be used selectively, and 5-HTP could be used to restore buffering effectiveness when impaired by isolation.

column13: Apigenin Adjunct: A Means for Neuronic Recovery Following Traumatic Brain Injury
column14: Traumatic brain injury, or TBI, is a leading cause of death and disability in the United States, caused by falls or accidents involving head trauma. Survivors of such a high collision impact to the head often do not lead normal lives afterwards due to brain damage hindering the victims mental processing. Currently, no treatment exists to reverse or alleviate the effects of a TBI, such as motor function and speech, and improvement can only be achieved through speech and physical therapy. Apigenin, with the chemical composition C15H10O5, and commonly found in leafy greens, is a promising solution to combat the degenerative effects of TBI due to the chemical containing antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that may aid neuronic recovery. This experiment utilized Drosophila melanogaster, or the common fruit fly, due to the similar neurotransmitters and neural paths between the fly and human brain. A counterweight pulley vertical acceleration model induced TBI in D. melanogaster. The injured flies were then fed fly food infused with varying concentrations of Apigenin: 10 µM, 20 µM, 40 µM. Experimental groups were compared against control groups: flies with TBI and no treatment, flies with no TBI and no treatment, and flies with no TBI and treatment. Neuronic recovery was measured by the percentage of flies who chose a behaviorally disliked environment through an APS assay, analyzed through a non-parametric Kruskal Wallis test. Data collection is ongoing. This research could be a major step in advancing the treatment of brain injuries and preventing death caused by it.

column13: Breakthroughs in antibiotic resistance: How capped silver nanoparticles provide an intriguing alternative to antibiotics
column14: This project is investigating how different capping agents on silver nanoparticles influence bacterial quorum sensing gene regulation in Escherichia coli reporter strains. Quorum sensing is a cell-to-cell bacterial communication system that when it reaches a density threshold it regulates behaviors such as biofilm formation and virulence. Silver nanoparticles possess antimicrobial properties which can be used to combat the harmful behaviors of quorum sensing. This project examined whether silver nanoparticles capped with polyethylene glycol (PEG) or polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) have different effects on quorum sensing activity in E. coli reporter strains measured through luminescence. Silver nanoparticles were synthesized using PEG or PVA as capping agents with different concentrations for each (low: 1 microgram, medium: 10 micrograms, and high: 50 micrograms). E. coli reporter strains were exposed to controlled concentrations of each nanoparticle treatment alongside untreated control groups with no capping agents. Luminescence measurements were collected to evaluate changes in quorum sensing gene expression after exposure to the nanoparticles with capping agents. Data collection is still ongoing; however, results are expected to show that the capping agents significantly affect interactions between nanoparticles and bacteria and produce significant differences in luminescence compared to the control group. PEG-capped and PVA-capped nanoparticles will likely have notable levels of quorum sensing inhibition compared to the control. These findings may improve understanding of how nanoparticle especially capping agents affect bacterial communication and the results can help further boost the role of nanoparticles as alternatives to antibiotics and make it more prominent in medical fields.

column13: Language Learning Browser Extension
column14:
This project is a browser-based language learning extension that promotes contextual vocabulary learning during everyday web browsing. Inspired by existing tools such as Babbel’s Toucan, the prototype was developed as a Chrome extension using Manifest V3, JavaScript, HTML, and CSS. The system scans webpage text, identifies predefined English words, and replaces a subset with their French equivalents using a static bilingual dictionary. Replaced words are highlighted and display the original English term through when hovered over with a tooltip, allowing passive learning without interrupting reading flow.
The prototype was tested on a locally hosted website to evaluate the functionality, accuracy, and user-friendliness in a controlled environment. Results showed a word replacement accuracy of 100%, with a very low impact on page structure or performance. 
These results indicate that the browser extension can effectively pull language learning into digital environments. While the current model is limited by a static dictionary and no language processing, it shows the feasibility of passive learning through modifying text in real time. Future development could include grammatical processing, adaptive difficulty, and dynamic translations to enhance scalability and effectiveness.

column13: Multithreading and Memory Optimization fpr Multi-Agent Pathfinding
column14: This paper attempts to demonstrate the effectiveness of aggressive memory-pruning techniques in improving the performance of parallel processing for the multi-agent pathfinding (MAPF) problem. MAPF is a variation of pathfinding, a problem in which an algorithm is given a map of nodes and must find a route via edge connections from a start node to an end node; MAPF specifically attempts to coordinate multiple such paths to ensure that no two agents occupy the same position at the same time, or ‘crash’. Parallel processing has been shown to be effective for improving the performance of standard pathfinding algorithms, with the caveat that memory limitations often bottleneck the amount of parallel processing possible. In order to remedy that, this paper examines the technique of aggressively pruning any stored path sets which the program lacks the memory to actively process. The CBS algorithm described by Sharon et al. was used for testing: a standard implementation of the algorithm was run ten times each on five map configurations, and the algorithm’s times to complete these maps were compared against those of a modified version of the algorithms with the memory-pruning enhancements described in this paper. An average speedup/slowdown was calculated over all trials.

column13: Tennis Form Analysis Algorithm
column14: The project is an algorithm that analyzes and evaluates the form of a tennis stroke and provides feedback to help improve it. It is intended both to make advanced tennis more accessible by providing free personalized instruction and to enable tennis instructors to give feedback more easily by giving a clear picture of the player’s form. The project as it stands can do this for the slice serve. To use the project, the player films themselves doing a slice serve, then inputs the video into the program. The program uses a computer vision model to numerically describe the player’s motion, then the algorithm itself analyzes the motion and provides individual and overall scores as well as verbal feedback using a large language model. For the slice serve, form evaluation is based on the player’s trophy position, the frame of which the algorithm identifies. The project produces higher scores for slice serves performed by more skilled players. The scores for individual aspects of the player’s form as well as the feedback assembled by the LLM are useful. The project has been developed in such a way that it can be expanded to encompass additional strokes.

column13: CRYSTALCARE: Discovering Optimized Nanocrystal Framework Drug Delivery Systems For Universal Delivery Applications
column14: Over 90% of promising drug candidates fail FDA approval, primarily due to poor absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME) properties, not lack of efficacy. Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs), crystalline materials with surface areas exceeding 7,000 m²/g, can rescue failed drugs through targeted encapsulation. However, with 100,000+ known MOF structures, resource constraints previously demanded arbitrary experimentation. We developed CRYSTALCARE, a graph attention neural network that predicts optimal MOF-drug pairings by learning atom-level interactions between drug molecules and MOF frameworks. Unlike descriptor-based methods, CRYSTALCARE employs bidirectional cross-attention to identify chemically-meaningful binding patterns. The model was trained on multi-fidelity labels combining experimental literature data (n=127 pairs), molecular docking (n=4,850), and physics-informed heuristics (n=52,000) to predict improvements in ADME properties. CRYSTALCARE achieved R²=0.75 for ADME prediction and 92% accuracy for drug loading capacity. Cross-attention mechanisms provided 15% performance gain over baselines, with void fraction (28% SHAP importance) and pore diameter (22%) identified as key features. The model screens 10,000 MOFs in 2 hours, a 1,095× speedup versus traditional simulations. We synthesized top-ranked MOFs for the drugs SN-38, doxorubicin, and verteporfin via a UARM process and confirmed predictions (r=0.79 correlation). CRYSTALCARE-identified MOFs experimentally demonstrated 2.5× higher drug release (47.3% vs. 18.6%, p<0.01) and 2.7× loading efficiency (82.4% vs. 31.2%, p<0.001). CRYSTALCARE has identified optimized delivery systems for 40+ failed drugs across 28 diseases, representing $12 billion in pharmaceutical development.

column13: Comparing Efficacy of Bromelain in Electrospun Fibers and Hydrogels for Burn Wound Treatment in the Invertebrate Burn Wound Model of Galleria mellonella Larvae
column14: Burn wound infection is both a main clinical complication and leading cause of mortality among burn wound patients. Bromelain, an enzyme derived from pineapple, has been established as an effective topical treatment for burns due to its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and anti-edema properties. However, application of the technology mandates use of occlusive dressing for hours, which heightens the risk of infection and can be time-consuming and inefficient. Chitosan, an antimicrobial and antibacterial polymer, can be electrospun to form bandage-like sheets. By comparing the effects of chitosan-based treatments of electrospun nanofibers and hydrogels containing bromelain on the invertebrate burn wound model of Galleria mellonella larva (a model comparable to mammalian models), a more effective delivery system utilizing the effective enzyme can be determined. The electrospun fibers and hydrogels were tested on the larvae, monitoring their progression in terms of mortality once Bacillus cereus bacteria, a common burn infector, was inoculated onto the burn. A log-rank mortality statistical test was utilized to determine the difference in mortality rates between the delivering methods, which showed a statistically significant difference between the fiber and hydrogel treatments (p < 0.05). Further, an antibacterial assay demonstrated that the chitosan-bromelain fiber showed greater antibacterial resistance to B. cereus bacteria than the hydrogel of chitosan-bromelain. This study provides evidence that the most effective delivery method of bromelain which best improves treatment for burn wound care is electrospun nanofibers of chitosan, predicted to be due to the similarity of the fiber’s structure to the extracellular matrix.

column13: Determining the Ideal Substrate Composition of Cassava Peelings, Coconut Coir, and Rice Husks in Substrates to Increase Yield Strength and Mechanical Properties of Ganoderma Lucidum Mycelium Bricks
column14: With rapidly growing populations and pollution, sub-Saharan African countries are in need of cheap and environmentally-friendly alternatives to traditional construction materials. Mycelium bricks have potential in temporary construction applications; they are made of low-cost agricultural waste substrates inoculated by fungi. Common agriculture waste products in sub-Saharan Africa are sugarcane bagasse (fibrous remains of sugarcane), coconut coir, and rice husks (inedible rice byproducts). Mycelium bricks are significantly weaker than their traditional counterparts (ex. concrete, cement), potentially making them obsolete in housing or building projects as suggested. By testing varying weight concentrations of bagasse, coconut coir, and rice husks in mycelium brick substrate mixes, the goal was to maximize their potential yield strength while maintaining simple growing protocols for their wider adoption in rural sub-Saharan Africa. The following concentrations were tested: 60% rice husks, 20% bagasse, 20% coconut coir (rice husk heavy); 60% bagasse, 20% coconut coir, 20% rice husks (bagasse heavy); 60% coconut coir, 20% bagasse, 20% rice husks (coconut coir heavy). It was hypothesized that substrates of a higher weight concentration of rice husks relative to other substrates will yield bricks of highest yield strength due to the combined nutrient properties.

column13: Studying the Effects of Modifying Zeolite with Cationic Surfactant on the Removal of Acid Blue 25 Using Spectrophotometric Measurements
column14: Due to its prevalence in contaminated water and threats to humans and aquatic life, acid blue 25, an anionic pollutant dye, has been researched for removal (Ghodbane & Hamdaoui, 2009). Recent research has proposed using cationic surfactant modification on zeolite to better remove anionic pollutants and dyes (Bagheri et al., 2024). This experiment contains the novel idea of testing the removal of acid blue 25 with cationic surfactant (CTAB) modified zeolite. The procedure included: zeolite synthesis using various non-carcinogenic chemicals, surfactant modification via submersion in a CTAB solution, batch adsorption tests to test the modified zeolite’s absorbance capabilities and longevity, and absorbance measurements with a UV Spectrovis for comparison against the untreated acid blue 25 solution; the control was unmodified zeolite, which had an identical procedure but with surfactant modification removed. After statistical analysis, it was concluded that: the modified zeolite successfully removed acid blue 25 for the first batch (p<0.05), the modified zeolite did not maintain successful removal for batch 2 (p>0.05), and all zeolite is mostly reusable (p=1 between all batch 1 and batch 2 data). However, the limited amount of zeolite used in the experiment due to chemical availability and cost could have influenced results, especially reusability.

column13: Eco-friendly Hydrogels: Comparing Ailanthus altissima Biochar Amounts for Copper Adsorption in Water
column14: This research strived to create innovative low cost hydrogels for copper adsorption. Copper is not biodegradable and an excess amount in water can cause diseases, but current copper removal methods are expensive and ineffective. A novel method involving adsorption with hydrogels is the forefront of this research, as they are highly regarded as possible adsorpters since they are inexpensive and can be formed from abundant polymers like sodium alginate and cellulose. Different additives can help enhance copper adsorption properties, and biochar has an abundance of functional groups to bond heavy metal. This research focused on seeing the effect of different amounts of Tree-of-Heaven (Ailanthus altissima) biochar with a 0:1, 1:4, and 1:2 ratios of biochar to sodium alginate in hydrogels. It was hypothesized that a ratio of 1:4 would be most effective because there would be an abundance of functional groups and no excessive biochar crowding the pores. Hydrogels were physically crosslinked with sodium alginate and calcium chloride. Swelling ratio was also assessed to see how well hydrogels expose the functional groups. FTIR analysis demonstrated that hydrogels with biochar had an abundance of functional groups. Data and statistical tests demonstrated that there is statistical evidence that the groups are different with 2:1 SA:B ratio outperforming the others. Adsorption capacity demonstrated that 4:1 adsorption was much more efficient. This shows that a biochar additive can be a useful additive but different ratios should be studied.

column13: The Economic Viability of Selective Hydrodeoxygenation of Vanillin Using Various Palladium-based Catalysts
column14:
The burning of fossil fuels in combustion engines is a primary driver of man-made climate change, so there has been significant interest in finding alternatives. Biofuels, derived from renewable sources, provide opportunities for minimal carbon dioxide emissions while being plentiful in nature. Hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) of bio-compounds can create suitable fuels in combustion engines with little change to infrastructure, but hydrogen gas and specialized equipment are usually used, which increase expenses significantly and pose a threat to expanding to an industrial-scale. Literature has shown that palladium-based catalysts; like Palladium on carbon and Palladium on alumina; have properties that make them viable catalysts in HDO with hydrogen gas. However, usage of hydrogen gas requires specialized equipment, so the aim of this research was to determine if palladium-based catalysts are viable in conjunction with a hydrogen-donor solvent, which would make applying HDO at an industrial scale significantly easier. 
During GC-MS analysis of a control sample before the reaction (without a catalyst), a control sample after the reaction, a Pd/C sample, and a Pd/AlO sample, it was found that vanillin was the dominant compound in the former two, while vanillin was converted into creosols in the latter two at a statistically significant rate compared to the former. Pd/AlO was found to have a statistically higher conversion yield rate of desired compounds, in particular creosols, than Pd/C. Subsequently, economic analysis and several Monte Carlo simulations confirmed that a hypothetical firm using the same methodology at scale could reasonably turn a profit.

column13: The Effect of Iron-doping TiO2, ZnO, and SnO2 on their Band-Gap Energies
column14: Band-gap energies are important because of their use in determining efficiencies of reactions related to water purification and manufacturing. However, research on this is very recent, so many studies focus themselves on specific processes unique to individual semiconductors. In this study, the overall effect of iron-doping was investigated, with three common semiconductors used in photocatalytic experiments, as in titanium dioxide, tin dioxide, and zinc oxide, being tested. This was done through synthesis of iron-doped materials, and then the comparison of those materials with its original composition through absorption spectroscopy. This was then analyzed using a Tauc equation to get the band-gap energy. Overall, there was a significant difference between band-gap when iron-doping was implemented as compared to when it was not. The effect appeared to be consistent, with iron-doping providing a similar effect across different semiconductors. This allows for iron-doping to be used as a method to improve reaction’s efficiency, costs, and sustainability.

column13: Determining the Optimal Ratio of Fe2+:Fe3+ For a Magnetically Responsive and Electroactive Hydrogel With ROS Scavenging Capabilities
column14: Atrial fibrillation (AFib) is a dangerous arrhythmia associated with elevated risk of stroke and heart failure. AFib is associated with high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are reactive particles responsible for oxidative stress. AFib is further associated with blocked and decreased conduction in the heart, causing irregular heartbeats. An electroactive, magnetically controlled hydrogel with ROS scavenging capabilities could address these issues and work as an effective treatment for AFib. The purpose of this experiment was to find the ratio of Fe2+/Fe3+ that optimizes the abilities of such a hydrogel. A series of pyrrole/polyvinyl alcohol magnetic hydrogels were synthesized, each with a different ratio of Fe2+/Fe3+. They were all tested for magnetism with a neodymium magnet. Their respective electroactivities were analyzed by measuring their resistance with a multimeter. Their ROS control was measured by testing how much H2O2 they absorbed and then deposited in a solution of KMnO4, which was then checked for absorbance to see how much H2O2 was present. With these results, we hope to further knowledge about AFib treatment and hydrogel synthesis.

column13: Determining the Optimal Concentrations of Supplemental Acids When Anodizing Aluminum Alloy 2024 to Maximize Corrosive Resistance
column14: Anodization is a process in which materials with a passive layer, such as titanium or aluminum, is subjected to voltage within an acidic electrolyte solution that changes the properties of the oxide layer, usually making the oxide layer thicker. This is usually done in order to improve the physical and chemical resistances of the material. The purpose of the research was to determine the concentrations of three different supplemental acids that, when combined in the electrolyte solution, would provide the best corrosive resistance when anodizing aluminum alloy 2024. Anodization was performed with a 1.5 cm x 1.5 cm surface of aluminum, and a base electrolyte solution containing 5 % sulfuric acid by weight, along with the addition of one of either acetic acid, citric acid, or tartaric acid, in a concentration of either 1 %, 3 %, or 5 % of the electrolyte solution’s total weight. A control trial was also performed with only sulfuric acid in the electrolyte solution. Anodization was performed with a voltage of 25 V for 30 minutes, after which the aluminum was sealed through hydrothermal sealing at 96 °C for 20 minutes. The resulting corrosion resistance was determined through a potentiodynamic polarization test in a solution of 3.5 % NaCl by weight. The test was performed using the Rodeostat potentiostat, and was analyzed using the EC Lab software. Corrosion potentials and corrosion currents were determined for all of the 10 total trials.

column13: Determining the Effective Temperature Range That Pestalotiopsis Microspora is Able to Degrade PET and Polyurethane For Bioremediation Purposes

column13: The Effects of a PFOB and Ringer’s Solution Emulsion as an Artificial Blood Substitute in Lumbricus terrestris via Coelomic Injection
column14: With only 3% of the world donating blood, blood shortages have become widespread globally, causing a growing interest in artificial blood substitutes. Perfluorocarbon (PFC), capable of dissolving large volumes of oxygen, has shown promise as a blood oxygen therapeutic. Ringer’s solution, composed of various salts, has been used as IV fluid. The effects of combining perfluorooctyl bromide (PFOB), a type of PFC, and Ringer’s solution at a ratio of 62% PFOB, 3% powdered soy lecithin, and 35% Ringer’s solution as an artificial blood substitute was tested in Lumbricus terrestris, the common earthworm, along with control groups of distilled water, emulsified PFOB, and pure Ringer’s solution. Earthworms were put into hypoxia using a hypoxia chamber constructed from an acrylic tank with constant flows of nitrogen gas and air to create an atmosphere of 10% oxygen. Each worm was injected with a solution amounting to 5% of their body mass. After 48 hours, hemolymph was extracted and lactate assays were conducted. Lactate concentrations were expected to decrease if the solution was effective, as higher internal oxygen levels should result in less frequent cellular respiration, and, subsequently, lower lactate concentrations. An ANOVA test yielded a p-value of 0.12, indicating there may be some difference in lactate concentrations between groups, but there is weak evidence suggesting the PFOB and Ringer’s Solution mixture effectively decrease lactate concentrations. However, it can be concluded that PFOB and Ringer’s Solution affects internal oxygen levels and may be a viable option for use as a blood oxygen therapeutic.

column13: SPECTRA: De Novo Broad-Spectrum Sepsis Inhibitor Design Across 27 Protease Targets via a Multi-Architecture Generative Adversarial Pipeline
column14:
Sepsis has a mortality rate of 40-60%, resulting in over 11 million lives lost annually. This process is driven by the simultaneous collapse of four proteolytic axes, including caspase apoptotic dysregulation, metalloproteinase tissue destruction, serine cascade amplification, and cysteine cytotoxicity, which results in multi-organ failure. No FDA-approved protease inhibitors for sepsis exist, and drug discovery is hindered by the inability to address the entire dysregulation simultaneously. This study presents the first multi-architecture generative adversarial (GAN) pipeline that is engineered to design, validate structures, and chemically optimize inhibitors for all 27 impacted sepsis proteases. The parallel GAN architectures (trained on 11,551 MEROPS protease substrate cleavage events) are the core of the pipeline. The model includes a Conditional GAN with protease-class embeddings, a WGAN trained using Wasserstein loss with a gradient penalty, and PrismGAN, a novel architecture that utilizes spectral normalization, self-attention for long-range amino acid placement, and conditional batch normalization. Candidates were processed through ESMFold 3D structure and AutoDock-Vina molecular docking in 781 simulations. The pipeline yielded 28 excellent binders (>10 kcal/mol) with a mean affinity of -7.25 kcal/mol (Conditional) and -7.25 kcal/mol (Prism). Equivalence testing (TOST, δ = 0.75 kcal/mol) confirmed the GAN protein sequences are equivalent to the biological MEROPS substrates (p_lower < 0.001, p_upper = 0.021). The 27-peptide panel spanned -4.93 to -11.88 kcal/mol, with a 67% favorable drug profile. Candidates were extended into three covalent warhead variants: aldehydes, boronic acids, and hydroxamates to form into an inhibitor.

column13: Nutsolver: Computing Optimal Poker Strategies
column14: This project presents the design and implementation of a solver that calculates the game theory optimal strategy in No-Limit Texas Hold’em Poker. Unlike perfect information games like chess where everything about the current game state is known, the two cards of the opponent are hidden in poker. However, a Nash equilibrium spot is known to exist when there are two players, meaning that there is an optimal, unexploitable strategy of playing. The goal of this project is to develop an open-source solver that can accurately and efficiently calculate said strategy. Poker can be represented as a game tree, where each node contains information on the current gamestate, and a player’s strategy in a given spot. In each leaf node, a player wins the pot; from there, the expected value of each previous node can be calculated by backtracking. Chance-sampling Monte Carlo Counterfactual Regret Minimization (CFR) is used to calculate Nash equilibrium by running two agents against each other that aim to exploit each other’s strategy until equilibrium is reached. Currently, the solver is able to solve any spot in poker starting with five community cards. Given the stack size, pot size, board cards, player ranges, and bet/raise sizes, the solver is able to calculate the optimal Nash equilibrium strategy. Future enhancements include upgrading the solver so that it’s able to solve starting from earlier betting rounds, implementing other Monte Carlo CFR algorithms, and adding features like nodelocking to be able to better analyze exploitative strategies.

column13: Wildfire Risk Prediction Using Vegetation Stress in Australia
column14: Over the last several years, Australia has experienced a large amount of wildfires, losing 35% of its forests between 2016-2021. The problem is that current warning systems only react after fires start, instead of being more predictive. My research will explore whether we can predict the severity of fires where fires are most likely to rapidly spread by monitoring how stressed vegetation becomes before it burns. I plan to use satellite data to track vegetation health through indicators like VOD and soil moisture, which shows when plants are dry and fire-prone. By combining historical fire records with over two decades of satellite imagery, I hope to identify patterns that might predict the potential spread if a fire were to happen. Currently, my model is able to categorize fires into low, medium, or high severity with an accuracy of around 70%. My end goal is to build an interactive map that will show real-time vegetation stress and predicted fire zones, giving communities the chance to prepare before a major fire hits.

column13: Tessera: A Scalable Digital Twin for Climate-Resilient Cities
column14:
Cities worldwide face escalating climate risks, including extreme heat, flooding, and infrastructure stress, demanding tools that can proactively design resilient urban environments. Current urban digital twins often focus on single hazards or require extensive real-world data, limiting their usefulness for multi-hazard scenario planning in data-scarce regions. This project introduces Tessera, a planning-oriented digital twin framework that integrates Urban Heat Island (UHI) modeling, flood risk simulation, synthetic city generation, and Decision-Induced Urban Power Twins (DUPT) to create a virtual laboratory for climate policy and infrastructure planning. Tessera’s core UHI model uses a hybrid ConvGRU architecture that fuses dynamic climate data with static urban features, while its synthetic city pipeline enables intervention testing where detailed city data is unavailable. DUPT extends this framework by generating spatially plausible urban power-grid structures, allowing planners to evaluate how climate adaptation decisions interact with energy infrastructure.

Tessera demonstrates strong predictive and planning performance across global urban environments, achieving 0.80°C UHI MAE and 1.01°C UHI RMSE, along with 0.2565 flood MAE and 0.2877 flood RMSE across 34 cities. The DUPT component further shows promising infrastructure realism, with 52.1 ± 30.6% OSM alignment within 100 meters and an edge-length KS statistic of 1,109 ± 1,594. Together, these results position Tessera as a scalable decision-support system for stress-testing heat, flood, and power-grid interventions under future climate scenarios.

column13: Analyzing and Mitigating Bias in AI-Based Resume Screening Systems for Fair Hiring Outcomes
column14: Hiring processes have changed a lot over the years and are now increasingly using AI in processes. AI is often used to screen and shortlist resumes, and this is raising concerns about there potentially being bias in this form of decision-making. The main goal of this study is to look at whether these AI-based screening systems are producing unequal outcomes across groups of different demographics. Many synthetic resumes were created with controlled variations in demographic indicators, including names that were associated with certain genders or ethnicities. Then, these resumes were processed through different LLM-based resume screening systems to analyze the difference in scores based on a controlled rubric system. To address issues, a fairness-oriented tool was made to be used by small businesses and companies to screen resumes with more speed while also being equitable and improving fairness in hiring decision. This project shows how AI systems can be reinforcing existing inequalities that are present in society if they are not designed to look out for them. The findings and process emphasize the importance of having fairness and transparency in automated decision-making systems and show how blind trust can lead to less equitable recruitment practices.

column13: Determining the Effect of the Combined Treatment of Sulforaphane and Luteolin on Cell Viability and Apoptosis in 4T1 Cells
column14: Today, breast cancer is the second leading cause of cancer related deaths around the world. Breast cancer metastasis is specifically where breast cancer cells from the breast where they originate, to other parts of the body leading to organ failure. There are currently several methods of treating breast cancer such as radio therapy and immunotherapy, but the results of these methods are usually inconsistent. In order to look into a novel treatment that has potential to improve the prognosis of breast cancer patients, the drugs luteolin and sulforaphane are being combined to determine their efficacy. Both drugs are known for their anticancer properties and ability to cause cell death in breast cancer cells. The acid phosphatase and caspase-3/7 assays were used to measure cell viability and apoptotic effect, respectively. Data collection has been completed, and it has been determined that individually, both drugs are able to inhibit the necessary pathways in 4T1 cells in order to decrease cell viability (P<0.01). However, when combined, these treatments seemed to upregulate cell viability which was possibly caused due to intrinsic drug resistance. Yet, analysis on the combined treatments and their effects on apoptosis in cells is still ongoing. So far it can be concluded that while the drugs inhibit cell growth and viability when administered separately, they instead stimulate these factors when their combined treatments are added to cells meaning that they, in fact, do not have synergistic effects when combined.
