column13: Assessing the effectiveness of combined treatment of Gold Nanoparticles (AuNPs) and Fucoidan on induction of apoptosis in A549 lung cancer cells
column14: Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are commonly investigated when considering the usage of nanomaterials as cancer treatment. They may be used to encapsulate drugs, or induce individual cytotoxic effects such as oxidative stress. Fucoidan is a polysaccharide derived from brown seaweed, known to exhibit promising anticancer effects through targeting of the PI3k-AKT pathway. This project aims to determine the synergistic effects of AuNPs and Fucoidan through induction of apoptosis in lung cancer cell line A549. A549 cells were subcultured and seeded at a density of 1 x 105 cells per well. Fucoidan solution was prepared at a concentration of 1mg/ml, and both Fucoidan and AuNPs were diluted in respective cell media at three concentrations (25, 50, 100 µg/ml) Controls studied the effect of each treatment concentration individually, and experimental rows included both solutions. After 48 hours of treatment, A Caspase 3/7 assay was run to quantify rates of apoptosis as a result of each treatment. Two trials have been conducted, and data collection is ongoing.

column13: Determining the Effect of Herbal Melanin Extract from Nigella sativa on Barrier Integrity and Inflammation in a Caco-2 Cell Model of Lichen Planus
column14: Lichen planus (LP) is a T-cell mediated chronic autoimmune condition that affects the skin barrier, causing itchy lesions on the skin and mouth area. Currently, the reason that cytotoxic T-cells attack the mucous membranes is unknown, leading to increased inflammation and membrane permeability. Present treatments include retinoids and corticosteroids which have inhibited the rapid release of proinflammatory cytokines, further reducing inflammation. Scientists have determined a positive correlation between MUC5AC, a mucin protein, and mucous membrane barrier integrity. The study at hand explores herbal melanin extract, derived from Nigella sativa, as a possible treatment to increase IL-1B mediated cytokine production to further increase MUC5AC production, improving barrier integrity. The herbal melanin was extracted via acid aggregation, alkali solubilization, and repeated centrifugation and filtration. For the Lucifer Yellow permeability assay, a 24-well transwell plate was seeded with Caco-2 cells and an LPS concentration of 1 μg/mL. Once a cell monolayer formed, the apical components of the transwell were treated with varying concentrations of melanin extract (1.25 μg/mL, 2.5 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL). The Lucifer Yellow dye was prepared and added to the apical components, and the fluorescence was measured. Data collection and analysis of the Lucifer Yellow permeability assay are still ongoing. Trials have thus far shown strong confidence that concentrations of melanin extract showed varied fluorescence readings, indicating that the treatment impacted barrier permeability. Further research using the Lucifer Yellow permeability assay will be conducted and analyzed producing more quantitative results.

column13: Investigating the expression of DEAD-box helicase 6 as a Therapeutic Target in Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia Cells
column14: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) is the most common childhood cancer. While therapies for the overactive cancerous white blood cells exist, relapse rates remain high: 50% for adults and 30% for children. A study done with Acute Myeloid Leukemia found that DEAD-box helicase 6 (DDX6) protein depletion increases in Thioredoxin-interacting protein (TXNIP), a tumour suppressor with key regulation in diabetes and inflammation.  To model the depletion of DDX6, siRNA reverse transfection was used, with DDX6 siRNA concentrations of 10nM, 20nM, 30nM added to an empty 96-well white plate. Reh cells (a model for ALL) were added on top and the well plate was incubated for 24-48 hours. Then an ATP Luminescence Assay was conducted, where Glo Titer Reagent was added to the wells and put under a plate reader. The light reading was quantifiably proportional to the cell viability. As expected, the cells with no siRNA had the highest cell viability with an average reading of 2800000 RLUs. The Kruskal-Wallis Test for the remaining treatments revealed no statistically significantly different luminescent readings (p>0.05). The treatment control had an average reading of 2300000 RLUs which brings up concerns for  Lipofectamine reactant toxicity for Reh cells, which have otherwise not been found. These findings present that Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia does not contain the same pathways between DDX6 and TXNIP as Acute Myeloid Leukemia and most likely DDX6 depletion is not a suitable therapeutic target for ALL.

column13: Ipomone as a Senolytic in C2C12 Cells
column14: Senescence is a state of permanent cell cycle arrest in which damaged cells cease replication but do not undergo programmed cell death (apoptosis), accumulating in the body over time. Cellular senescence is a major contributor to organismal aging, a complex biological process which significantly limits the human lifespan and increases the risk of organ failure, cancer, and dementia. Senescent cells resist apoptosis, the body’s natural clearing mechanism, creating a burden which cannot be cleared by the immune system. This problem can be alleviated through an emerging class of chemicals known as senolytics, which target senescent cells and induce apoptosis. Ipomone is a novel plant-derived diterpenoid compound which has been shown to induce cell death or apoptosis in vitro. Ipomone’s previous interactions involving senescence-associated biochemical pathways indicate potential as a senolytic. The compound was synthesized from gibberellic acid using an iodine-mediated process and purified by column chromatography. C2C12 murine myoblast cells were cultured and seeded in well plates, then treated with hydrogen peroxide (600µM) for 2 hours, creating a sub-lethal amount of oxidative stress to induce senescence. Treatment was removed and cells were left to recover for 48h, allowing the stable senescent phenotype to develop. The senescence marker β-galactosidase was measured using a colorimetric β-galactosidase staining reagent, then analyzed for absorbance at 405nm in a plate reader. Ipomone will be added to control and hydrogen peroxide treated cells at concentrations of 0, 100, 300, and 500 µg/mL. This treatment is hypothesized to reduce senescence as modeled by beta-galactosidase expression.

column13: Vocalize
column14: Current diagnostic methods for neurodegenerative diseases often rely on expensive, inaccessible equipment such as MRI machines or surveys by clinical professionals. As a result, equipment creates a significant barrier to early detection, specifically in resource-limited areas. Vocalize seeks to revolutionize early disease detection by transforming voice recordings into early detection through the use of machine learning. Recent findings show that human voice contains physiological and neurological biomarkers, information this project will utilize to develop a smartphone-based platform capable of detecting multiple diseases from short vocal samples. By leveraging public datasets including the Bridge2AI Voice dataset and implementing custom spectral subtraction audio processing pipelines to mitigate background noise, the system extracts acoustic features for training classical machine learning models and determining analysis of current cognitive risks. The system targets neurological conditions including Parkinson disease, Alzheimer disease, Dementia, and Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) through a unified 4-class detection pipeline. Initial model evaluation using stratified cross-validation demonstrates promising results, with AUC-ROC scores reaching 0.84 for an initial 3-class detection without MCI using multi-layer perceptron classifiers. The project includes a functioning smartphone prototype built with Swift, machine learning models implemented through TensorFlow, as well as comprehensive documentation. This research addresses the global need for accessible and early diagnosis tools by providing a scalable screening solution available on consumer smartphones.

column13: Housing Access Equity: Digital Solutions for Affordable Housing
column14:
Affordable housing is one of the most pressing challenges in today’s real estate market. Many people struggle to find accessible housing options, and while platforms like Zillow help users search for homes, they often fail to cater to low-income populations or provide localized data about affordability. My goal is to create a centralized real estate platform that connects individuals with affordable housing opportunities, helping them navigate zoning restrictions, eligibility criteria, and cost limitations that often block access to suitable homes. By bridging coding and database design with real-world housing data, this research may be able to spread from Washington, DC to underserving communities globally. Using data from HUD and local jurisdictions like Loudoun County or Montgomery County, this platform would generate location- based insights to better match users with realistic options. 

This research addresses how a centralized platform can improve access to affordable housing while exploring economic benefits of affordable renting. Key questions including how zoning and construction costs affect housing supply, what factors lead to homelessness, and what design or business elements make real estate platforms sustainable and effective is what is projected to answer.
