column13: Investigating the Protective Effects of CAHS-Inspired  Desiccation Methods on Enzyme Stability
column14:
Enzymes are critical to vaccines and biotechnology, yet they rapidly denature when exposed to heat or dryness, due to this refrigeration is needed which in turn increases costs and limits access in remote areas. Tardigrades survive extreme dehydration using intrinsically disordered CAHS proteins that form glass-like protective matrices. Similarly, disaccharides such as trehalose and sucrose stabilize proteins through vitrification, creating an amorphous solid state that preserves structure during desiccation. This study tested whether CAHS-inspired desiccation using these sugars preserves enzyme activity after drying. Chicken liver catalase (0.5 mg/mL) was used as a model enzyme due to its availability, sensitivity to desiccation, and simple assay. Samples were prepared in 50 mM potassium phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), with treatment groups receiving trehalose or sucrose and a control receiving buffer only (n = 6 per group). Samples (1 mL) were desiccated over silica gel at 25°C for 72 hours until constant mass, then rehydrated for 30 minutes. Activity was measured by adding 0.1 mL sample to 5 mL of 0.5% H₂O₂ and recording bubble height after 2 minutes. Percent activity was calculated relative to a fresh control (≈ 11.5 mm). Trehalose with slow drying retained the highest activity (7.68 ± 1.03 mm, 67%), followed by sucrose (57%), trehalose with rapid drying (58%), and no protectant (46%). These results show that trehalose, especially with slow desiccation, best preserves enzyme function, supporting room-temperature storage applications in biotechnology.
Boothby, T. C., Tapia, H., Brozena, A. H., Piszkiewicz, S., Smith, A. E., Giovannini, I., ... Goldstein, B. (2017). Tardigrades use intrinsically disordered proteins to survive desiccation. Molecular Cell, 65(6), 975–984. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2017.02.018
Crowe, J. H., Carpenter, J. F., & Crowe, L. M. (1998). The role of vitrification in anhydrobiosis. Annual Review of Physiology, 60(1), 73–103. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev.physiol.60.1.73
Hesgrove, C., & Boothby, T. C. (2020). The biology of tardigrade disordered proteins in extreme stress tolerance. Cell Communication and Signaling, 18(1), Article 53. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12964-020-00670-2

column13: The Investigation of Dietary Modulation of PI3K-Driven Overgrowth Phenotypes in Drosophila melanogaster
column14: Hemihyperplasia is a rare overgrowth syndrome that affects approximately 0.0012% of the population, with individuals facing physical asymmetry, potential organ complications, increased tumor risk, and related medical and psychological challenges. Overgrowth syndromes are rare and understudied, characterized by excessive or asymmetric tissue growth, often caused by dysregulation of conserved growth pathways such as phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI3K). To test how dietary sugar influences genetically driven tissue growth, Drosophila melanogaster was used as a model organism due to its conserved growth pathways and ability to allow tissue-specific genetic manipulation. PI3K signaling was altered in the posterior wing compartment using the targeted overexpression of the GAL4-UAS system. Flies with constitutively active PI3K and PI3K crossed with the two suppressors, PTEN and RNAi, were reared on either a standard diet or a high-sugar diet containing 20% sucrose, creating six total treatment groups. The adult wings of the offspring were dissected following eclosion, mounted on slides, and imaged under magnification. Posterior wing area was measured using ImageJ to assess relative growth differences between genotypes and dietary conditions. Statistical comparisons were performed using a two-way ANOVA test to evaluate the effects of genetic suppression and the diet on wing size. Genetically reducing PI3K signaling enhances growth in genotypes with elevated PI3K activity and partially counteracts suppression. These results suggest that environmental factors such as diet may play a role in modulating growth pathways associated with PI3K signaling, highlighting the significance of gene-environment interactions in regulating tissue growth and providing insight into how dietary factors may influence PI3K-related overgrowth syndromes.

column13: The Investigation of a Novel Plant Based Solution’s Concentration on Drosophila melanogaster Reproduction
column14: Fruit flies of the Drosophila genus pose a significant threat to food security, infesting over 400 crops and resulting in extensive food waste and loss from production to consumption. Conventional pest management strategies rely heavily on synthetic pesticides, which damage ecosystems, harm beneficial organisms, and increase pesticide resistance. This study investigates the effectiveness of a plant-based insecticide derived from the native plant Solidago virgaurea (goldenrod) as a sustainable alternative by examining how different solution concentrations affect the reproductive rate of Drosophila melanogaster. In a previously conducted study prior to this study, powdered Solidago virgaurea successfully reduced the rate of reproduction in Drosophila. Goldenrod leaves were infused in 95% ethanol and diluted to a 4% concentration, which was applied to a trial population. An analysis of the solution was also conducted to identify the compounds that were responsible for insecticidal properties previously observed using a Gas Chromatograph-Mass Spectrometry machine. Reproductive rates were measured over 6 days through larval counts. It is predicted based on preliminary data that the 4% Solidago virgaurea solution will result in significant reduction of reproduction. The purpose of this study is to determine the efficacy of a liquid concentration of a goldenrod-based insecticide in reducing Drosophila reproduction. If effective, this research could support the use of native, plant-based pesticides as environmentally friendly alternatives to synthetic chemicals, promoting sustainable agriculture, biodiversity, and reduced ecological harm. This research also opens doors to insecticidal research done on more pressing insects such as mosquitos.

column13: Dose-Dependent Effects of Origanum vulgare Oil on Glycogen Accumulation in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
column14: Type 2 diabetes is a widespread metabolic disorder characterized by impaired glucose regulation and long term health issues. Current treatments are costly and come with many side effects, which could lead to more opportunities to find plant-based compounds as potential regulators. Origanum vulgare is a medicinal plant rich in bioactive compounds such as phenolics and flavonoids, and has been traditionally been shown to exhibit antioxidant activity and influence cellular metabolism. This study aimed to investigate the effects of Origanum vulgare oil on glycogen accumulation in Saccharomyces cerevisiae, a eukaryotic model organism with conserved glucose regulation pathways. However, limited research has been done to show how Origanum vulgare affects cellular energy storage pathways at the cellular level. The purpose of this study was to investigate whether Origanum vulgare oil alters glycogen accumulation in Saccharomyces cerevisiae. Yeast cultures were grown on YPD agar plates and exposed to varying concentrations of Origanum vulgare oil and stained with iodine to visualize glycogen levels. Glycogen intensity was scored on a standardized 1-5 scale based on staining darkness. Data was recorded using Data Classroom and analyzed using statistical tests to compare the treatment groups to the control. Preliminary results suggest that Origanum vulgare oil alters glycogen accumulation in a dose-dependent manner. Some concentrations were associated with reduced glycogen staining compared to the control, indicating a possible effect on glucose storage pathways. As of April, data collection is ongoing.

column13: Evaluating the Effect of Temperature, Current, and Voltage on EV Battery Sensor Performance
column14:
Since their invention, electric vehicles have become safer over time. However, battery fires are still a pertinent issue throughout the world. The Battery Management System (BMS) is the computer that regulates and controls the environment of the battery pack and relies on voltage, current, and temperature sensors inside the battery pack to function. These sensors can degrade due to adverse elements like extreme temperatures, oversupply of voltage, and oversupply of current. The failure thresholds of typical battery sensors should be found while in a battery pack for future prevention of malfunctions. 
An array of sensors was constructed connected to a battery to simulate an electric vehicle load. Using this apparatus, the environment around the sensors was altered until the sensors malfunctioned, according to the weaknesses mentioned above. While transmitting sensor values to a PC, no more values or incorrect values will reveal where the failure thresholds of the battery sensors are. 
From this procedure, preliminary data of a control group of sensors were collected. In a normal environment, each sensor was able to read accurate values of its surroundings. Variable data was then collected, exposing the sensors to the extreme conditions mentioned above. This data then supported that an incorrect supply of voltage (<5V) was the condition that made the sensors the most unreliable. The voltage sensor was the most reliable overall, the NTC thermistor was somewhat reliable, and the current sensor was the least reliable.

column13: Utilizing Temperature Induced Flow and In-Pipe Turbines to Harness Energy from Data Center Cooling
column14:
Data centers (DC’s) account for more than 25% of Virginia’s annual electricity consumption. This is largely because extensive cooling systems must be implemented to counteract the heat produced by the hardware. As the growth of AI is increasing the demand for data storage and processing, improved data center cooling systems are required to keep this industry energy viable and decrease the effect of DC’s on local electricity and water grids. This project explores thermosiphoning and in-pipe turbines as ways to make modern cooling systems more efficient by harnessing the heat produced by the hardware.
A closed loop of PVC and Galvanized steel was created to test the efficacy of temperature induced flow as a way of replacing pumps in cooling systems. As different parts of the loop are heated the difference in temperature causes a pressure gradient that induces a repetitive circular flow in the system. An in-pipe turbine was installed into the downflow part of the loop in order to convert the mechanical energy into electricity. The system was tested by filling it up with water and heating up one side of the loop while cooling the top.
Data will be collected on what rate of flow was achieved. Additionally, it will be determined what amount of electricity the turbine was able to produce in watts. Next, it would be ideal to test the cooling capabilities of this prototype to determine if it would be viable for implementation in data centers.

column13: Generating Electricity From The Passive Movement of a Tuned Mass Damper to Improve Skyscraper Energy Efficiency
column14:
As the world turns more to clean energy as a future solution, many areas of the world have started utilizing wind and solar energy. However, this is not as feasible in dense urban environments due to the lack of wide open space for these systems to occupy. The Structural Kinetic Energy Conversion System would fix this issue by passively generating clean energy through the conversion of motion from a skyscraper’s tuned mass damper, or TMD, to electricity using a combination of counterweights and motors acting as gravity batteries.
This involves creating a scaled physical prototype integrating a modified TMD with a pendulum system, motors, and secondary counterweights. As the main counterweight moves side to side, it incrementally transfers horizontal to vertical motion, lifting side weights that drop to drive motors and generate electricity. The prototype is tested on a shake table, where voltage output and structural stabilization are measured to determine the efficiency of the system. 
During testing one motor using one secondary counterweight, it was able to produce just around 15 V. The testing was done as the counterweight dropped, and the slight deviation shown in the data points can be attributed to the imprecise nature of the prototype. 
When scaled up, the small scale system currently produces around 30V with both motors in use, and future research will include scaling this number to a feasible large scale model. Future progress will include shortening all wires, widening the base of the tower, and decreasing the torque of the motors.

column13: Optimizing Layer Adhesion Strength for Fused Deposition Modeling using Acetone and Cyanoacrylate
column14:
The real world applications extend to PLA 3D printed parts in applications such as non-load bearing car parts, containers, especially in custom components where other creation techniques have major drawbacks in the areas of price or design, in addition to many others. 

These results were achieved when the PLA specimens were coated in a combination of acid and adhesive, using a mason jar filled with the chemicals and diluted with water. Creating both a strengthened outer layer and protective coating around the print, strengthening the parts in this way significantly improved the weakest specimen in the test meaningfully. 

Testing of this project consisted of impact testing on the PLA charpy impact specimens. The major results were first, that PLA color has a significant effect on the strength and print consistency of the specimens. In the experiment red pieces were more than 15% stronger on average than their brown counterparts, but also had the 3 outliers in the testing results. Leading to Brown pieces being chosen for chemical tests, with the idea that consistent testing would be more valuable than outright part strength. Final testing showed that the chemical compound increased the impact strength of the weakest part by ~13% and the mean by ~5%.

Continued testing of this idea could extend applications to load bearing /temperature resistant parts. Helping to open up new options in the market previously limited to uncustomizable injection molded parts, or weak and inconsistent 3D printed parts.

column13: Utilizing a Zinc Oxide and Lactic Acid Coating to Reduce Mold and Bacterial Growth on PLA Food Storage Containers
column14:
Polylactic acid (PLA) is a biodegradable plastic that has been increasingly used in food storage, but, its main drawback is its lack of antimicrobial properties, contributing to mold growth, food spoilage, and increased food waste (Shao et al, 2022). Poor food preservation also raises public health concerns by increasing the exposure to harmful bacteria. Applying a natural antimicrobial surface coating on PLA containers to improve food safety performance while being environmentally friendly is a solution that is important to be looked at. 
	An antimicrobial coating using lactic acid and zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO) was prepared using a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) binder and applied to the inner surfaces of PLA food containers using a controlled brush-coating method. Lactic acid was chosen for its ability to lower pH and inhibit microbial growth, while ZnO nanoparticles were used due to their proven antimicrobial performance (Duan et al., 2022; Kim et al., 2020). Coated and uncoated containers were used to store fragaria x ananassa under identical, room-temperature conditions. Mold development was monitored over the course of one week.
	Results from the first two trials showed that the coated PLA containers exhibited some improvement in mold reduction compared to uncoated containers. Although the coating was applied successfully to the PLA surface, its antimicrobial effectiveness was limited when tested.
	The findings suggest that while surface coating is a feasible and low-cost method, further research and testing on the coating formula and concentration will be explored.

column13: Detection of Diabetic Foot Ulceration via Plantar Pressure Sensors in Soles
column14:
Diabetic neuropathy, a complication of poorly managed diabetes, is when continuous, untreated high blood sugars damage nerves in the feet. With a lack of sensation in the foot, imperceivable cuts and scrapes can lead to infection, ulceration, and ultimately amputation. The high medical costs that come with this method of treatment is oftentimes unaffordable to many diabetics. There is a significant correlation of low to middle income areas and diabetes diagnoses rates (The Lancet), so costly procedures such as amputation –on top of the general costs of diabetes– are not sustainable for many individuals. 

Mitigation is key rather than treatment. Embedding plantar pressure sensors into a sole allows for continuous gait monitoring. Additionally, a program analyzing pressure distribution differences is an effective indicator for potential hotspots in diabetics’ feet who experience loss of sensation. The sole is made from silicone, which was originally a 3D model printed as a placeholder sole block. Silicone was poured around the block in an acrylic box, then poured again after removing the block. An entirely silicone sole is leftover. Next, pressure sensors were wired and placed underneath the sole. The current prototype is at this stage.

 Preliminary testing reveals that the sensors can successfully detect differences in pressure, which shows promise for further data collection. Moving forward, the implementation of more sensors will give higher accurate readings of plantar pressure. Additionally, a more fleshed out program that is able to automatically detect changing force distributions would be optimal.

column13: Developing Hydrophobic Coatings to Prevent Water-Induced Degradation on Stainless Steel Bridges
column14:
Corrosion of stainless-steel bridge infrastructure caused by long-term water exposure represents a critical threat to public safety and leads to billions of dollars in annual maintenance costs. As climate change continues to intensify precipitation, humidity, and fluctuations in temperature, existing protective coatings fail to prevent long-term degradation, which can eventually lead to bridge failure. Because of this, coatings which chemically repel water from the surface, also known as hydrophobic coatings, have emerged as a solution.

The effectiveness of several hydrophobic coating systems to prevent corrosion in bridges was evaluated. Stainless steel samples were cleaned of contaminants using several solvents and coated with three hydrophobic systems: stearic acid at 30% wt, 33% wt, and 37% wt; silicon dioxide nanoparticles/polydimethylsiloxane at 4% wt, 12% wt, 20% wt; and
tetraethoxysilane/polydimethylsiloxane at 7% wt, 15% wt, and 25% wt. Coatings were applied
through controlled dip-coating methods. 

Hydrophobicity was measured using water contact angle testing, and long-term resistance to water was evaluated through mass change calculated from a month-long saltwater exposure test. There was a strong correlation found between high hydrophobicity and high long-term resistance to water. Stearic acid demonstrated the highest hydrophobicity and long-term resistance to water, followed by tetraethoxysilane/polydimethylsiloxane, and lastly silicon dioxide nanoparticles/polydimethylsiloxane. Overall, stearic acid shows significant potential to prevent corrosion and failure in steel bridges, along with silane-based compounds.

Further research could evaluate coatings using different hydrophobic materials, such as carbon nanotubes, and additional testing could be done including ice and abrasion resistance tests.

column13: Design and Evaluation of a Custom Fused Granular Fabrication Extruder for Recycling 3D Printing Thermoplastics
column14:
Additive manufacturing (AM), particularly fused deposition modeling (FDM), can generate substantial waste through failed prints and support structures; a study estimates that over 379,000 kg of plastic waste is generated by three-dimensional-printing (3DP) in the UK alone. The leading recycling solution in AM is mechanical recycling, a process that involves shredding waste and extruding a filament for later use. Since this process requires specialized machines that are only feasible in industrial environments, mechanical recycling is only available to consumers through post-consumer recycling services. Additionally, samples of plastic that have been mechanically recycled have been shown to exhibit inferior mechanical properties and demonstrate poor printability. 
The relatively novel process of fused granular fabrication (FGF), which involves printing directly from pelletized plastic, has been investigated as an alternative to the standard fused filament fabrication (FFF). To further expand upon this research, the viability of FGF as an alternative recycling solution for polylactic acid (PLA), polyethylene terephthalate glycol (PETG), and acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS) is investigated. An FGF extruder prototype was constructed and adapted to fit a SOVOL SV06 3D printer. Tensile and impact strength tests will be performed on recycled PLA, PETG, and ABS samples printed using the FGF printer. These properties will be compared to samples printed from virgin filaments with an FFF printer. Mechanical deterioration over recycling cycles will be measured and compared to existing findings for mechanically recycling parts. Printability will be compared by analyzing the printing parameters required to reliably print using the FGF and FFF printers.

column13: Analyzing the Efficacy of Electricity in Improving the Penetration of Germicidal Material into Antibiotic Resistant Bacteria
column14:
Antibiotic resistant bacteria (ARB) are a prevalent problem, especially when causing sepsis in hospitals. Current methods for combatting ARB include disinfectant sprays and brass doorknobs, but brass tarnishes and sprays have to be manually applied constantly. As such, developing a doorknob that uses low-current electricity and copper in conjunction to kill ARB would reduce sepsis deaths in hospitals. 
An experiment was developed utilizing E.Coli K-12 in petri dishes using a control, copper alone, and copper with electricity to determine efficacy of adding low-current circuits to reduce bacterial growth on doorknobs. The shock damages the cell membrane, allowing copper ions into the cell. Firstly, experiments on E.Coli K-12 in petri dishes using a control, copper alone, and copper with electricity were done for proof of concept. Results demonstrate that copper does inhibit the growth of bacteria across the entire petri dish. There were more than twice as many colony forming units on the control plate when compared to the copper plate. However, the metal is not effective enough to be considered a disinfectant on its own. Even though the amount of CFUs on it was low, it was not low enough to be safe for human interaction. As well as this, results indicate that electricity with copper is far more effective, fully disinfecting a petri dish after two hours.
Results of this final lab experiment indicate that the two disinfecting factors work together, and are able to be extrapolated to the prototype to prove that it would work in a practical setting.

column13: Optimizing PHA Accumulation in Cupriavidus necator Using Enzyme-Assisted Extraction as a Solvent-Reduced Alternative
column14:
Petroleum-based plastics are a major contributor to pollution in terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems due to their resistance to biodegradation. Although biodegradable alternatives exist, many rely on solvent-intensive extraction processes that utilize toxic chemicals and reduce overall environmental sustainability. Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) are biodegradable polymers synthesized intracellularly by bacteria that offer a renewable alternative to traditional plastics when extracted through environmentally safer methods.
Bacterial cultures of Cupriavidus necator were grown under nutrient-controlled conditions to promote intracellular PHA accumulation. Following incubation, a solvent-reduced, enzyme-based extraction approach was implemented instead of conventional solvent recovery. Lysozyme was applied to disrupt bacterial cell walls, followed by trypsin treatment to digest intracellular proteins and non-polymeric cellular components. The remaining polymer-rich material was isolated, washed, and dried to recover PHA.
Solid material was obtained following enzymatic digestion and drying that displayed physical characteristics consistent with partially purified PHA, including resistance to water solubility and thermoplastic behavior upon heating. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) provided visual confirmation of intracellular PHA granules and successful polymer recovery following enzymatic treatment. While polymer yield was lower than that of solvent-based extraction when scaled for comparison, analysis of yield relative to culture volume demonstrated substantial polymer recovery. These results support the feasibility of enzyme-based, solvent-reduced PHA extraction as a safer and more environmentally conscious alternative at small scale. Future work will focus on optimizing bacterial growth conditions, enzyme concentrations, and treatment durations to increase polymer yield, as well as evaluating the scalability and economic viability of this approach for sustainable bioplastic production.

column13: Developing a Coating Created With Barium Sulfate and Titanium Dioxide to Create a Cool Coat Solution For Concrete Buildings
column14:
Urban heat islands make cities up to 5–7°F hotter than nearby rural areas. Concrete and asphalt absorb solar energy and release heat slowly. This increases energy use, worsens air quality, and reduces comfort. Reflective coatings reduce heat absorption and lower surface temperature.

Concrete bricks were coated with standard roof paint, commercial reflective roof paint, and an experimental paint mixture. The mixture used a low-VOC base with titanium dioxide and barium sulfate. Titanium dioxide reflects visible and ultraviolet light. Barium sulfate reflects a wider range of solar radiation. A halogen lamp simulated sunlight. Surface temperature was recorded before heating, after heating, and after 20 minutes.

Current results show reflective paint reached 66–68°F and cooled to about 63°F after 20 minutes. Regular roof paint reached higher temperatures and cooled more slowly. Reflective coatings reduced heat absorption and improved cooling.

Low-cost reflective coatings reduce concrete surface temperature and limit urban heat island effects. Future work will test different pigment ratios, repeat trials, and test coatings outdoors.

column13: Investigating Cryogenic Cupping and Its Effect on Cooling Depth in Simulated Human Tissue
column14:
Athletic injury rates have increased in recent years as athletes continue to train at higher intensities, creating a greater demand for effective recovery methods. Cryotherapy and cupping therapy are both commonly used in sports medicine, but limited research has explored how combining these methods may influence tissue temperature dynamics. The purpose of this project was to investigate how suction affects the depth and distribution of cooling through a layered tissue model, with potential applications in athletic recovery and physical therapy.
A phantom forearm model was constructed to simulate skin, fat, muscle, and bone layers, with temperature sensors embedded at multiple depths to record thermal changes over time. Baseline testing was conducted using a standard ice pack placed on the surface of the phantom limb to verify sensor responsiveness and establish a control condition. A cryogenic cupping system was then developed, consisting of a small insulated reservoir containing chilled water, which was circulated through flexible tubing surrounding suction cups to deliver controlled cooling while applying negative pressure.
Testing showed that while both methods reduced temperature at all depths, the cryogenic cupping system produced slightly greater cooling at deeper layers compared to the ice pack alone, suggesting that suction may enhance heat transfer through tissue. These results indicate that combining suction with cryotherapy has the potential to improve cooling penetration, although the difference was modest.
Future work could focus on optimizing suction strength, flow rate, and cup design to further enhance effectiveness and better understand the relationship between mechanical deformation and thermal distribution.

column13: Designing and Optimizing Aerospike Nozzle Propulsion Models Using ANSYS Fluent
column14:
Rocket engines can lose up to 10–15% of their efficiency at off-design altitudes because traditional bell nozzles cannot adjust to changing atmospheric pressure conditions. Aerospike nozzles have shown the potential to maintain higher efficiency across a wider range of altitudes by allowing exhaust gases to expand more naturally against the surrounding air, reducing energy losses during ascent.
This engineering research project examines the comparison between aerospike propulsion and a traditional bell nozzle in terms of pressure distribution, thrust-related values, and overall efficiency. Rather than focusing solely on theoretical comparison, the study applies computational simulation tools to evaluate real performance differences and explore potential aerospace applications.
ANSYS Fluent was utilized to design both aerospike and traditional rocket nozzles. Detailed CAD geometries were created and prepared for simulation. A structured meshing process was completed to divide each nozzle geometry into small computational elements, allowing accurate analysis of exhaust flow behavior. Special attention was given to mesh density near the throat and expansion regions to enhance solution accuracy and effectively capture pressure gradients.
Simulations will be run to evaluate flow characteristics within each nozzle design. The results will show thrust-related values, pressure distribution patterns, and efficiency differences between the two selected nozzle types. Expected results include more uniform pressure adaptation along the aerospike surface and improved performance at varying pressure conditions compared to the traditional bell nozzle. At this stage, finalized CAD models, structured data tables, and completed mesh setups provide a strong foundation for full simulation testing and detailed performance evaluation.

column13: Evaluating the Effectiveness of Strigolactone and Comfrey on the Growth of Pleurotus ostreatus mycelium to Simulate Stereocilia to Reverse Hearing Loss
column14:
Hearing loss affects over 430 million people worldwide, with 90% of cases due to the loss of inner ear cells, stereocilia: the inner ear follicles essential for hearing (Corwin). As stereocilia regrowth is not possible in humans, so investigating whether Pleurotus ostreatus mycelium can serve as a model for stereocilia regeneration and which stimulants create the most growth. Both stereocilia and fungal mycelium contain actin-based filament networks and branching structures, demonstrating their structural similarity, allowing the determination whether Pleurotus ostreatus mycelium grown on hydrogel scaffolds could simulate stereocilia growth patterns and respond to growth stimulators.
Gelatin methacryloyl hydrogels were inoculated with Pleurotus ostreatus mycelium, and then inoculated with nutrient solutions and plant-derived growth stimulators. The groups included nutrients and root extract added to the hydrogel to determine which stimulant provides the most growth. The fungal growth was monitored through observations of the amount of spores and length of fungi. This was then compared to images of human stereocilia to analyze their similarities.
The results demonstrate that there was an average of five fungi grown under the control, 12 under root extract, and one under the strigacolone. The branching and actin structures also resembled stereocilia when looked at under a microscope. These observations demonstrate that Pleurotus ostreatus mycelium grown on hydrogels can potentially serve as a model for stereocilia regeneration. Future steps will try and determine how this stereocilia simulation structure reacts to other growth mediums and hydrogel mixtures to create more accurate data.

column13: Improving the Flame-Retardant Qualities of a Glove Through Nanotech Enhancements for Upper Extremity Personal Protective Equipment
column14: Firefighter personal protective equipment is effective for every piece except for gloves. Through the use of nanotechnology, the material strength, durability, and internal temperature can be improved. Hands are the most commonly burned body parts as they are constantly in the proximity of the fire. By doing said enhancements, a new glove model can be created that increases the length at which the glove can work while on fire as well as decreasing the amount of heat that permeates the shielding to the hand. But, to get to that point, a solution was created of resin and nano-silica and applied to the glove. Once complete, the comparison study can be performed to determine the effectiveness of the particles. In a successful situation, the prototype remains effective in the fire for longer and reads lower temperatures than the control. In both of the tests, the coated glove read lower interior temperatures at both the lower and higher temperatures. This quantitative testing confirmed the coated glove had 4% and 8% more heat prevented than the uncoated glove. If given more time, a qualitative analysis could be performed to directly observe how the exterior of the glove reacts in direct heat.

column13: Synthesizing an Enhanced P. ostreatus Biosorbent Utilizing Zinc Oxide Nanoparticles for Sustainable Wastewater Dye Degradation
column14:
The textile industry accounts for 20% of global wastewater pollution from improper dye disposal and runoff. The dyes used in garment production contain harmful chemicals and carcinogens that are capable of harming ecosystems by blocking photosynthetic reactions and poisoning organisms. Biosorption is a technique used to combat this pollution that utilizes organic biomass to adsorb dye particles from water. However, when these spent biosorbents are disposed of in landfills, they contain the same contaminants as the dyes they adsorb and can thus spread their toxins into the rest of the environment.
	Incorporating the photodegrative properties of zinc oxide nanoparticles into a Pleurotus ostreatus (oyster mushroom) biosorbent could create a sorbent that can both sustainably degrade dye and be recycled for future biosorption. A dried oyster mushroom biosorbent was soaked in a ZnO nanoparticle dispersion and used to degrade a methyl orange dye solution. When tested via spectrophotometry for total dye adsorption after 96h, it was found that the ZnO-enhanced biosorbent only adsorbed 5.7% of the dye while the control sorbent adsorbed 81.4% dye. While the results indicate that the enhanced biosorbent is significantly disadvantaged when it comes to dye adsorption, this is likely because the ZnO nanoparticles blocked the pores that the methyl orange molecules would have bonded to. As a result, further studies may incorporate alternate photodegrative agents or alternative production methods to synthesize an enhanced sorbent.

column13: Utilizing a Titanium Dioxide and Zinc Oxide Nanoparticle Coating to Reduce Biofilm Formation on Urinary Catheters
column14:
Biofilm-associated infections remain a major challenge in healthcare because bacteria easily adheres to medical device surfaces and develops resistance to conventional antibiotic treatment. Developing and evaluating a dual-layer antibacterial coating composed of titanium dioxide (TiO₂) and zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles, which is designed to reduce bacterial adhesion and biofilm formation, was the focus of this study. Through surface engineering, the safety of using biomedical devices, such as catheters, may be improved.
	
Polyvinyl chloride catheter segments were cut, sterilized, and sequentially dip-coated with a polydopamine (PDA) adhesion layer followed by TiO₂ and ZnO nanoparticle dispersions. Surface morphology and layer presence were examined using optical digital microscopy and scanning electron microscopy following conductive sputter coating. Imaging confirmed the presence of nanoparticle layers on the catheter surface.

The coated and uncoated control segments were exposed to non-pathogenic Escherichia coli and Bacillus subtilis cultures, and biofilm formation was quantified using crystal violet staining to determine whether the coatings reduced bacterial attachment compared to the control. The coated segment demonstrated a 31.8% decrease in biofilm formation for E. coli and a 21.64% decrease for B. subtilis, compared to the control, as measured by 590 nm absorbance values. The prototyping process and layer results suggest the feasibility of constructing a multilayer antibacterial coating, with potential relevance for improving the safety of biomedical devices in clinical settings.

column13: Investigation of Tungsten Thermophotovoltaic Cells in a Geothermal Setting
column14: As of 2025, 74% of U.S. energy is generated from fossil fuels, making it the second biggest carbon contributor globally (ClientEarth Communications, 2025). Additionally, new U.S. policies are loosening environmental limitations that were once in place for companies in regards to energy sourcing, meaning the energy market is leaning towards fossil fuels now more than ever before. To compete with the convenience of  nonrenewable energy, green energy systems constantly need to find new ways to be more efficient and cheaper. Solar, hydro, and even wind energy are constantly coming up with new technology; however one of the most untapped sources currently is geothermal energy, accounting for less than 1% of U.S. total energy generation. Using thermophotovoltaic cells, an emerging energy solution, geothermal energy production can double its efficiencies and become a steady contender for renewable energy generation. The quantum mechanics involved in the TPV setup simplify the mechanical aspect of current geothermal extraction, and lowers future maintenance, which can be traded off in the form of complex initial fabrication. TPV systems are being optimized in various lab settings and have shown promising potential for implementation in actuality. This type of system requires further research, but relies on the geological heat powering the thermal emitter, which radiates photons to a photovoltaic cell to generate electricity. Choosing proper materials, site location, system setup, fabrication schematics, and data manipulation, a blueprint can be created for a geothermal system to contribute to the implementation of this design in the future.

column13: Determining the Favorability of Intraspecific Cooperation over Interspecific Cooperation by Darwinian Evolution Using a Genetic Algorithm
column14: This research used a genetic algorithm in Python to simulate the evolution of two species interacting in confrontations for resources to find out if an individual in a species would choose to cooperate more with another member of its own species than a member of another species. Individuals were assigned genes that determine their probability to choose to compete. The two genes that determine the increase or decrease in probability to compete depending on if the confrontation is interspecific or intraspecific were compared. Each individual’s fitness was affected by obtaining or losing resources through confrontations. The most fit individuals were chosen to cross over its genes with a member of its own species to create the next generation. Using game theory, when cooperating, the individuals have to split the resources or receive some punishment. This research obtained results for two parameters of reward and punishment, one where the reward and punishment were the same and one where the punishment was greater than the reward. Both parameters saw an overall inclination to compete and saw there was a statistical difference found using a chi-square test between the two genes influencing interspecific competition and intraspecific competition. Although the difference in overall choices in intraspecific cooperation versus interspecific cooperation was small, the statistical difference in the genes showed there was a slightly more inclination towards interspecific competition indicating some favorability towards intraspecific cooperation. The statistical difference in the two genes when punishment and reward were the same was smaller but still significant.

column13: Traffic Forecasting via the Addition of External Features
column14: Medium-term traffic forecasting can be difficult due to the dynamic nature of many factors impacting the choices of drivers. This notebook covers how incorporating a more complete subset of those factors into a deep learning model can improve the accuracy of the resultant model, from macro-sized events like rain, to micro-sized disruptions with disproportional impacts (like crashes). This notebook builds upon another traffic forecasting model called GMAN (Zheng et al., 2019), which is based on an encoder-decoder structure where both consist of multiple attention blocks. Experimentally, these features are demonstrated to be very valuable to the model. Specifically, I demonstrate an improvement of ~33% over the same model with only traffic patterns when measured using MAE on a z-normalized dataset.

column13: Improving Plant Disease Detection with Multi-Modal Machine Learning, Combining Image and Numerical Data
column14: Global food insecurity is significantly worsened by crop diseases, which currently record for an estimated 20-40% loss in annual yield. While existing deep learning models using leaf images have shown high accuracy, they remain inherently reactive, identifying symptoms only after physiological damage is visible. This research proposes a more comprehensive multimodal machine learning system that combines a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for image analysis with numerical soil data, such as pH, moisture, and NPK levels. By analyzing a dataset of over 17,600 images across 13 distinct plant classes (including Tomato, Potato, and Pepper), this study tests the hypothesis that a fused model will achieve a higher F1 score than single-modality models by capturing latent environmental stressors that precede disease.

column13: A Graph Based Physics-Informed Neural Network for Real-Time Air  Pollution Forecasting
column14:
Accurate real-time forecasting of PM2.5 concentrations remains a
major challenge due to sparse monitoring networks and the complex
physical dynamics governing pollutant transport. This study proposes
a graph-based physics-informed neural network (PINN) designed to
improve air pollution forecasting under sparse observational
conditions while maintaining computational feasibility for real-time
deployment. The model represents monitoring stations as nodes in a
35×35 spatial graph and integrates graph convolutional networks
(GCNs) with physically grounded diffusion and advection constraints.
A gated fusion mechanism combines learned graph representations
with physics-based partial differential equation components to model
pollutant transport dynamics more effectively.
The framework was evaluated on the Beijing PM2.5 dataset across
multiple forecasting horizons. At the 24-hour horizon, the model
achieved an MAE of 42.00 and RMSE of 52.88, with strong
performance maintained across shorter horizons. Training efficiency
was optimized to 29 epochs in under five minutes, demonstrating
feasibility for real-time applications. Results indicate that integrating
physical diffusion–advection principles with graph neural networks
improves modeling accuracy while preserving scalability, suggesting
a promising direction for deployable environmental forecasting
systems.

column13: Multi-Objective Optimization of Sustainable Aviation Fuel Blends Using a Physics- and Kinetics-Informed Turbofan Simulation
column14: Multi-objective optimization of jet engine sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) blends remains computationally intractable: traditional 1D thermodynamic cycle analysis lacks spatial fidelity, while high-resolution CFD with detailed chemical kinetics is too expensive for optimization sweeps. This project addresses this gap by developing an open-source turbofan digital twin combining Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) with high-fidelity combustion modeling to enable scalable, fuel-dependent SAF optimization across thrust, TSFC, NOx, and lifecycle CO2 simultaneously. The framework integrates three PINN modules (a 1D Nozzle PINN, a Turbine PINN, and a 2D Locally Enhanced PINN incorporating axisymmetric RANS residuals) with Cantera combustion simulations using the CRECK mechanism for SAF evaluation and the Stanford HyChem mechanism for Jet-A1 baselining. Thermodynamic outputs from the combustor propagate directly into the flow equations. To ensure spatial fidelity, the 2D LE-PINN is fine-tuned and validated using the NASA NPARC Sajben transonic diffuser dataset, rigorously benchmarking wall-pressure and velocity profiles instead of relying on synthetic CFD pipelines. Validation against the ICAO Engine Emissions Databank yielded R² = 0.9969 for NOx. The system achieved exact mass conservation and inlet state reproduction with maximum relative error under 0.464%. At simulated cruise conditions, the optimized Bio-SPK blend converged at 81.58 kN thrust, TSFC of 29.60 mg/(N*s), and an 80% lifecycle CO₂ reduction relative to conventional Jet-A1. By replacing PDE solvers with RANS-informed surrogates, this framework makes high-fidelity SAF design accessible on standard hardware, helping a critical pathway for aviation decarbonization.

column13: Comparing Treatments of Selenium and Levothyroxine on a Hypothyroidism Model Through Cell Inflammation in Vitro
column14: Hypothyroidism is a chronic endocrine disorder characterized by insufficient production of thyroid hormones thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3). This leads to metabolic dysregulation, fatigue, weight gain, and cardiovascular issues. The current treatment, Levothyroxine, is a synthetic T4 hormone associated with long-term side effects, including increased risks of cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, and cancer. Increasing evidence suggests selenoproteins, derived from selenium, can improve thyroid function by reducing inflammation through antioxidant defense rather than replacing hormones. This study investigates the effects of sodium selenite and Levothyroxine on an inflammation-based hypothyroidism model in vitro. Selenium was selected for its role in the synthesis of selenoproteins, particularly glutathione peroxidase 3 (GPx3). Once inflamed with lipopolysaccharide, cells were treated with sodium selenite or Levothyroxine, alongside control groups. Cell viability was assessed using an acid phosphatase assay, and GPx3 expression was measured via Western blot. A Kruskal–Wallis test showed a significant difference in cell viability between the Levothyroxine and selenium treatment groups; however, many selenium trial values were above the assay’s quantifiable range, showing that neither treatment negatively affected cell viability. An ANOVA followed by a post-hoc Tukey’s Range Test on GPx3 protein levels from the Western blot revealed a significant increase in GPx3 expression in the selenium treatment group compared to the Levothyroxine group and the controls, supporting the hypothesis. This study provides further evidence for selenium as a potential alternative to synthetic hormone replacement. Further studies utilizing thyroid-specific cell lines and direct hormone quantification assays are recommended to better explain selenium’s role in thyroid homeostasis.

column13: Simulating Urban Evacuation Traffic Under Authoritative Hurricane Forecasting with Agent-Based Modeling
column14: In today’s age with such interconnectedness, human response to catastrophic events illustrate crucial patterns of behavior led by physicality, emotion, and social influence. This research dives into emergency government communication and traffic congestion, as individuals in an urban environment have been simulated to see how they respond to a hurricane across different time phases. This simulation replicates geospatial data to reflect real-world geography on an abstracted level, namely building and road infrastructure. Each agent has a set of psychological and social traits, including risk tolerance, social conformity, and stubbornness. This variation enables emergent behavior to be portrayed. Combined, outside influences like government forecast announcements and media institutions determine whether the agent in the model evacuates and seeks shelter, ignores the warnings and remains stationary, or gets stuck in traffic and panics. The final product is a computational simulation in GAMA with visualizations depicting evacuation status over time, average traffic density, evacuation wave distribution, cumulative evacuation rate, system pressure indicators, average road speed coefficient, and decision factors. The resulting data provides valuable insights on approximate evacuation trends in the context of a week-long hurricane. However, due to computational limitations, large-scale dynamics were unable to be modeled because of the restricted vehicle speed and bounding size of the cities. Further research should be conducted on both ends of the spectrum: (1) expanded geography with large, detailed cities and (2) abstracted representation of agents using boids in small, focused locations.

column13: Food Allergy Finder
column14: Food allergies affect millions of individuals worldwide, often limiting safe dining options for them and creating anxiety when eating outside. Even though there are existing allergy tracking applications, they lack personalized allergen information and are unable to identify specific restaurant menu items that are safe for the user to consume. This leaves users without specific actionable guidance that they can use when they attempt to dine outside. This project proposes a mobile application designed to deliver personalized, allergen-safe restaurant menu recommendations. The application combines user-specified allergen profiles with menu data from restaurant franchises and analyzes ingredient lists to filter and present safe menu options to the user. In addition, geolocation services are integrated to provide the user with safe restaurants nearby. The proposed solution improves accessibility, safety and most importantly, convenience for individuals with food allergies, assisting with bridging the gap between consumer health and restaurant accessibility.

column11: Note - my project category could potentially reach into TEHA
column13: Design and Implementation of a Library-based Game Engine using C++
column14:
The goal of this project is to create a library based game engine using C++. As the name suggests, the project will be structured after a library, possessing excellent portability and ease of access. One goal for the creation of this project is to help beginners learn C++ through a more generalized coding experience rather than the classic flashy game engines. Another goal is to provide an alternative to developers who enjoy raw coding experiences rather than working with visual tools, or if they want to disassociate their games with big game engine companies. In order for this project to be successful, a modular approach with many abstraction layers will be used. The modular approach will provide a high level of customization for what parts the user may or may not want, while multiple layers of abstraction will adapt the engine for beginners and experts alike.
As of current, there are many details to the foundation of the engine, consisting of a math library, a carefully constructed central system, and a windows-based windowing system. Due to the low-level nature of C++, many more memory and performance considerations have been required for each step, resulting in an extensive amount of time being spent (the engine as a whole has 4 iterations, while each component has gone through at least 2 reconstructions). There is currently a quick demo that proves the solidity of the foundations, with the next steps being to start implementing real object-based 3d graphics.

column13: Killing Time: Creation of a Turn-Based 2-D RPG Video Game Within the Godot Engine
column14: Killing Time is a 2-D RPG video game. The player controls a doodle on a sticky note that travels through an office, completes a quest, and slays sentient office supplies. Their ultimate goal is to defeat the evil chronomancer (another doodle on a sticky note) who is causing chaos. The game consists of an introduction sequence, a central area to collect a quest, and a boss battle that concludes the previously acquired quest. While playing, the player has choices during dialogue of what their character says and choices during combat to determine the moves their character makes. The game is built in the Godot Engine and all assets were original creations in pixel art creating software like Aseprite. To continue the project, more combat maneuvers would be given to both the player and enemies to increase the combat’s complexity. Additionally, more levels would be implemented to increase the length of the game. Sound effects and music are another layer that can be added to enhance the overall playing experience.

column13: FragrAI : AI Fragrance Recommendation Engine
column14: A common source where people go to look at and research fragrances is a hub called  Fragrantica. This software may have all information necessary for fragrances, but the user interface is unideal. FragAI aims to build a website inspired by Fragrantica but with a cleaner, more efficient interface and added features. Beyond enhancing the user interface and the website’s navigability, the core significance of the project lies in helping individuals with their day-to-day lives. Fragrances can boost confidence, elevate mood, and even influence social interactions. This is because they can enhance pheromones, making the fragrance user more appealing to individuals around them. FragrAI uses an OpenAI API, which is then designed to recommend fragrances tailored to individual users on a user interface. To achieve this, FragrAI will ask users consent-based questions about their personality, physical traits, lifestyle, and have them enter a free-response answer detailing all the information about themselves.. The model will then analyze a fragrance database to suggest fragrances and dupes (similar but cheaper fragrances). Each recommendation will include details such as name, designer, longevity, description, notes analysis, price, and dupes, matched with a similarity score corresponding to how well the fragrance matched the question answers. Additionally, the website will feature a color scheme that appeals to all age groups, intuitive navigation, and seamless integration of the FragrAI model for a smooth experience. If time permits, more features can be added, which includes storing liked fragrances, creating user profiles, note recommendations, and creating fragrances from scratch.

column13: Implementing Adaptive Winglets Utilizing Shape Memory Alloys
column14:
Aircraft winglets improve fuel efficiency by reducing wingtip vortices, but fixed-shape winglets are a compromise because they cannot adapt to different flight phases. During takeoff, high lift is required, while during cruising, minimizing drag is most important. Airlines spent approximately $291 billion on jet fuel in 2024 (IATA, 2024), and commercial aviation produces roughly 1 billion metric tons of CO₂ annually (IEA, 2023). Even small aerodynamic improvements could result in significant economic and environmental savings. Wind tunnel tests were conducted to show whether a morphing winglet can improve efficiency across multiple flight conditions.

A small-scale prototype was designed using CAD software and fabricated with 3D-printed components and nitinol shape memory alloy (SMA) wire. The winglet transitioned between a blended wingtip and split scimitar configuration through SMA heating. Wind tunnel testing was conducted at multiple airspeeds and angles of attack to measure lift and drag, and lift-to-drag (L/D) ratios were calculated.

At the takeoff angle, the blended wingtip produced higher L/D ratios, increasing from approximately 7.0 to 9.0 as speed increased, compared to 5.2 to 7.8 for the split scimitar. At the cruising angle, the split scimitar outperformed the blended wingtip, increasing from about 12 to 40, while the blended configuration increased from roughly 7 to 29. The SMA mechanism reliably achieved shape change and maintained structural stability. These results support that morphing winglets can improve aerodynamic efficiency, with future work focused on electrical actuation.

column13: Optimizing Composite 3D Printed Running Shoe Plates as a Replacement to Expensive Carbon Fiber Standards
column14:
How efficiently a runner uses their energy at a submaximal pace is called an individual's running economy. Current running shoes incorporate a plate made from carbon fiber which results in an improved running economy. Plated running shoes have a high manufacturing cost with pairs ranging in price between $200 and $300. Development of a customizable 3D printable plate would lower manufacturing costs, allowing for an introductory shoe providing a more affordable option for new runners.

Customized plates were prototyped based off of a Brooks Ghost midsole design and 3D modeled in CAD software. Three types of carbon-fiber filaments with additives were then tested: carbon-fiber and thermoplastic polyurethane (CF+TPU), carbon-fiber and nylon (CF+PA6), and carbon-fiber and polycarbonate (CF+PC). Mechanical tests were conducted to determine which material had the optimal structural performance through force displacement graphs. A biomechanical VO2  test was completed to determine the oxygen intake, to verify the improvement of running economy. VO2 test data was collected with and without CF+PA6 plates. The data was then cleaned so that the plates could be compared to current industry carbon-fiber plates.

The CF+PA6 plates had the most energy return during the mechanical testing phase, proving that the plates increased the energy return of the modified shoe. The subsequent VO2 test proved that the running economy was also improved showing lower oxygen intake and overall efficiency. These data points suggest that these low cost plates have the potential to significantly lower manufacturing costs compared to current industry production.

column13: Optimization of Nose Cones for Atmospheric Reentry of SmallSATs
column14: Current allowance for smallsat ablation at end of life causes the release of metric tons of aluminum oxide and wastes billions of dollars every year. By creating an affordable nose cone to allow the satellite to survive reentry, wasted money and air/space pollution is limited while capabilities are increased through the allowance of biological and material tests, testing repetition, sensor calibration, and more. CFD simulations determined the optimal nose shape and size by calculating static margin and wall heat flux. Cone performance was confirmed using AoA stability, peak heating, and orbital decay simulations. Results showed the 0.5 m tall power-law cone was the most effective shape, with a static margin of 0.33 cal and peak heating of around 230 W/m². The optimized cone reached a maximum surface temperature of 1007.62 °C. AoA simulations plotted pitching moment versus angle, producing a slope of −4.5×10⁻⁵. Purposeful variations in cone heading resulted in 2.56 days of touchdown variation in orbital decay simulations. A composite of Nextel 440 and alumina–silica ceramic fiber blanket was selected based on measured thermal loads. Because the pitching moment slope was negative, the cone generates restoring moments and is stable. Combined with heating remaining below the material limit and variable touchdown by controlling orbital decay, these results indicate the 0.5 m power-law cone can survive reentry while maintaining a controlled, predictable descent. Future work will improve simulation accuracy and begin materials testing.

column13: Development of a Self-Leveling Patient Transport System Using Inclinometer-Based Feedback Control
column14: Increasing participation in outdoor sports has heightened the need for trail rescue and emergency medical services in challenging terrain. At the moment, extraction methods, including backboards and stokes baskets, place patient loads fully on providers. One in four Emergency Medical Technicians (EMTs) sustain career ending back injuries within the first four years of work (EMS Safety, 2011). These injuries are largely caused by moving patients and compensating for traditional EMS cot failures on uneven terrain (Fratta et al., 2020). This project develops a self-leveling patient transport system using inclinometer-based feedback control.  To accomplish this, two dynamic inclinometers in combination with a four actuator system are utilized. One inclinometer monitors the patient section while the second monitors the base.  These inclinometers communicate with one another through a series of code to adjust actuators and maintain patient section levelness. Testing includes obtaining video recordings from the front and side perspectives as the prototype moves through a series of specified angles. Video analysis compares the predicted angles and the resultant adjustments made by the system to quantify angle errors.  Final testing resulted in average angle errors of 3.30 degree along the x-axis and 3.94 degrees along the y-axis.  Future research should address precise calibration of inclinometers, energy efficiency, and cost optimization for real-world application.

column13: AI-Driven Browser Extension to Reduce Content Scrolling
column14: Modern day social media platforms encourage continuous content consumption, leading to a behavior commonly referred to as “doomscrolling,” where users repeatedly view short or recommended content with little meaningful engagement. This project presents the design and implementation of a browser extension that detects potential doomscrolling behavior on YouTube and introduces graduated interventions to encourage mindful consumption. The system employs behavioral signals such as scroll distance, interaction frequency, session duration, and engagement with videos or Shorts. These signals are analyzed using a hybrid detection approach that combines rule-based heuristics with a lightweight machine learning model to estimate the probability of doomscrolling. Based on the detected behavior, the extension deploys a three-level intervention method consisting of awareness banners, suggestion prompts, and temporary scroll resistance mechanisms. The objective is to not block content but to introduce moments of reflection that help users regain awareness of their browsing behavior. This project demonstrates how real-time behavioral analysis and subtle interface nudges can be used to promote healthier digital habits while maintaining a positive user experience.

column13: RegiMon: Monitoring the Windows Registry in Real-Time
column14:
The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database containing key-value pairs governing core operating system behavior including security configurations, user permissions, and software privileges. Because of its central role in system operation, the Registry is a prime target for malware seeking to establish persistence. The registry has both a stable and volatile copy, and while existing tools such as RegRipper or RegShot analyze the on-disk registry through point-in-time snapshots, there is a significant gap in monitoring the volatile registry which allows malicious activity to go unnoticed.
This project presents RegiMon, a lightweight real-time registry monitoring application built in Rust using the Tauri framework. RegiMon takes advantage of the Windows API call RegNotifyChangeKeyValue() to continuously monitor the volatile RAM copy of the registry, detecting modifications, deletions, and insertions to the registry as they occur. Upon detecting a change, the application alerts the user and offers immediate remediation options, including the ability to undo suspicious modifications. RegiMon runs as a system tray application, minimizing performance overhead and avoiding interference with existing security software.
Testing was conducted in a sandboxed Windows 10 environment using manual registry edits and system settings changes. RegiMon successfully detected all changes in real time, demonstrating its viability as an early detection mechanism. Following these tests, the Love Bug virus was used against RegiMon, and RegiMon was able to prevent the persistence mechanisms Love Bug attempted to create. Future work will extend monitoring to a larger set of keys and implement persistent logging.

column13: Blender Animation Add-On
column14: Digital 3D animation is a skill that requires a large amount of time and skill to be successful. This can cause 3D Animation to be a large obstacle for hobbyists and small productions that may not have the time or funds to create high quality animations for their projects. This project aims to create an addon for the free digital 3D suite Blender that will provide tools to make 3D animation more accessible for hobbyists. This problem will be addressed with a Posing Assistance tool. Posing assistance will help users create more accurate and believable animations by adding tools to determine if a pose is physically accurate and balanced. It will also feature tools to correct unbalanced poses to make them balanced. Together these tools will help users create animations more quickly to allow more time for other elements of production.

column13: The Effect of Differing Levels of Copper and Aluminum in a CuAlSn Alloy on the Strength-to-Weight Ratio of the Alloy
column14:
This study investigates how varying the composition of copper, aluminum, and tin in a CuAlSn alloy affects strength-to-weight ratio. Lightweight, durable metals are increasingly important in engineering fields such as transportation, aerospace, and manufacturing, where reducing mass while maintaining mechanical performance can improve fuel efficiency, cost effectiveness, and operational lifespan. Although copper- and aluminum-based alloys are widely used, limited research has systematically examined how specific CuAlSn ratios influence both mechanical strength and thermal stability.
It was hypothesized that alloys with higher copper concentrations would perform best due to copper’s strong mechanical properties and excellent thermal conductivity. Seven alloy compositions were prepared with constant tin content (5%) while copper was decreased from 90% to 60% and aluminum increased from 5% to 35%. Each sample had a total mass of 7 g and was cast using an induction furnace at approximately 1100°C, then poured into preheated molds and cooled under controlled conditions. Pure copper served as the control.
Mechanical performance was evaluated through tensile testing using a Mark-10 F305 force tester, and strength-to-weight ratio was calculated from measured force and sample mass. 
The results of this study are expected to identify an optimized CuAlSn alloy composition that balances low weight, high strength, and thermal durability. Findings may support the development of more efficient and cost-effective materials for demanding industrial applications.

column13: Creating Heterogeneous Enzyme-Metal Hybrid Catalysts (EMHCs) Using Candida Antarctica Lipase B (CALB) Enzymes and Ruthenium Nanoparticles to Improve the Synthesis of Enantiopure Chiral Amines
column14: Enantiomers typically exhibit different biological activities, pharmacological properties, and toxicities. Due to this, especially in pharmaceutical research, it is crucial to ensure that only one side of the enantiomer is present in order to achieve successful drug development because the other side of the enantiomer may have harmful, undesirable side effects. The goal of this project was to synthesize enzyme-metal hybrid catalysts made of ruthenium nanoparticles and CALB enzymes on a porous magnetic wrinkled organosilica carrier in order to conduct chemoenzymatic dynamic kinetic resolution of 1-phenylethylamine. A heterogenous magnetic wrinkled organosilica carrier was created with an iron oxide core and a silica coating. This was characterized through SEM imaging and FT-IR. We plan to immobilize the CALB enzyme and ruthenium metal on this carrier and test this on the enantiomer, phenylethylamine to determine conversion yield and enantiomeric excess percentage. The enantiomers of 1-phenylethylamine were analyzed using the NMR.

column13: The Effect of Different Sodium Hydroxide Concentrations on Carbon Dioxide Absorption in a System with Chlorella vulgaris Encapsulated in Calcium Alginate Beads
column14: Increasing concentrations in atmospheric carbon dioxide is a major driver of climate change, showing the need for more efficient and sustainable carbon capture technology. This project explores a hybrid carbon capture system that integrates chemical absorption using sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and biological fixation using the microalgae Chlorella vulgaris. To mitigate the harmful effects of high pH concentrations on the microalgae, the cells are immobilized within calcium alginate beads, which provide protection while allowing gas diffusion and nutrient exchange. Sodium hydroxide is introduced gradually into the system to better control pH conditions and maintain microalgal viability. The study investigates how varying initial NaOH concentrations influence overall carbon dioxide absorption in the combined system. It is hypothesized that an optimal concentration range exists where chemical absorption is maximized while without significantly inhibiting biological activity. By identifying this balance, the project aims to evaluate the effectiveness of integrating chemical and biological approaches for enhanced carbon capture. The findings could inform the development of scalable, hybrid systems for reducing CO2 emissions in real-world applications.

column13: Targeting Lycorma delicatula Ootheca Using Candida rugosa and Goat Lipase Enzymes in Leesburg, Virginia
column14: The spotted lanternfly (Lycorma Delicatula) is an invasive species that has spread across the eastern United States, causing significant agricultural damage, particularly to vineyards. Currently, control methods to curb the spread rely heavily on the application of chemical pesticides, which are cost-inefficient, environmentally harmful, and inconsistently effective. A major challenge in controlling this species is the protective ootheca surrounding its eggs, a waxy, water-resistant coating that limits pesticide penetration. Egg cases were collected from various trees in Loudoun County, Virginia, and the ootheca were separated into uniform samples. Baseline characterization included mass and visual observations, and Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Samples were then treated with fungal and animal-derived lipases and compared to controls over 5-day and 20-day exposure periods under practical, non-optimized conditions. Initial FTIR analysis of untreated samples revealed a predominantly protein-rich structure with hydroxyl and amide functional groups, while ester signals were weak or masked. These findings suggest that while the ootheca is highly resistant, ester-linked components may still be present and vulnerable to enzymatic hydrolysis. Lipase treatment may therefore disrupt the ootheca by increasing permeability or weakening its structure. This novel research provides baseline chemical characterization of SLF ootheca and supports further investigation into enzyme-based, environmentally safer pest control strategies.

column13: Investigating if Resveratrol Can Reduce Seizure-like Activity in Shaker Mutant Drosophila by Reducing Oxidative Stress
column14: Epilepsy and seizure disorders are often linked to neuronal hyperexcitability, frequently modeled in Drosophila melanogaster. My study investigates the neuroprotective potential of the polyphenol Resveratrol, an anti-oxidant, in mitigating seizure-like activity within the Shaker mutant strain, a genetic model for human channelopathies characterized by defective voltage-gated potassium channels. By employing hydrogen peroxide H20 as a mechanistic probe to induce systemic oxidative stress, the research evaluates whether Resveratrol’s antioxidant properties can "rescue" the seizure phenotype. Seizure severity was quantified through high-speed camera tracking of duration and spasm frequency following a standardized 10-second vortex assay. Statistical analysis using a One-Way ANOVA confirmed significant differences among treatment groups, p < 0.001. Results indicate that Resveratrol treatment significantly reduced seizure duration compared to the H20 group, successfully moving the phenotype toward baseline levels. These findings suggest that Resveratrol effectively mitigates seizure-like activity by neutralizing oxidative stress, underscoring the potential of antioxidant-based co-therapies to manage genetic seizure disorders by targeting the cellular redox environment.

column13: Formation of Dual-Species Biofilms of Pseudomonas fluorescens and Listeria innocua
column14: On food processing surfaces, Pseudomonas fluorescens forms a biofilm with Listeria monocytogenes protected by extracellular polymeric substances, leading to food spoilage and poisoning. Studying this dual-species biofilm is difficult, as L. monocytogenes is classified as a BSL-2 organism and inaccessible in BSL-1 labs. An emerging surrogate for L. monocytogenes is BSL-1 organism Listeria innocua, which can form monospecies biofilms. The current study evaluated L. innocua’s potential as a surrogate for L. monocytogenes in forming dual-species biofilms by determining whether L. innocua formed biofilms with P. fluorescens. Stainless steel coupons were incubated in TSB medium with P. fluorescens, L. innocua, or both species. Following incubation, coupons were stained with crystal violet to quantify adherent biofilm biomass. Greater absorbances correlated with greater biomass. In DataClassroom, absorbances were compared using a Kruskal-Wallis test and Dunn’s test with Bonferroni correction. L. innocua, P. fluorescens, and dual-species biofilms had absorbances 0.0578±0.00702, 0.0726±0.00206, and 0.0559±0.00347, respectively, which were not statistically significant from each other (p>0.05), indicating the biomasses of the three biofilms were similar. The results were all statistically significant (p<0.05) from the negative control absorbance 0.0336±0.00180, indicating a biofilm was formed in all three cultures. Additionally, growth was observed when adherent biofilm cells from the dual-species culture were streaked on Listeria selective agar, confirming the presence of L. innocua in the dual-species biofilm. However, presence of P. fluorescens could not be determined.

column13: Music Visualizer
column14: A program that enhances the experience of listening to music and songs. Music is very audibly appealing and adding a visual aspect can improve the listening experience. The visual aspect will consist of lights and LEDs to display an array of colors that correspond to the song that is being played. This process is in real time reacting to the sounds as the notes and music are being played. The project provides an experience similar to a concert light show with music and it also instantly choreographs the lights rather than preparing a light show beforehand. This allows for implementation of freestyles, street performances, and DJ’s who regularly change music spontaneously. My planned solution is a row of spotlights and LED lights that are connected to a Python program that has live audio input. The program will take audio input and display colors based on the tone, notes, and rhythm of the song.

column13: Seizure Sense
column14: Approximately 60 million people around the world suffer from seizures and it can happen to anyone of all ages from infants to elderly. The problem is that seizures are unpredictable and they often can happen without any warning. This can be extremely dangerous as the patient may fall and injure themselves. The individual can lose consciousness, muscle contractions that can hit objects, and also falling over. Additionally, SUDEP can occur when an individual is alone or even asleep leading to apnea which further endangers their life. I aim to develop a device that can detect seizures and issue a warning to the individual or those nearby, thereby helping to prevent injury and ensuring they receive timely assistance, such as medication. I’m focusing on detecting tonic-clonic seizures as they are the easiest to predict. This device consists of an apple watch that detects heart rate and temperature paired with a EMG sensor and accelerometer that detects muscle contractions and breathing patterns.

column13: Emotion Aware Music Therapy
column14: Music therapy has been used as a reliable way to reduce stress and induce relaxation since it was formalized after the second world war. However, current music therapy is inaccessible for many potential users and cannot adapt to a user's real-world emotions. Currently, it requires regular consultations with a medical professional, requiring the patient to invest significant time and money. Additionally, prescribed playlists are unable to change outside of consultations, creating scenarios where the provided music isn't as effective on the user's current emotion. However, recent research has allowed software to determine a user's emotion in real time through the use of artificial intelligence, making music therapy more accessible and adaptive. These new methods, however, use metrics that require the user to take additional action to determine their emotion, such as taking facial photos, talking to chatbots, or speaking into a microphone. This takes the user out of the listening experience, making the therapy less effective. This project proposes a solution that uses a wearable device to passively collect electrodermal activity (EDA) and photoplethysmography (PPG) data to determine the user's emotion with a neural network. This is then used to interface with Spotify to recommend music that fits the user's current mood and guides them to a target mood. All of this combined creates a seamless listening experience for the user that delivers effective therapy with minimal action required from them.

column13: Developing a Morphing Aerofoil to Optimize the Lift to Drag Ratio of an Aircraft During Flight
column14: Standard aerofoils are optimized for one condition of flight. Aircraft, however, face all kinds of flight conditions during travel. By enabling an aerofoil to morph its shape, it can change how it reacts to different flight conditions. Taking a standard aerofoil design, namely the NACA2416, internal structures and mechanics morph the shape of the aerofoil in many different permutations to optimize the lift to drag ratio during flight. The aerofoil has two main control surfaces. The first is on the leading edge of the foil allowing control over the redirection of oncoming airflow and pressure fields. The second is on the trailing edge of the foil allowing for redirection of existing air and vacuum fields. The control surfaces are manipulated by two servos, one 20kg/cm and one 25kg/cm torque motors. The control surfaces are made of TPU95A, a flexible 3D printing material that maintains its shape when stressed. The center of the foil is made of PLA and houses the servos. The servos are controlled by an off board arduino circuit board. The circuit board compares the known lift to drag ratio of a static NACA2416 foil to the known lift to drag ratio characteristics of the morphing foil. The data is mapped to select the most optimal morphing state under given flight conditions. The aerofoil was fabricated on a Prusa XL 5T Input Shaper 3D printer via multi material printing. The resulting dynamic aerofoil should achieve a greater average lift to drag ratio than a static NACA2416 aerofoil.
